title: XSS: Url field accepts javascript:// and data:// schemes — rendered as raw href
evidence: if (filter_var($normalised, FILTER_VALIDATE_URL) === false) { ... $fail(...); }
suggestedFix: Before FILTER_VALIDATE_URL, add an explicit scheme allowlist: $allowed = ['http', 'https', 'ftp', 'ftps']; $scheme = parse_url($normalised, PHP_URL_SCHEME); if ($scheme === null || !in_array(strtolower($scheme), $allowed, true)) { $fail(...); return; } — The regex at normaliseUrl() line 97 already lets 'javascript://' through unchanged because it has the '://' pattern. FILTER_VALIDATE_URL returns true for 'javascript://alert(document.cookie)' on all tested PHP 8.x versions. The TSX component at UrlField.tsx:15 renders href={url} with no sanitisation.

verdict.reasoning:
Direct PHP execution confirmed: `filter_var('javascript://alert(document.cookie)', FILTER_VALIDATE_URL)` returns the input string (truthy), not `false`. PHP's FILTER_VALIDATE_URL follows RFC 3986, which considers `javascript:` a valid scheme and `//alert(document.cookie)` a valid authority component, so the filter passes it through. The same applies to `data://`, `vbscript://`, `JAVASCRIPT://` (case-insensitive), and `file://`.

`normaliseUrl()` at Url.php:97 uses `preg_match('#^[a-z][a-z0-9+\-.]*://#i', $trimmed)` to detect schemes: any input that already carries `://` is returned unchanged, which means all the above variants bypass normalisation and reach `FILTER_VALIDATE_URL` unmodified. The regex does not filter on which scheme is present.

`buildRules()` at Url.php:75 only calls `$fail()` when `FILTER_VALIDATE_URL === false`. Because it returns a truthy string for `javascript://...`, the closure does not reject the value and validation passes.

UrlField.tsx:15 renders `href={url}` with no sanitisation. `String(value)` at line 10 is a type cast, not a sanitiser. `rel="noopener noreferrer"` at line 17 prevents opener/referrer leakage but does not neutralise dangerous schemes.

Severity is adjusted from critical to high. `javascript://` (with `//`) requires a newline-after-comment bypass (e.g. `javascript://x%0aalert(1)`) to execute in Firefox; Chrome does not execute it on anchor click navigation. However, `data://text/html,<script>...` also passes validation and is rendered as an href in the same code path, and `data:` URIs can execute scripts in anchor-click contexts in several browsers. `vbscript://` affects IE/legacy Edge. `file://` passes too. The combination of multiple dangerous schemes surviving validation and being rendered unsanitised is a real security defect; the "critical" label overstates the `javascript://` specific risk but the overall finding is genuine.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct in principle but needs two adjustments. First, apply the scheme allowlist check inside `normaliseUrl` (or as a separate guard called first in `buildRules`) before `FILTER_VALIDATE_URL`, not after — the regex at line 97 passes all `://` forms through unchanged so the allowlist must run before that path returns. Second, the allowlist should use `strtolower(parse_url($trimmed, PHP_URL_SCHEME))` on the original trimmed value (not on `$normalised` post-prepend) because `normaliseUrl` can change the string. Recommended allowlist: `['http', 'https', 'ftp', 'ftps']`. Also fix `fill()` at Url.php:104: it calls `normaliseUrl` but does not re-validate the scheme, so a `javascript://` value that passed validation can still reach the model via `fill()`. On the TSX side, add a runtime guard in `UrlFieldDisplay` before rendering the href: `const safe = /^https?:\/\//i.test(url)` and render a plain `<span>` instead of an `<a>` when the check fails, as a defence-in-depth measure independent of server-side validation.

title: XSS: UrlField.tsx renders href without scheme allowlist — stored javascript:// executes on click
evidence:
<a
  href={url}
  target="_blank"
  rel="noopener noreferrer"
>
suggestedFix: Either sanitise on the backend (the correct fix — see Url.php finding above) or add a frontend guard: const safeUrl = /^https?:\/\//i.test(url) ? url : '#'; then use href={safeUrl}. Both layers should be hardened; defence in depth applies here.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. UrlField.tsx line 15 renders `href={url}` with no scheme guard. The PHP backend (Url.php) does not block javascript: URLs: normaliseUrl() uses the regex `#^[a-z][a-z0-9+\-.]*://#i` (line 97), which matches `javascript://` as a valid RFC 3986 scheme and returns it unchanged; filter_var(..., FILTER_VALIDATE_URL) accepts any RFC 3986-syntactically-valid URL and does not enforce an http(s)-only allowlist, so `javascript://comment%0Aalert(1)` passes validation, is stored verbatim by fill(), and reaches the TSX href unchanged. No sanitisation or scheme check exists in either layer. The test suite has no coverage for javascript: inputs. The vector is real but requires attacker write-access through the admin panel (a less-trusted admin role), which reduces exploitability compared to an unauthenticated or CSRF path. Stored XSS in an admin panel can still be used to escalate privileges by stealing session cookies of higher-privilege admins, making this genuinely high severity. The claimed critical severity is an overstatement — critical applies when unauthenticated users are affected or RCE is reachable.
verdict.fixNote: The correct fix is a scheme allowlist on the backend in Url.php, not just the frontend. In buildRules(), after FILTER_VALIDATE_URL passes, add a check that the stored scheme is http or https: parse_url($normalised, PHP_URL_SCHEME) must be in ['http', 'https']. The fill() method should also reject or strip non-http(s) schemes. The frontend guard (const safeUrl = /^https?:\/\//i.test(url) ? url : '#') is defence in depth but not a substitute for the backend fix, because values can be set programmatically (resolveUsing callbacks, seeders, API writes) bypassing form validation entirely.

title: fill() ignores closure-based readonly — writes proceed when readonly(Closure) is set
suggestedFix: Replace `if ($this->readonly)` with `if ($this->isReadonly())`. The `isReadonly()` method already handles the Closure path correctly (lines 440-449). The same bug exists verbatim in Password::fill() at line 148 — fix both. Slug::fill() calls parent::fill() so it inherits the fix automatically.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the source directly.

`readonly(bool|\Closure $value)` at Field.php line 422-433 only mirrors the value into `$this->readonly` when `is_bool($value)` is true (line 428-429). When a Closure is passed, only `$this->readonlyResolver` is set; `$this->readonly` remains `false` (its initialised default at line 46).

`fill()` at line 314 checks `if ($this->readonly)`, which evaluates the raw boolean. For a Closure-based readonly declaration this boolean is always `false`, so the guard never fires and the model attribute is written unconditionally.

`isReadonly()` at lines 440-449 correctly handles both paths: it evaluates the Closure when `$this->readonlyResolver instanceof \Closure`, and falls back to `$this->readonly` otherwise. But `fill()` bypasses it entirely.

`Password::fill()` at line 148 is an independent override with the identical raw `if ($this->readonly)` check, so it shares the same flaw.

None of the `fillFields()` callers in ResourceController, HasOneController, HasManyController, MorphOneController, or MorphManyController call `isReadonly()` before dispatching to `fill()`, so there is no upstream guard that would compensate.

The suggested fix (replace `if ($this->readonly)` with `if ($this->isReadonly())` in both `Field::fill()` and `Password::fill()`) is correct and sufficient. `Slug::fill()` delegates to `parent::fill()` so it inherits the fix automatically.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as stated. Two files need the one-line change: `Field.php:314` and `Password.php:148`. No other fill() overrides share the pattern — Slug delegates to parent so it inherits the fix automatically. After the fix, also confirm there are no remaining direct reads of `$this->readonly` outside of `isReadonly()` itself (the grep above shows line 341 in `toArray()` already calls `$this->isReadonly()`, and line 448 inside `isReadonly()` is the correct terminal read — both are fine).

title: Password::fill() also uses raw $this->readonly instead of isReadonly()
suggestedFix: Replace `if ($this->readonly)` with `if ($this->isReadonly())` — same fix as Field::fill(). When a developer uses Password::make('password')->readonly(fn($r) => ...) on an update-only form, the hash is still written to the model.

verdict.reasoning: Password::fill() at line 148 guards with `if ($this->readonly)`, reading the raw boolean property directly. Field::readonly() at line 428 only mirrors the value into `$this->readonly` when the argument is a bool — when a Closure is passed, `$readonlyResolver` is set but `$this->readonly` remains false. isReadonly() (lines 440-449) correctly invokes the Closure, but fill() never calls isReadonly(); it reads the boolean directly. Result: any Password field declared with a Closure — e.g. `->readonly(fn($req) => $req->isMethod('PUT'))` — will silently write (and hash) the incoming value even when the field should be immutable. The same raw-boolean guard exists in the base Field::fill() at line 314, but Password overrides fill() and repeats the flaw. The finding is genuine. Severity is assessed as medium rather than high because the plain-boolean form (the most common use) works correctly; only the Closure form is broken, which is a narrower developer usage pattern. The impact when triggered is a security bypass (password overwrite that should be blocked), so it is not low.
verdict.fixNote: Both Password::fill() and the base Field::fill() should replace `if ($this->readonly)` with `if ($this->isReadonly())`. Fixing only Password::fill() leaves the same bug latent in every other field subclass that relies on the base implementation.

title: buildRules() emits duplicate 'required' when rules(['required',...]) is combined with auto-detection
suggestedFix: The isRequired() auto-detection (rulesHaveRequired()) causes 'required' to be prepended AND the literal 'required' from $extraRules to also be present in the merged output. Fix: after building $merged, deduplicate the string rules: $merged = array_values(array_unique($merged)); — or, preferably, skip adding 'required' at line 1393 when it is already in $extraRules: if ($this->isRequired() && !in_array('required', $this->extraRules, true)) { $rules[] = 'required'; }

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is real and confirmed by direct code inspection.

Execution path: when a developer writes `Text::make('email')->rules(['required', 'email'])`, the following happens:
- `$this->extraRules` = `['required', 'email']`
- `isRequired()` falls through to `rulesHaveRequired()` (Field.php:607), which scans `$this->extraRules` and finds `'required'`, returning `true`
- `buildRules()` line 1393 appends `'required'` to `$rules` (the local base array)
- Line 1414: `$extraRules = $this->extraRules` — still `['required', 'email']`, unchanged
- Line 1429: `array_merge($rules, $extraRules, $contextRules)` produces `['required', 'required', 'email']`

No deduplication occurs at any point: not in `buildRules()`, not in the subclass overrides (`Email.php`, `File.php`, `Password.php`, etc. all call `parent::buildRules()` and then append further rules), not in `ResourceController::validateRequest()` (the `array_filter` on line 1640 only strips `'required'` on updates, not on creates), and not inside Laravel's own `Validator` (the `array_unique` in that class applies to `$excludeAttributes`, not the rule list).

The duplicate `'required'` is harmless in practice — Laravel evaluates the rule once it passes — but the array returned to the validator genuinely contains two identical `'required'` strings when this code path is triggered. The defect is exactly as described.

Severity assessment: low. There is no validation-outcome regression (the field is still correctly required), no security impact, and no user-visible error. It is noise/redundancy in the rule array and a latent correctness concern if any downstream code ever counts or deduplicates rule strings by position. The reporter's `medium` claim is slightly high; `low` is more accurate.
verdict.fixNote: The cleaner fix is the guard approach proposed by the reporter: at Field.php line 1392, change `if ($this->isRequired())` to `if ($this->isRequired() && !in_array('required', $this->extraRules, true))`. This avoids emitting the duplicate at the source. The `array_unique` alternative (on `$merged` after the merge) would also work but could silently collapse distinct `Rule` object instances that happen to stringify identically, making it riskier as a blanket deduplicate. The guard on `$extraRules` is precise and surgical. Note: the same guard does NOT need to check `$this->rulesResolver` output because that path (line 1415-1419) overwrites `$extraRules` with the closure result, meaning the closure-returned `'required'` string is still deduplicated if the guard checks `$extraRules` after the resolver runs. To cover both paths, the guard should be applied after line 1420 (after the resolver is resolved), or checked against both `$this->extraRules` and the resolved `$extraRules`.

title: Number::min() and max() accumulate duplicate validation rules on repeated calls
suggestedFix: Each call appends a new rule to extraRules without removing any prior min:/max: entry. Calling ->min(5)->min(10) results in ['min:5', 'min:10'] — Laravel validates both, effectively enforcing min:10 with dead noise at min:5. Calling ->min(5)->max(3) creates an impossible constraint with no error at configuration time. Fix: strip any existing min:/max: rule before appending: $this->extraRules = array_values(array_filter($this->extraRules, fn($r) => !is_string($r) || !str_starts_with($r, 'min:'))); followed by $this->extraRules[] = "min:{$min}";

verdict.reasoning:
The code at `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Number.php:28-44` is exactly as described. Both `min()` and `max()` blindly append to `$this->extraRules[]` without removing any prior entry for the same constraint.

The parent class `Field.php` provides no deduplication anywhere in the rules assembly path. Line 1429 assembles the final rule list via `array_merge($rules, $extraRules, $contextRules)` and `array_values()` — no `array_unique()`, no prefix-stripping. The only `array_unique` call in the file (line 534) is for `$this->dependentFields`, unrelated to validation rules.

The existing test at `FieldTest.php:117-123` calls `->min(0)->max(99)` exactly once each — it never exercises the repeated-call path and would not catch this defect.

Consequently:
- Calling `->min(5)->min(10)` produces `extraRules = ['min:5', 'min:10']`. Laravel evaluates both; `min:5` is dead noise and the effective constraint is `min:10` — functionally "correct" by accident but with an unused stale rule that cannot be inspected or removed by the consumer.
- Calling `->min(5)->max(3)` (a common mistake when reconfiguring a field in a `dependsOn` closure, as done in `DependsOnSyncTest.php:38-43`) produces `['min:5', 'max:3']` — an impossible constraint that Laravel silently enforces, rejecting every numeric input.
- The `dependsOn` pattern in the codebase (`DependsOnSyncTest.php:38-43`) calls `$field->required(...)` inside a closure that runs on every sync request. If a consumer similarly called `$field->min(...)` inside a `dependsOn` closure — a natural pattern — the rule list would grow unboundedly with each sync cycle.

The severity is assessed as medium (matching the claim): it requires a specific usage pattern (calling `min()`/`max()` more than once on the same field instance, or via a reactive `dependsOn` closure) to manifest. It does not directly affect the common single-call path. However, the `dependsOn` closure pattern in the codebase makes this more likely to be hit in practice than a purely theoretical double-call scenario.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct in principle. For completeness, the strip should also cover the `max:` prefix symmetrically. In the `min()` method: filter out any `$r` where `is_string($r) && str_starts_with($r, 'min:')` before appending. Same for `max()`. One additional edge case the reporter did not mention: if `->min()` or `->max()` is called inside a `dependsOn` closure (which re-runs on every sync request), the rule list grows by one entry per sync cycle — that path deserves a specific test as well.

title: Slug reserved-word guard silently bypassed when reserved list contains mixed-case entries
suggestedFix: reserved() stores strings verbatim. generate() (which uses Str::slug) always returns lowercase. The in_array($generated, $this->reserved, true) check at line 214 therefore fails when the developer passes ['Admin', 'Login'] because 'admin' !== 'Admin'. Fix: normalise the reserved list to lowercase at insertion time: $this->reserved = array_values(array_map(fn ($v) => strtolower((string) $v), $reserved));

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Slug.php in full.

reserved() at lines 84-88 stores the incoming array verbatim, applying only a (string) cast — no lowercasing.

generate() at lines 190-193 delegates to Str::slug(), which always returns a lowercase string.

buildRules() at line 214 does in_array($generated, $this->reserved, true) with a strict (===) comparison. Because $generated is always lowercase and $this->reserved may contain mixed-case entries such as ['Admin', 'Login'], the strict comparison yields false and the guard silently passes.

There is no secondary normalisation, no strtolower call before the comparison, and no case-insensitive alternative path anywhere in the file. The fill() method at line 258 also goes through generate() before persisting, so the value that reaches the DB is always lowercase, confirming the mismatch is genuine and not compensated elsewhere.

The severity remains medium rather than high: reserved() is called by the developer building a resource (not by an untrusted end user), so no direct security boundary is crossed. It is, however, a real silent logic defect — passing the idiomatic PHP capitalised list ['Admin', 'Login'] produces a broken guard with no error or warning.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (normalise to lowercase at insertion time in reserved()) is correct and sufficient. An equally valid alternative is to change the in_array call to use strtolower($generated) against an already-lowercased list, but normalising at insertion is cleaner because it keeps getReserved() consistent and makes the stored list self-documenting.

title: rulesHaveRequired() has a tautological condition — the second OR branch is dead code
evidence: if (is_string($rule) && (str_starts_with($rule, 'required') || $rule === 'required')) {
suggestedFix: str_starts_with($rule, 'required') is true for every string that starts with 'required', including 'required' itself — the subsequent || $rule === 'required' is therefore never reachable. Simplify to: if (is_string($rule) && str_starts_with($rule, 'required')) { — no behaviour change, but the dead branch signals a possible past intention to also check object rules with string representation, which is handled by the is_object() branch below.

verdict.reasoning: Line 626 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Field.php reads: `if (is_string($rule) && (str_starts_with($rule, 'required') || $rule === 'required'))`. The second disjunct `$rule === 'required'` is unreachable: `str_starts_with('required', 'required')` evaluates to `true` in PHP because every string starts with itself (empty prefix rule plus exact match). Any input that satisfies `$rule === 'required'` already satisfies `str_starts_with($rule, 'required')`, so the OR short-circuits before the equality check is ever evaluated. No guard elsewhere changes this — the is_string() guard on the outer condition is orthogonal. The finding is real: the branch is dead code. However, it carries zero behavioral impact. The method returns the correct boolean for all possible inputs (string shorthand rules, object Rule instances). This is purely a readability / signal-clarity issue. There is no data leak, no wrong result, no security implication, and no scenario where fixing or not fixing it changes program output. Severity is correctly assessed as low.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct and safe: replace the condition with `is_string($rule) && str_starts_with($rule, 'required')`. No behavior change. The dead branch should not be misread as evidence of a missing object-rule path — that case is already handled by the `is_object()` branch immediately below.

title: PasswordConfirmation doc-drift: docstring promises automatic 'confirmed' backend rule but none is ever added
evidence:
* The field is never persisted to the model (fill is a no-op). Backend
 * validation relies on Laravel's `confirmed` rule applied to the paired
 * `Password` field
suggestedFix: Password::buildRules() never adds 'confirmed'. ResourceController::validateRequest() iterates fields independently and has no logic to link a PasswordConfirmation field back to its sibling Password field. The 'confirmed' rule must be added manually: Password::make('password')->rules(['confirmed']). Either (a) update the docstring to say 'the developer must add ->rules(["confirmed"]) to the Password field', or (b) implement automatic pairing: when buildRules() runs on Password and a PasswordConfirmation sibling exists with matching confirms(), inject 'confirmed' automatically.

verdict.reasoning:
The docstring on PasswordConfirmation.php:11-13 reads: "Backend validation relies on Laravel's `confirmed` rule applied to the paired `Password` field — this companion simply provides the second input and the UX we ship around it." This phrasing implies the framework or Martis wires up `confirmed` automatically.

Reading the actual code refutes that:

1. `PasswordConfirmation` does not override `buildRules()`, so it inherits `Field::buildRules()` which emits only the standard `required`/`nullable`/custom rules. No `confirmed` rule is ever produced by the confirmation field itself.

2. `Password::buildRules()` (lines 162-203) appends complexity constraints (minLength, regex patterns, common-password closure) but never checks whether a `PasswordConfirmation` sibling exists in the form, and never appends `'confirmed'`.

3. `ResourceController::validateRequest()` (line 1633) iterates fields independently — one `buildRules()` call per field, result keyed by `$field->attribute()`. There is no cross-field scan that would detect a `PasswordConfirmation` field and back-inject `'confirmed'` into the sibling `Password` field's rules.

4. The only places where `'confirmed'` actually appears as a rule are three hardcoded spots: `ProfileController.php:66`, `DefaultRegistersUsers.php:37`, and `DefaultResetsUserPasswords.php:31` — all written manually per endpoint, not produced by any `buildRules()` path.

Therefore a developer who registers `Password::make('password', 'Password')` alongside `PasswordConfirmation::make('password_confirmation', 'Confirm Password')` in a resource gets no backend `confirmed` validation at all unless they explicitly chain `->rules(['confirmed'])` on the `Password` field. The docstring's passive "relies on Laravel's `confirmed` rule applied to the paired Password field" gives no indication that this is the developer's responsibility, making it a genuine doc-drift defect.

Severity is medium (not high) because: the UI-side live-match indicator still provides UX feedback; the gap only materialises if a developer reads the docstring and assumes automatic wiring; and the fix is trivial (either clarify the docstring or add 3-4 lines of cross-field logic in `Password::buildRules()`).
verdict.fixNote: The docstring should be updated to explicitly state that the developer must add `->rules(['confirmed'])` to the paired Password field, or alternatively `Password::buildRules()` should be enhanced to auto-inject `'confirmed'` when a `PasswordConfirmation` sibling with a matching `getConfirms()` value is detected. The controller approach (scanning fields for a `PasswordConfirmation` instance and patching the paired field's rules) is also viable but more invasive. The docstring fix is the minimal safe correction.

title: Tag::extraAttributes silently drops relationSearchable: false
suggestedFix: The filter predicate removes any value that is strictly false. For boolean UI flags like relationSearchable this is wrong when the developer explicitly calls ->relationSearchable(false) — the key must still reach the frontend so the TSX can disable search. Either remove relationSearchable from the filtered set (always include it) or narrow the filter to only strip null/empty-string: fn ($v) => $v !== null && $v !== ''. The same filter also silently drops withPreview, displayAsList, and showCreateRelationButton when false — but since false is their default those are harmless omissions; only relationSearchable has a meaningful true-by-default / false-by-explicit-call contrast.

verdict.reasoning:
The PHP-side data-contract defect is real and confirmed at Tag.php:322. The array_filter predicate `fn ($v) => $v !== null && $v !== false && $v !== ''` strips `relationSearchable` from the serialized payload whenever the developer calls `->relationSearchable(false)`, because `false` is literally one of the excluded values.

However, the claimed severity of "high" — based on the assumption that the TSX honours the flag and silently shows search when it should be hidden — is wrong for the current codebase state.

Reading TagField.tsx in full (resources/js/components/fields/TagField.tsx) reveals that the component never reads `relationSearchable` at any point. The search input inside the dropdown (lines 262-283) is rendered unconditionally; there is no guard of the form `{field.relationSearchable !== false && <search-input />}` or equivalent. The flag is read by the PHP layer, serialized (or silently dropped) in `toArray()`, and then completely ignored by the TSX consumer. The frontend always shows the search box regardless of what the API payload contains.

Contrast with MorphTo.php line 464-476: its `extraAttributes()` returns a plain array without `array_filter`, so `relationSearchable: false` always reaches the frontend, and MorphTo's TSX does consume it (confirmed by the existing test at MorphToFieldTest.php:113-116 which asserts `$arr['relationSearchable']` is `false`). The Tag field has both a broken filter AND a TSX that never reads the key — a double-failure that cancels out at runtime.

The PHP filter bug is a latent contract defect: the moment a developer wires `field.relationSearchable` into the TSX (the natural next step for this feature), `->relationSearchable(false)` will silently appear to do nothing, with no error or warning. The fix described in the report is correct (remove `false` from the exclusion set, or exempt the boolean flags). But because the TSX consumer is not yet wired, there is zero observable regression today.

Adjusted severity: low (latent contract bug, no current user-visible impact).
verdict.fixNote: The fix is still warranted before any TSX wiring lands. The predicate at Tag.php:322 should be narrowed to `fn ($v) => $v !== null && $v !== ''` so that explicit `false` values for boolean flags survive serialization. Alternatively, pull `relationSearchable` outside the filtered set and always include it (as MorphTo.php does at line 470). The reporter's secondary note about `withPreview`, `displayAsList`, and `showCreateRelationButton` also being stripped when false is correct but harmless in the same way — their TSX consumers branch on truthiness, and `false` from the server is equivalent to the key being absent (both evaluate to falsy). Only `relationSearchable` has the true-by-default / false-by-explicit-call semantic that makes the omission meaningful once the TSX is wired.

title: HasMany::resolve and MorphMany::resolve call relationship method without method_exists guard
suggestedFix: Add a method_exists guard identical to the one BelongsToMany::resolve uses on lines 394-396: if ($this->showOnIndex) { $method = $this->relationship; if (method_exists($model, $method)) { return $model->{$method}()->count(); } }. Apply the same fix to MorphMany::resolve (line 343 in MorphMany.php). Without the guard a misconfigured relationship or a wrong model class causes a BadMethodCallException in the index view, which the frontend displays as a 500 instead of a graceful empty count.

verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (add method_exists guard identical to BelongsToMany::resolve lines 394-396) is correct and should be applied to both HasMany::resolve and MorphMany::resolve. validateRelationship already contains the right check but is never called from the production pipeline — it is test-only dead code with respect to runtime protection. Consider whether validateRelationship should be wired into a resource boot/registration step for early-fail behaviour instead of relying on a silent null fallback at resolve time.

title: MorphMany::resolve calls relationship method without method_exists guard
suggestedFix: Parallel fix to HasMany: wrap the call with method_exists($model, $this->relationship) before invoking the dynamic method. BelongsToMany (lines 394-396) already demonstrates the correct guard pattern.

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code is real. MorphMany::resolve at lines 342-344 calls $model->{$this->relationship}()->count() with no method_exists guard. If the relationship method is absent on the model, PHP throws a fatal BadMethodCallException at runtime.

The default constructor path calls $this->onlyOnDetail() at line 99, setting showOnIndex = false, so the unguarded branch is not reached in the common case. However Field::showOnIndex() (Field.php:816-821) is a public fluent method that any developer can call on a MorphMany instance to flip the flag to true, making the branch reachable. The unguarded call then becomes live.

The reporter's claim that "HasMany has the parallel fix with a method_exists guard" is factually wrong. HasMany::resolve (lines 353-354) has the identical unguarded pattern: `return $model->{$this->relationship}()->count();`. The asymmetry is only between BelongsToMany (which does have the guard at lines 394-397) and both MorphMany and HasMany (which do not).

MorphMany::validateRelationship (lines 322-337) does include a method_exists check, but that method has zero non-test callers in the package source - it is not called anywhere in the controller pipeline before resolve() runs. So it provides no runtime protection.

Severity adjustment: the original reporter rated this medium. Because the flaw requires a developer to explicitly call ->showOnIndex() against the documented detail-only default, this is a developer-misconfiguration scenario rather than a user-controlled input exploit - the risk surface is narrower than a typical medium bug. But it still produces an unhandled fatal error visible to the end user and has no graceful fallback, so low is the appropriate adjusted severity (not invalid).
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (wrap with method_exists before invoking) is correct and should be applied to both MorphMany::resolve and HasMany::resolve for consistency - HasMany has the same gap and was incorrectly described as already fixed. BelongsToMany::resolve lines 394-397 is the reference implementation to follow.

title: Closure-based ->readonly() is never evaluated in fill() across all relation fields
suggestedFix: Replace `if ($this->readonly)` with `if ($this->isReadonly())` in every fill() override that copies this pattern: Tag::fill (line 250), BelongsTo::fill (line 311), MorphTo::fill (line 286), MultiSelect::fill (line 195), and the base Field::fill (line 314). The Field::readonly() setter at lines 422-430 already documents that 'Closure values defer until isReadonly() is called' — the fill() implementations simply forgot to use the resolver. When a developer writes ->readonly(fn ($r) => $r->isMethod('PUT')), the Closure is never invoked in fill(), making the field writable even on updates.

verdict.reasoning: The bug is confirmed by direct code reading. `Field::readonly()` at Field.php:422-430 only sets `$this->readonly = $value` when `is_bool($value)`; when a Closure is passed it stores it in `$readonlyResolver` only, leaving `$this->readonly` at its default `false`. The comment on line 427 explicitly documents this: "Closure values defer until isReadonly() is called." Every `fill()` override then guards with `if ($this->readonly)` (the raw boolean), not `if ($this->isReadonly())`: Field.php:314, Tag.php:250, BelongsTo.php:311, MorphTo.php:286, MultiSelect.php:195. `isReadonly()` at Field.php:440-448 correctly resolves both bool and Closure paths and is already used in the serialization path (line 341: `'readonly' => $this->isReadonly()`), confirming the resolver was intentionally designed for lazy evaluation but was not applied to `fill()`. Result: `->readonly(fn ($r) => $r->isMethod('PUT'))` leaves the field always writable in fill() regardless of request method, because `$this->readonly` is never updated from `false` and the Closure is never invoked. The severity is genuinely high — it silently defeats any Closure-based write-access guard.

title: isShowCreateRelationButton() uses raw request() instead of safeRequest() in BelongsTo, BelongsToMany, MorphTo
suggestedFix: Replace request() with $this->safeRequest() in isShowCreateRelationButton() in BelongsTo (line 580), BelongsToMany (line 360), and MorphTo (line 226). This method is called from extraAttributes() -> toArray(), so if a non-HTTP context (artisan command, queue job, test without HTTP bootstrap) ever serializes a field with a Closure-based showCreateRelationButton, it will throw rather than returning false gracefully. safeRequest() already handles this by catching Throwable.

verdict.reasoning: The code at BelongsTo.php:580, BelongsToMany.php:360, and MorphTo.php:226 each call the bare `request()` Laravel global inside the Closure branch of `isShowCreateRelationButton()`. The parent class `Field` defines `safeRequest()` at Field.php:456, which wraps `app('request')` in a try/catch Throwable block and returns null when no HTTP context exists. Every other Closure invocation in BelongsTo itself (lines 254 and 316) already uses `$this->safeRequest()`, so the pattern is established and the omission in `isShowCreateRelationButton()` is inconsistent. If a queue job, Artisan command, or test without an HTTP request lifecycle ever triggers `toArray()` on a field where `showCreateRelationButton` is a Closure, `request()` will throw a BindingResolutionException (or similar) rather than returning a safe null/false. The fix (replace `request()` with `$this->safeRequest()` in all three files and treat a null return as false) is straightforward.

title: fillMultiple keepPaths not validated against model-owned paths — data corruption + file deletion
suggestedFix: Replace the keepPaths collection with an intersection check so only paths already stored in the model can be retained: `$keepPaths = array_values(array_intersect($rawExisting, $existingPaths));`. The same fix is needed in Image::fillMultiple (Image.php lines 210-238). Without this, an authenticated user can send arbitrary strings in `{attr}_keep[]` via a direct API POST. This causes (a) the model's real stored file to be deleted from disk (it falls into $removedPaths because it is not in the attacker-controlled $keepPaths), and (b) arbitrary path strings to be persisted in the model's JSON file list, which then appear in resolve() output and can be passed to subsequent delete calls.

verdict.reasoning:
Read /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/File.php lines 306-351 directly. The `fillMultiple` method at line 325-330 builds `$keepPaths` by iterating over `$rawExisting`, which is `$value['existing']`. At line 333, `$removedPaths = array_diff($existingPaths, $keepPaths)` — everything stored in the model but absent from the attacker-supplied array is deleted from disk. There is no `array_intersect($rawExisting, $existingPaths)` guard anywhere in the method.

Tracing the caller at ResourceController.php:1454, `$existingPaths` (confusingly named — it is actually the keep-list, not the stored paths) is taken directly from `$request->input($attr.'_keep', [])` with only an `is_array` coercion and then passed as `'existing' => $existingPaths` to `field->fill()`. No intersection against the model's actual stored paths is performed before or after the call.

Two concrete impacts confirmed:
1. Data loss: an authorized user can send an empty or incomplete `documents_keep[]`, causing the real stored files that are not listed to fall into `$removedPaths` and be deleted from the storage disk via `deletePathFromDisk()` (line 334-336). This is also reachable by accident from a misbehaved client, not just malicious intent.
2. Path injection: arbitrary strings supplied in `documents_keep[]` bypass the string+non-empty check at line 327 and are merged into `$allPaths` at line 350, then persisted as JSON in the model column (line 351) and later returned by `resolveMultiple()` to the frontend.

The authorization check `authorizedToUpdate` at ResourceController.php:343-344 guards the whole resource update but does not scope which per-field path values can be kept — it grants or denies the entire update, so a legitimately authorized user is inside the trust boundary after that gate.

The same pattern exists in Image::fillMultiple at Image.php:209-238 (confirmed by grep output showing lines 210-218 mirror the same structure).

Severity assessment: high is appropriate. The delete-from-disk consequence (impact #1) means a legitimate user or a misbehaved client can cause irreversible file loss on any multiple-file or multiple-image field. Impact #2 is a lower-severity injection but still a data integrity issue. No privilege escalation is needed beyond "update this resource".
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct: replace the `$keepPaths` collection loop with `$keepPaths = array_values(array_intersect($rawExisting, $existingPaths))`. This ensures only paths already stored on the model can be retained, so an attacker-supplied list of arbitrary strings cannot (a) cause stored files to be deleted by exclusion or (b) inject foreign paths into the JSON column. The same fix must be applied to Image::fillMultiple at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Image.php lines 209-238, which is an exact structural copy of the vulnerable pattern.

title: Image::resolveMultiple silently ignores thumbnailResolver and previewResolver closures
suggestedFix: Mirror the single-mode resolve() logic (lines 268-279) inside resolveMultiple. Pass `$model` as a `use` capture and apply both `$this->previewResolver` and `$this->thumbnailResolver` when they are set: `$url = $this->previewResolver !== null ? (($this->previewResolver)($path, $model) ?? $disk->url($path)) : $disk->url($path);` and the equivalent for `thumbnailResolver`. Without this, calling `Image::make('gallery')->multiple()->thumbnail(fn($v, $m) => 'https://cdn.example.com/...')` on a multi-image field silently falls back to disk URLs, making the CDN/signed-URL pattern non-functional in multiple mode.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Image.php confirms the defect exactly as described.

Single-mode resolve() (lines 268-280) correctly applies both resolvers:
- previewResolver: lines 268-270 check `$this->previewResolver !== null` and call it with `($path, $model)`.
- thumbnailResolver: lines 273-274 check `$this->thumbnailResolver !== null` and call it with `($path, $model)`.

resolveMultiple() (lines 306-319) does neither:
- Line 307: `$url = $disk->url($path)` — $this->previewResolver is never consulted.
- Lines 308-311: falls straight through to getThumbnailPath() disk logic — $this->thumbnailResolver is never consulted.

Additionally, the array_map closure at line 306 only captures `$disk` via `use ($disk)`, so even if the code were patched to check the resolvers, it would need `$model` added to the use-list (the fix note in the suggested fix is correct on this point).

The model IS available on the method signature (line 295: `protected function resolveMultiple(Model $model, string $attr): array`), so the fix is straightforward. There is no other guard or indirection that would apply the resolvers before this point: the only early-return above is the resolveCallback check at line 249, which exits before reaching resolveMultiple and is an entirely separate mechanism.

Severity is correctly assessed as medium: this is a documented, opt-in feature (thumbnail(Closure) is explicitly advertised for CDN/signed-URL use cases at lines 90-94 and the property docblock at lines 43-64) that silently degrades to disk URLs in multiple mode, making the CDN pattern non-functional when both multiple() and a closure-based thumbnail/preview are combined. No data loss or security impact.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct in substance. One addition: the array_map closure must also capture $model alongside $disk — change `use ($disk)` to `use ($disk, $model)` — because both resolver closures receive ($path, $model) per their declared signatures (lines 51 and 60). Without capturing $model the closures cannot be invoked inside array_map even after the resolver checks are added.

title: buildItemRules() not called in relationship controllers — MIME/size bypass for multi-file fields
suggestedFix: All four relationship controllers (HasManyController, MorphManyController, HasOneController, MorphOneController) have their own private `validateRequest` copies that are missing the `buildItemRules()` block present in ResourceController lines 1658-1663. Extract the item-rules logic into a shared protected method on a base controller or a trait, and call it from every `validateRequest` implementation. Without this, a multiple-file File or Audio field placed inside a HasMany or MorphMany resource bypasses all MIME-type and size validation for individual uploaded files — the only rule applied is that the top-level value is an `array`.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms the defect exactly as described.

`File::buildRules()` at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/File.php:503-517 shows that when `$this->multiple === true` the method returns only `['required'|'nullable'|'sometimes', 'array']` — it intentionally omits `file`, `mimes:`, and `max:`. Those per-item constraints live in `buildItemRules()` (line 540-557), which produces `['file', 'mimes:...', 'max:...']` to be applied under the wildcard key `attribute.*`.

`ResourceController::validateRequest` at line 1658-1663 correctly calls `buildItemRules()` and registers the `attribute.*` rules. All four relationship controllers (`HasManyController:515-548`, `MorphManyController:453-486`, `HasOneController:493`, `MorphOneController:434`) each have their own private `validateRequest` copy that calls only `buildRules()` and never `buildItemRules()`. No shared base class or trait bridges the gap.

Consequence: a multiple-file `File` or `Image` field placed on a HasMany/MorphMany/HasOne/MorphOne resource receives only the top-level `array` rule on the uploaded value. Every individual uploaded file in the array is accepted regardless of MIME type or size, bypassing configured `acceptedTypes` and `maxSize` constraints. This is a user-controlled upload path (the HTTP multipart payload).

The severity is correctly assessed as medium. It is not critical because it requires a developer to have deliberately placed a multi-file field inside a relationship resource (an uncommon configuration), and exploitation requires authenticated access (authorization is upstream of this validation). Unauthenticated exploitation is not possible in the standard Martis setup, which is why this does not rise to high. The bypass is real and complete however — not a partial miss — so medium is appropriate.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (extract shared logic into a trait or base controller method) is correct. One simpler alternative: add the `buildItemRules()` block verbatim after the `$rules[$field->attribute()]` assignment in each of the four `validateRequest` copies, matching the ResourceController pattern at lines 1657-1663. The trait approach is cleaner because the same duplication will recur whenever a new relationship controller is added. Either way, all four controllers must be updated atomically — patching only HasManyController still leaves the other three broken.

title: Image::getThumbnailPath produces a trailing dot when the stored path has no extension
suggestedFix: Guard the extension concatenation: `return $prefix.$filename.'_thumb'.($ext !== '' ? '.'.$ext : '');`. This mirrors the same pattern already used in `generateStorageFilename` (File.php line 228). While Image validation enforces known image extensions making this unreachable in the normal flow, a model record with a legacy or manually-set path value without an extension would produce a trailing-dot thumbnail path that silently fails all `$disk->exists()` checks and can cause unexpected storage writes with malformed filenames.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at Image.php:423 exactly matches the report: `return $prefix.$filename.'_thumb.'.$ext;` with no guard when `$ext === ''`. The parallel guard already present in File.php:228 (`($ext ? '.'.$ext : '')`) confirms this was missed here.

However, the practical impact is significantly lower than the reporter implies. Two mitigating facts:

1. Normal upload flow cannot produce an extension-less stored path. The `'image'` validation rule plus `mimes:jpg,jpeg,png,gif,webp,bmp` (applied in both single and multiple modes via buildRules/buildItemRules) reject any upload without a recognized image MIME type. Laravel's `hashName()` (the default filename generator) appends the client extension. The `preserveOriginalName` path (File.php:224-228) already guards the no-extension case itself.

2. Every call site that consumes the malformed path from `getThumbnailPath` wraps it in a `$disk->exists($thumbPath)` check (lines 277, 309, 344-347, 361-364) before doing anything with it. A trailing-dot path like `uploads/photo_thumb.` returns false from `exists()`, so resolve falls back to `$url` silently — no render error, no 500. The delete path passes it to `Storage::disk()->delete()`, a safe no-op for a non-existent file. Thumbnail generation only runs when an `UploadedFile` is present (lines 168-169, 231-232), meaning a real upload with a MIME-matched extension is always available.

The only real scenario is a model record seeded or migrated with a manually-set extension-less path (legacy data). In that case the `exists()` guard absorbs the defect completely. The trailing-dot thumbnail path is never exposed to the user and no malformed storage write occurs. The fix (match File.php:228 pattern) is correct and low-risk but the severity is lower than "low" — it is an unreachable edge case in the normal application lifecycle that, even if triggered via legacy data, is silently handled by existing guards.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is valid: guard the concatenation as `$prefix.$filename.'_thumb'.($ext !== '' ? '.'.$ext : '')` to match File.php:228. That said, severity stays at low rather than invalid because the bug is a genuine code inconsistency — the absence of the guard is a latent defect even though all current callers neutralise it via `$disk->exists()`. A future caller that skips the exists-check (e.g. a direct thumbnail-URL assertion in a test or a new resolver variant) would expose the trailing dot. The fix costs one line and closes the gap.

title: Image::generateThumbnailWithGd has no case for BMP even though 'bmp' is in acceptedTypes
suggestedFix: Add a BMP case to both the source-load match and the output match: `'image/bmp', 'image/x-bmp' => @imagecreatefrombmp($sourcePath)` (available since PHP 7.2). Also add `'image/bmp', 'image/x-bmp' => imagebmp($thumb)` to the output match. Without this, uploading a BMP image when `thumbnail()` is configured silently skips thumbnail generation — the GD match returns `false`, the `$src === false` guard exits early, and no thumbnail is written or error raised. The field continues to work (falls back to the original URL as thumbnailUrl) but the developer expectation is violated.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/Image.php line 67 confirms `'bmp'` is in the default `acceptedTypes`. The `generateThumbnailWithGd` method (lines 448-454) loads the source image with a match on MIME type that covers jpeg/png/gif/webp but has no case for 'image/bmp' or 'image/x-bmp', falling to `default => false`. The guard at lines 456-458 (`if ($src === false) { return; }`) then exits early, skipping thumbnail generation entirely. The output match at lines 495-501 would also be wrong for BMP even if a source were loaded — it defaults to JPEG output with no BMP case — but this second issue is moot because execution never reaches it. The Intervention Image path (line 429) is unaffected and handles BMP correctly via its driver. The bug manifests only when Intervention Image is absent (GD fallback). No other guard, helper, or caller resolves this: the caller at line 432 passes through unconditionally when Intervention is not present. The severity is low: the original file is stored and served normally; only the GD-generated thumbnail silently fails to be written, with no error raised and no user-visible corruption.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as described: add `'image/bmp', 'image/x-bmp' => @imagecreatefrombmp($sourcePath)` to the source-load match, and add a corresponding `'image/bmp', 'image/x-bmp' => imagebmp($thumb)` case to the output match. Note that `imagecreatefrombmp()` was introduced in PHP 7.2, so it is safe for the PHP 8.2+ requirement. It is also worth adding a transparency/alpha preservation block for BMP analogous to the existing PNG block if alpha-channel BMPs need to be supported, though standard BMP files do not carry transparency.

title: MarkdownField: unsanitized marked.parse() output fed to dangerouslySetInnerHTML — stored XSS
suggestedFix: Pass the output through DOMPurify (or isomorphic-dompurify) before injecting it. Add `dompurify` to package.json and wrap the return: `return DOMPurify.sanitize(marked.parse(content, { async: false }) as string)`. This applies to both the display path (line 65) and the preview path (line 100). `marked` v17 removed the deprecated `sanitize` option and does NOT strip `<script>`, `onerror=`, or other XSS vectors by design — it is a parser, not a sanitizer. No sanitizer is present anywhere else in the JS bundle.

verdict.reasoning: Direct code inspection confirms the finding on every cited line. `renderMarkdown()` (lines 11-26) passes `marked.parse(content, { async: false })` unsanitized for the `'default'` and `'commonmark'` presets; only the `'zero'` preset performs HTML-escaping. The raw return value is assigned to `html` via `useMemo` and fed directly to `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: html }}` at line 66 (display path) and line 101 (input preview path). No DOMPurify or equivalent sanitizer exists anywhere in `resources/js/` (grep confirms zero hits for dompurify/sanitize/xss). `package.json` lists `marked: ^17.0.5` with no sanitization peer dependency. The installed `node_modules/marked/README.md` carries an explicit warning that marked does not sanitize output and recommends DOMPurify. The `value` prop originates from user-supplied database content (the textarea at line 110 writes `e.target.value` directly). An attacker with write access to any Markdown field can store `<script>` or `onerror=` payloads that execute in every admin browser that renders that record.
verdict.fixNote: The fix applies to `renderMarkdown()` itself so both call sites are covered in one change: `return DOMPurify.sanitize(marked.parse(content, { async: false }) as string)`. Add `dompurify` and `@types/dompurify` to `package.json` and import at the top of the file. The `'zero'` branch is already safe (manual HTML-escaping) and does not need DOMPurify. Also audit the other unsanitized `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` usages found in the same sweep: `TrixField.tsx` (raw DB value), `MartisTooltip.tsx` (author-controlled but still rendered in admin context), `GateModal.tsx` (`message`), and `FieldWrapper.tsx` (`help`) — these are lower-severity but share the same pattern.

title: DateTime extends Date but never sets withTime=true, so resolve() strips the time portion from DateTimeInterface values
suggestedFix: Add an initializer that sets the correct defaults, e.g. override `make()` to call `->withTime(true)`, or add a constructor that calls `parent::__construct($attribute, $label)` and then `$this->withTime(true)`. Without this, `Date::resolve()` formats any `\DateTimeInterface` value with `displayFormat = 'Y-m-d'`, silently dropping the time portion. `extraAttributes()` also emits `withTime: false` and `displayFormat: 'Y-m-d'` for `type: 'datetime'`, misleading the frontend.

verdict.reasoning: DateTime.php (/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/DateTime.php) is exactly 20 lines and only overrides `type()` to return `'datetime'`. It has no constructor, no `make()` override, and no property initializer. It inherits Date's defaults verbatim: `protected bool $withTime = false` and `protected string $displayFormat = 'Y-m-d'` (Date.php lines 17-19). Date::resolve() at line 62 always formats DateTimeInterface values using `$this->displayFormat`, so a `DateTime::make('published_at')` field resolves `new \DateTime('2025-06-15 14:30:00')` to `'2025-06-15'`, silently discarding the time portion. Date::extraAttributes() at lines 74-77 also emits `withTime: false` and `displayFormat: 'Y-m-d'` for the field, so the React frontend receives incorrect metadata and renders a date-only picker. The test at FieldTest.php:431-438 only asserts `toBeString()`, which passes despite the wrong value — it would need to assert `toBe('2025-06-15 14:30:00')` to catch this. There is no guard elsewhere: no parent constructor call sets withTime, no trait injects it, and the only way to get correct behaviour today is to explicitly call `->withTime()` on every DateTime field — which contradicts its purpose as a dedicated datetime field. The defect is confirmed as real. Severity is high: any resource using DateTime::make() without the manual ->withTime() call silently strips the time from stored and displayed values (data-loss).

title: BooleanGroup::requireAll() reads $this->options (always [] when options() was called with a Closure), making the constraint a no-op
suggestedFix: Change to `return $this->minChecked(count($this->getOptions()));`. When `options()` is called with a Closure, `$this->options` is set to `[]` (line 77) and `$this->optionsResolver` holds the closure. `requireAll()` then computes `minChecked(0)`, which applies no constraint at all. The fix makes it use `getOptions()` which resolves the closure first.

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is confirmed by directly reading the source.

`options()` at line 75-79 explicitly sets `$this->options = []` and stores the closure in `$this->optionsResolver` when called with a Closure. `requireAll()` at line 153 calls `count($this->options)`, which is `0` at that point. `minChecked(0)` applies no constraint (the guard at line 137 passes `max(0, 0) = 0`, which is stored as the minimum). The validation closure in `buildRules()` at line 278 will never fire because `$min` is `0` and `$checked >= 0` is always true.

`getOptions()` exists at lines 200-210 and correctly checks `$this->optionsResolver !== null` before invoking the closure with the current request (or null). Using `count($this->getOptions())` in `requireAll()` would resolve the closure and return the real count.

The risk is real but context-bounded: it only affects developers who chain `->options(Closure)->requireAll()`. A developer who passes a static array to `options()` is unaffected because in that branch `$this->options` is populated correctly. The bug silently passes no validation constraint, which is a correctness defect with security implications if the BooleanGroup was used to enforce that all required permissions/flags are acknowledged. Severity is medium rather than high because: (1) it requires a specific calling pattern (closure + requireAll together), (2) it is a developer API defect, not a user-controlled bypass, and (3) no existing code in the package appears to use this combination — the closure pattern is intended for dynamic DB/config-driven flag lists, and requireAll() is a preset for "every flag mandatory", which are semantically unlikely to be used together in practice.
verdict.fixNote: The proposed fix is correct: change line 153 from `count($this->options)` to `count($this->getOptions())`. One additional consideration: `getOptions()` invokes the closure with `$this->safeRequest()`, which may return null at field-definition time (before any HTTP request context exists). The closure must therefore tolerate a null Request argument. This is already documented in the `options()` docblock (`@param \Closure(Request|null)`) and is not a new concern introduced by the fix, but the caller should be aware.

title: KeyValue hardcoded English defaults ('Key', 'Value', 'Add Row') bypass the i18n system
suggestedFix: Change the property defaults to `null` (making them `?string`) and resolve them in `extraAttributes()` with `__('martis::messages.key_label') ?? 'Key'`, etc. Add the translation keys `key_label`, `value_label`, `add_row` to `resources/lang/en/messages.php`, `pt_PT/messages.php`, and `pt_BR/messages.php`. The fallback hardcoded string is fine as a last resort but the translation system should be the primary path, consistent with `Boolean::extraAttributes()` which already does this correctly for `trueLabel`/`falseLabel`.

verdict.reasoning: All three properties on KeyValue (keyLabel, valueLabel, actionText) are typed as non-nullable string with hardcoded English defaults at lines 22-26 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Fields/KeyValue.php. The extraAttributes() method at lines 273-282 passes them directly to the frontend payload with no __() call. Boolean::extraAttributes() at lines 103-109 of the same folder follows the correct pattern: properties default to null and are resolved via __('martis::messages.yes') / __('martis::messages.no') inside extraAttributes(), falling back to null only if the translation itself fails. The translation keys key_label, value_label, and add_row are absent from all three lang files (en, pt_PT, pt_BR). The base Field::extraAttributes() returns an empty array and performs no i18n wrapping. No other mechanism resolves these strings through the translation system. A developer can call .keyLabel() / .valueLabel() / .actionText() to pass a manually translated string, which is a valid workaround but not a systemic fix. The fix as proposed is correct but also requires adding the three translation keys to all three lang files.
verdict.fixNote: The fix requires two coordinated changes: (1) change the three properties to ?string with null defaults and resolve via __() inside extraAttributes() mirroring Boolean, and (2) add key_label, value_label, and add_row to resources/lang/en/messages.php, pt_PT/messages.php, and pt_BR/messages.php. The suggested fix correctly identifies both steps but lists the translation keys without showing what values to add in pt_PT and pt_BR, which must be provided as part of the same commit to satisfy the project i18n rule (EN + pt_PT + pt_BR mandatory per memory entry).

title: Country::extraAttributes() sends the full 195-entry country list on every field serialization, with no caching
suggestedFix: Cache the country list in a static property so it is constructed only once per process: `protected static ?array $cachedCountryList = null;` and in `countryList()` add `if (self::$cachedCountryList !== null) { return self::$cachedCountryList; }` before the array literal. Additionally, for pages with multiple Country fields the payload bloat is multiplied — consider moving the list to a dedicated endpoint or shipping it as part of the field schema only on forms (not index).

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code inspection. `Country::countryList()` at line 78 returns a hardcoded 197-entry array literal with no static property, no memoization, and no caching of any kind — `grep -n "static"` on the file returns only method signatures, never a static property. `extraAttributes()` at lines 311-318 calls `self::countryList()` unconditionally. The base `Field::toArray()` at line 378 merges `$this->extraAttributes()` on every single serialization with no form-only or context guard. PHP's opcode cache preserves the compiled bytecode but allocates a fresh heap array on each invocation, so every field serialization (index rows, detail view, create form, update form) independently constructs the full 197-entry list. Multiple Country fields on the same page multiply the payload accordingly. There is no existing mitigation: no `static ?array $cachedCountryList`, no `Cache::remember()`, no deferred-endpoint pattern. The Timezone field has an analogous pattern (`groupedList()` called from `extraAttributes()` without caching), confirming this is not an isolated oversight. The finding is real. Severity remains low: the defect causes per-request allocation overhead and JSON payload inflation, but has no correctness or security consequences. It only becomes meaningfully expensive on index pages that render many rows each containing a Country field, or forms with multiple Country fields — uncommon but plausible layouts. The suggested static memoization fix is the correct minimal remedy.
verdict.fixNote: The static-property memoization suggested (`protected static ?array $cachedCountryList = null`) is the right fix for the allocation cost within a single process. However, the secondary suggestion — "move the list to a dedicated endpoint or ship it only on forms" — would require a contract change between the PHP serializer and the TSX component (the frontend currently reads `field.countries` from the schema payload). That is a larger refactor worth a separate proposal, not bundled with the caching fix. Also note that `resolveCountryName()` (line 285) and `resolveCountryFlag()` (line 299) each call `self::countryList()` in a linear scan; memoizing the list helps those too, but a keyed lookup map (`array_column(..., null, 'value')`) would reduce their O(n) scan to O(1) at the same time.

title: syncField context=update checks wrong gate — view-only user can probe update-field metadata and reactive callbacks
evidence: if ($context === 'update' && ! $instance->authorizedToCreate($request) && ! $instance->authorizedToViewAny($request)) {
suggestedFix: The condition uses an OR-conjunction: it blocks only when BOTH authorizedToCreate AND authorizedToViewAny are false. A user who has viewAny but not update (read-only role) passes the gate and can invoke syncField with context=update to trigger reactive callbacks and enumerate update-field definitions. Replace with `! $instance->authorizedToUpdate($request)` — or for context=create, keep `! $instance->authorizedToCreate($request)`. The guard should be a direct match to the permission required for that context.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by direct code inspection.

Line 728 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/ResourceController.php:

```php
if ($context === 'update' && ! $instance->authorizedToCreate($request) && ! $instance->authorizedToViewAny($request)) {
```

By De Morgan's law this gates only when the user has NEITHER `create` NOR `viewAny`. A user with `viewAny` (read-only role) but not `update` passes the guard, proceeds to `fieldsForUpdate()` and `syncDependent()`, and can therefore enumerate update-field metadata and trigger reactive callbacks without holding the `update` permission.

The code comment on line 727 says "a user who cannot create/update the resource cannot probe sync data" — confirming the intended check was `update`, but the implementation checks `create || viewAny` instead.

The three authorization methods all exist on `Resource` (confirmed at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Resource.php:619), so `authorizedToUpdate` is callable. The route sits behind `martis.auth` + `martis.2fa` middleware so unauthenticated access is blocked, but there is no route-level or middleware-level guard that would compensate for the wrong in-controller check — the per-resource authorization is entirely the controller's responsibility here.

The claimed severity of "high" is appropriate: this is an authenticated privilege escalation where a view-only user can probe write-context field schema and reactive callbacks for any resource they can view. There is no compensating control in the package codebase that would prevent this.

The suggested fix (replacing the guard with `! $instance->authorizedToUpdate($request)`) is the right direction. One secondary note: `authorizedToUpdate` passes `$this->model` to the policy, and since the resource is instantiated as `new $resourceClass` without a model, `$this->model` is `null`. `checkPolicy` handles a null model by calling `$policy->update($user)` without a model argument. Consumer policies whose `update()` method declares `(User $user, Model $model)` as required would throw a PHP TypeError. However, this is a pre-existing pattern used by `authorizedToCreate` on line 731 (which also calls `checkPolicy('create', null)`) and is out of scope for this specific finding.
verdict.fixNote: The fix direction is correct. One nuance: `authorizedToUpdate` calls `checkPolicy('update', $this->model)` and since the resource is constructed as `new $resourceClass` with no model, `$this->model` is null. `checkPolicy` then calls `$policy->update($user)` with no model argument. Consumer policies whose `update()` signature requires a `Model $model` argument will throw a TypeError at runtime. The safe fix is to either (a) pass a fresh model instance (`new (static::model())`) so the policy call is valid, or (b) add a dedicated `authorizedToUpdateAny()` helper analogous to `authorizedToViewAny()` that checks the `update` ability without a model. The `authorizedToCreate` call on line 731 uses the same null-model pattern and would have the same issue, so this is a pre-existing systemic concern across the syncField gate, not introduced by the fix.

title: show?context=update returns raw update-field values to users with view but not update permission
suggestedFix: The show endpoint checks only authorizedToView regardless of the requested context. When context=update it should additionally check authorizedToUpdate, and when context=create it should check authorizedToCreate. A view-only user can currently call GET /api/resources/{resource}/{id}?context=update and receive unformatted raw field values that are only meant for the edit form. Add a context-specific gate check after the view check: `if ($context === 'update' && !$res->authorizedToUpdate($request)) return forbidden` and `if ($context === 'create' && !$res->authorizedToCreate($request)) return forbidden`.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the actual code. The `show` action in `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/ResourceController.php` at lines 213-228 performs a single authorization gate: `authorizedToView`. After that gate passes, it branches on the `?context` query parameter with no further authorization check. A user holding only `view` permission (policy returns `true` for `view`, `false` for `update`) can issue `GET /api/resources/{resource}/{id}?context=update` and receive the raw, unformatted attribute values that are meant exclusively for the edit form — serialized via `$field->resolve($model)` (`$forDisplay=false`) at ResourceController.php line 1789.

No compensating guard exists anywhere in the call chain:
- `routes/martis.php` line 451 registers `GET /resources/{resource}/{id}` with no `can:` middleware.
- `checkPolicy()` in `Resource.php` lines 828-862 treats `view` and `update` as fully independent abilities; there is no cascade or implication from view to update.
- The `ResourceUpdate.tsx` frontend page (line 50) fires `GET /api/resources/{resource}/${id}?context=update` unconditionally on mount, so the only defence against an unauthorized user seeing raw edit-form data is that the UI wouldn't normally navigate to `/edit` — but a curl call bypasses that entirely.

The `context=create` branch (lines 223-225) has the same missing `authorizedToCreate` check, though `context=create` is a less natural attack vector (no record id needed for create forms in the normal flow) and `fieldsForCreate` defaults to `fields()` same as `fieldsForUpdate`.

The severity is correctly assessed as **high**: raw field values (before any `displayUsing()` transform) leak to users who should only see the formatted detail view. Depending on what fields developers put in `fieldsForUpdate` (hashed tokens, internal flags, sensitive computed columns), this constitutes a genuine information-disclosure vulnerability. The fix described in the report is correct: add `if ($context === 'update' && !$res->authorizedToUpdate($request)) return forbidden` and the equivalent for `context=create` between lines 215 and 218.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct and sufficient. Insert both checks immediately after the `authorizedToView` guard (after line 215) and before the `$context = $request->query(...)` line. No other compensating mechanism exists in the route layer, middleware, or policy resolver that would catch this. The `context=create` branch needs its own `authorizedToCreate` check for the same reason.

title: replicateFields skips per-model field authorization (isAuthorizedForModel) by passing a keyless replica
suggestedFix: Eloquent's replicate() strips the primary key, so any canSeeForModel callback that evaluates the model's key (e.g. owner-scoped policies) receives a null-key replica instead of the real record. Additionally, serializeModel() calls isAuthorizedForModel on the real model, but replicateFields never calls isAuthorizedForModel at all. Fix: before calling $field->resolve($replica), check `if (method_exists($field, 'isAuthorizedForModel') && !$field->isAuthorizedForModel($request, $model)) continue;` — using the original $model (which has a key), not the replica.

verdict.reasoning: The defect is confirmed by direct code inspection. The `replicateFields` loop at lines 623-630 of ResourceController.php has no call to `isAuthorizedForModel`. The only skip inside the loop is for `File` instances. By contrast, `serializeModel` (line 1780) explicitly calls `isAuthorizedForModel($request, $model)` before resolving every field. Any field protected with `->canSeeForModel(...)` or `->canSeeUsingPolicy(...)` will be correctly stripped from index, detail, and update payloads but will have its raw value exposed by the replicate pre-fill endpoint. The null-key problem is also real: `Eloquent::replicate()` unsets the primary key, so a Gate policy passed the replica model receives a null key and may evaluate incorrectly. The access gate at line 612 (`authorizedToReplicate`) guards the resource-level permission but does not substitute for field-level `canSeeForModel` authorization. The severity is lower than "high" because the path requires authentication and the `authorizedToReplicate` check, making it an insider/role-scoped bypass rather than an open exposure, but it is a genuine authorization bypass on sensitive field values for users who hold replicate permission.
verdict.fixNote: The fix should pass the original `$model` (not `$replica`) to `isAuthorizedForModel`, since the replica has a null primary key which breaks Gate policies that evaluate `$model->getKey()`. The check should be: `if (method_exists($field, 'isAuthorizedForModel') && !$field->isAuthorizedForModel($request, $model)) { continue; }` placed before `$field->resolve($replica)` at line 628, using `$model` (the real record fetched at line 604) as the second argument.

title: Inline-create depth guard is permanently inert — X-Martis-Inline-Create-Depth is never sent by the frontend
suggestedFix: The frontend InlineCreateModal.tsx never sends X-Martis-Inline-Create-Depth (verified by grep over all resources/js). The header default of '0' means the condition is always false and the depth guard never fires. Either (a) make the frontend send the header with an incrementing depth counter, or (b) remove the dead guard and enforce depth at the schema layer by already stripping showCreateRelationButton from BelongsTo/MorphTo fields in fieldsForInlineCreate() (which inlineCreateSchema already does at line 669). Without one of these fixes, nested inline creates (creating a record that itself opens another inline create) are not blocked server-side.

verdict.reasoning:
The header `X-Martis-Inline-Create-Depth` is confirmed to be absent from all frontend code. A full grep over `resources/js/` returns zero hits for the string, while the only non-controller reference is in a test file (`ReplicateInlineCreateTest.php:202`) that manually injects the header to exercise the guard. The controller guard at line 808 therefore never fires in production: the default `'0'` is always used and `'0' !== '0'` is always false.

However, the depth restriction is not completely unenforced. The `inlineCreateSchema` endpoint (lines 669-676) deliberately sets `showCreateRelationButton: false` on every `belongs_to` and `morph_to` field in the schema it returns. `BelongsToField.tsx` line 364 reads `showCreateRelationButton === true` (strict equality), so the button is hidden and the `InlineCreateModal` is never rendered inside an inline create context. This schema-layer guard prevents a nested inline create from being initiated by the user at all.

The net assessment: the server-side header guard is dead code (confirmed real defect), but the practical impact is low because the schema layer already blocks the nested-create UI path. The finding's claim that nested inline creates "are not blocked server-side" is accurate in the narrow sense that the HTTP guard is inert, but the broader claim that the feature is unprotected overstates the severity — the UI path is closed by the schema response. Severity is low rather than medium: exploitation would require a manually crafted POST request bypassing the normal UI, which is already a non-standard attack surface for an authenticated admin panel.
verdict.fixNote: The schema layer at `inlineCreateSchema` (ResourceController.php lines 669-676) already enforces depth by stripping `showCreateRelationButton` from `belongs_to`/`morph_to` fields before the schema is returned to the frontend. `BelongsToField.tsx` gates both the button and the `InlineCreateModal` on `showCreateRelationButton === true`, so the nested create UI is never available inside an inline create modal. The server-side header guard is dead code but the depth constraint itself is met. The cleanest fix is option (b) from the suggestion: remove the dead header guard from `inlineCreateStore` and add a code comment to `inlineCreateSchema` documenting that stripping `showCreateRelationButton` is the intentional depth enforcement point. No frontend changes are needed.

title: per_page=0 or negative bypasses the ceiling guard — paginate(0) returns empty pages, paginate(-N) may throw on some engines
suggestedFix: min() only caps the ceiling. A caller passing per_page=0 gets LIMIT 0 (valid SQL, silently empty result), while per_page=-5 results in LIMIT -5 which raises an error on PostgreSQL. Add a floor: `$perPage = max(1, min(...))`. The same pattern applies to the relatable endpoint: resolveRelatableSearchResults only uses max(1,...) when $relatableSearchResults is set; when it is null, `min($requestPerPage, 100)` has no floor.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at ResourceController.php:131-136 confirms the defect. `$perPage = min((int)$request->query('per_page', ...), 100)` applies only a ceiling; a user-supplied `per_page=0` yields `min(0,100)=0` and a `per_page=-5` yields `min(-5,100)=-5`. Both are passed without a floor to `$query->paginate($perPage)`. Laravel's `Query\Builder::paginate` delegates to `forPage($page, $perPage)` which executes `offset(($page-1)*$perPage)->limit($perPage)` — no internal guard. `LIMIT 0` is valid SQL that silently returns zero rows (deceptively empty response with HTTP 200), while `LIMIT -5` produces a driver error on PostgreSQL. Laravel's short-circuit in `paginate` (`$total ? $this->forPage(...)->get() : collect()`) only bypasses the query when the table is empty, not when `$perPage` is non-positive.

For the relatable endpoint (ResourceController.php:1318-1322), `resolveRelatableSearchResults` is called with `(int)($request->query('per_page', '20'))`. Inside Resource.php:1536-1544, the `$cap === null` branch returns `min($requestPerPage, 100)` — again no floor — while the `$cap !== null` branch correctly applies `max(1, min($requestPerPage, $cap, 100))`. So the floor guard is inconsistently present only when the resource declares an explicit `$relatableSearchResults` cap.

No middleware, form-request validation, or other upstream guard was found that sanitizes `per_page` before either code path. The finding is confirmed at both cited locations.
verdict.fixNote: The fix for the primary index path is `$perPage = max(1, min((int)$request->query('per_page', ...), (int)config('martis.pagination.max_per_page', 100)));`. For the relatable endpoint, the `$cap === null` branch in `Resource::resolveRelatableSearchResults` should return `max(1, min($requestPerPage, 100))` to match the guarded branch. Severity stays medium: the `per_page=0` case is a silent bad response (not a crash or data leak), and the negative case only raises an engine error on PostgreSQL, not MySQL/SQLite.

title: handleDatabaseError maps only MySQL vendor codes — unique-constraint and FK violations are silently downgraded to a generic error on Postgres/SQLite
suggestedFix: errorInfo[1] is the MySQL-specific vendor code. On PostgreSQL the unique-constraint SQLSTATE is '23505' in errorInfo[0], and FK violations are '23503'/'23505'. On SQLite, constraint violations return code 19 in errorInfo[1]. Add SQLSTATE-based fallback: check errorInfo[0] for '23505' (unique) and '23503'/'23000' (FK) when the MySQL-specific code doesn't match. This ensures users on Postgres get the precise 422 validation error with field mapping instead of a 500 generic message.

verdict.reasoning: The code at ResourceController.php:1395 reads `$code = (string) ($e->errorInfo[1] ?? '');` and the match arms are all MySQL vendor codes ('1048', '1062', '1364', '1451', '1452'). There is no SQLSTATE-based fallback on `errorInfo[0]`. On PostgreSQL, unique-constraint violations surface as SQLSTATE '23505' in errorInfo[0] while errorInfo[1] contains the pg driver error code (not '1062'), so they fall through to the default branch which returns a serverError response instead of a 422 with field mapping. FK violations similarly fall through. On SQLite, errorInfo[1] is 19 (SQLITE_CONSTRAINT), also not matching any arm.
