The same MySQL-only pattern is duplicated verbatim in at least 6 sibling controllers (MorphManyController, HasOneController, HasManyController, MorphOneController, BelongsToManyController, MorphToManyController). Severity is medium: the failure mode is a degraded user experience (generic error instead of a precise field-level 422) rather than a crash, data corruption, or security exposure. No guard, helper, or framework layer compensates for the missing SQLSTATE check.
verdict.fixNote: The defect is also present in the 6 sibling controllers, each with their own copy of handleDatabaseError. ResourceController is the only one that even has the MySQL-specific 422 path for '1062'; the others return serverError for unique constraint violations on all drivers including MySQL. A shared trait or base-controller method with SQLSTATE-aware mapping would fix all sites at once.

title: BelongsToMany attach: related record fetched from global scope, not relationship scope — IDOR on relatedId
suggestedFix: The attach, detach, and updatePivot actions all look up the related model with a global `::find($relatedId)` before calling `canAttach` / `canDetach` / `canUpdatePivot`. Because `canAttach` / `canDetach` default to `authorizedToUpdate` on the parent (not a model-level gate on the related record), a user who can update *any* parent can attach any related record that exists in the database — including records that belong to other tenants or that the user should not be able to see. Fix by either: (a) scoping the lookup through the relationship's `relatable` query so only permitted candidates are addressable, or (b) adding a `authorizedToView` check on the related resource instance after the lookup, before proceeding.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the full file. At line 278 (attach), line 359 (attachMany batch loop), line 482 (detach), and line 542 (updatePivot), the related record is retrieved with a bare `$relatedModelClass::find($relatedId)` against the global Eloquent scope — no relationship-scoped query, no tenant scope, no "is this ID within the set the user is permitted to see" check.

`canAttach` (lines 730-738) is the only authorization gate on the related record. It calls `authorizedToAttach($request, $relatedModel)` if that method exists on the parent resource. When it does not exist, the fallback is `$parentInstance->authorizedToUpdate($request)`, which only checks whether the current user may update the parent model. No check on the related model is performed in the fallback path.

The `attachableIndex` endpoint (lines 137-233) does apply a `relatableQueryUsing` closure and excludes already-attached IDs from the candidate list presented to the user. But the `attach` POST at line 278 does not cross-validate the submitted `related_id` against that same scoped query. A user who can enumerate IDs out-of-band (or guess them) can submit an ID that would never appear in the picker and have it attached.

Concretely: in a multi-tenant application using default Martis authorization (no custom `authorizedToAttach`), a user with update rights on their own parent record can POST any `related_id` — including records belonging to a different tenant — and the system will attach it. This is a true IDOR on the related-side of a BelongsToMany attach.

Severity is adjusted to **medium** rather than high: (1) the user must already hold `authorizedToUpdate` on the parent — it is not a zero-permission exploit; (2) the impact is a relationship link being created, not direct data exfiltration from the related record; (3) the fix path is well-defined (scope the lookup through the relationship, or add `authorizedToView` on the related resource after the lookup). The same unscoped `::find` pattern also appears in `attachMany` (line 359), `detach` (line 482), and `updatePivot` (line 542), so all four mutation endpoints share the same exposure.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. The most robust approach is (a): before calling `canAttach`, re-run the `relatedModelClass` query through the same scoped builder used by `attachableIndex` — honouring `relatableQueryUsing` if set, or at minimum going through the relationship's `relatable()` query — so that IDs outside the permitted set return a 404 before authorization is even evaluated. Alternatively (b): after the bare `::find`, instantiate `new $relatedResourceClass($relatedModel)` and call `authorizedToView($request)` on it; this adds a model-level gate. Both fixes need to be applied to all four mutation methods: single `attach`, batch `attachMany`, `detach`, and `updatePivot`.

title: MorphToMany attachableIndex: relatableQueryUsing closure receives no form-draft (feature gap vs BelongsToMany)
suggestedFix: BelongsToManyController passes a third `$formDraft` argument when the closure arity is >= 3 (v1.8.2 feature). MorphToManyController always calls the closure with 2 arguments, silently ignoring any arity-3 closure. Developers who move a BelongsToMany field to MorphToMany will get a silent regression. Apply the same ReflectionFunction arity check and form-draft collection logic used in BelongsToManyController.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms the discrepancy exactly as described. In /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/MorphToManyController.php lines 161-165, the relatable closure is called with exactly two arguments ($request, $query) and there is no arity check and no form-draft collection. grep for "collectFormDraft", "ReflectionFunction", and "formDraft" returns no output at all from that file.

In contrast, BelongsToManyController.php lines 171-182 contains the full v1.8.2 feature: it calls $this->collectFormDraft($request) to collect the parent form draft, uses (new \ReflectionFunction($relatableClosure))->getNumberOfParameters() to detect arity, and passes the form draft as a third argument when arity >= 3. The private collectFormDraft method is defined at line 701 of BelongsToManyController.

The gap is genuine: a developer who defines a relatableQueryUsing closure with three parameters ($request, $query, $formDraft) on a MorphToMany field will silently receive null/unset for $formDraft because the controller always calls $relatableClosure($request, $query) with no third argument. There is no shared helper, trait, or parent class that provides this behavior to MorphToManyController. The fix described (applying the same ReflectionFunction arity check and collectFormDraft call) is accurate.

Severity assessment: the original reporter called this "low". I agree it is not critical — it only affects developers who explicitly opt into the three-argument closure signature on a MorphToMany field, and no runtime error is thrown (PHP ignores extra declared parameters when fewer are passed to a Closure). However the silent regression for someone migrating a BelongsToMany field to MorphToMany is a real trap. "Low" is appropriate.

title: BelongsToMany/MorphToMany index: sort column passed directly to orderBy without validating against sortable fields
suggestedFix: HasManyController::applySorting() and MorphManyController::applySorting() both validate the sort parameter against the resource's declared sortable field attributes before calling orderBy. BelongsToManyController::index() and MorphToManyController::index() do not: they call `$query->orderBy($rawSort, ...)` on any non-empty string. While Eloquent/PDO will parameterize the value, the column name itself is interpolated into the SQL and not quoted by all drivers in all configurations. An attacker can trigger an error response that leaks schema information, or in edge-case driver configurations, inject SQL. Apply the same sortable-field allowlist used in the HasMany/MorphMany controllers.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the actual code. BelongsToManyController.php:82-84 calls `$query->orderBy($rawSort, $direction->value)` with the only guard being `is_string($rawSort) && $rawSort !== ''`. There is no allowlist check against the resource's declared sortable fields.

HasManyController::applySorting() (lines 495-505) and MorphManyController::applySorting() (lines 568-576) both collect sortable attributes via `$field->isSortable()` on flattened field definitions and return early if the sort parameter is not in that set before calling orderBy. BelongsToManyController and MorphToManyController (MorphToManyController.php:85-87 has the identical pattern) both skip this step entirely.

Eloquent's `orderBy` interpolates the column identifier directly into the SQL string; it is not a PDO bound parameter. An unauthenticated or low-privilege user who can reach the index endpoint (which only requires `authorizedToView` on the parent) can supply any arbitrary column name and receive a database error response leaking schema information (column names, data types, table structure). On some drivers and configurations this also opens timing-based blind injection channels. The `direction` parameter is safe because it goes through `SortDirection::tryFrom()`. The sort column is not sanitized in any way beyond the empty-string check.
verdict.fixNote: Both BelongsToManyController::index() and MorphToManyController::index() need the same allowlist guard used in HasManyController and MorphManyController: flatten the related resource's fields, filter by isSortable(), extract attributes into an array, and return without calling orderBy if the sort parameter is not in that array. The $relatedResourceClass variable is already in scope at both call sites, so no additional context is needed.

title: handleDatabaseError in BelongsToManyController/MorphToManyController uses MySQL-specific error codes only
suggestedFix: The HasMany/MorphMany/HasOne/MorphOne handleDatabaseError methods also include error codes 1048 and 1364. The BelongsToMany and MorphToMany versions omit them. More importantly, all six controllers use MySQL-specific numeric error codes (errorInfo[1]). On PostgreSQL the relevant codes are SQLSTATE strings like '23505' (unique violation) and '23503' (FK violation). On SQLite they are different again. The pivot attach path is the most likely to hit these on a non-MySQL database. Consider branching on `$e->getCode()` (SQLSTATE) alongside the vendor code for cross-driver portability.

verdict.reasoning: The code confirms both sub-claims. At BelongsToManyController.php:797 and MorphToManyController.php:719, handleDatabaseError reads `$e->errorInfo[1]` (the MySQL driver-specific vendor code) and matches only '1062', '1451', '1452'. The four other relationship controllers (HasManyController:591, MorphManyController:529, HasOneController:551, MorphOneController:492) and ResourceController:1395 all additionally match '1048' (NOT NULL violation) and '1364' (DEFAULT value missing), confirming the inconsistency is specific to the BelongsToMany/MorphToMany pair. On the cross-driver point: all six controllers use `$e->errorInfo[1]` (PDO index 1 = driver-specific integer code). On PostgreSQL the equivalent constraint violation codes live in SQLSTATE strings like '23505' and '23503' accessible via `$e->getCode()` or `$e->errorInfo[0]`, never as integer 1062/1451/1452. On SQLite the codes differ as well. The practical consequence is that on any non-MySQL driver, every QueryException falls through to the generic 'A database error occurred.' message. The defect is real but the impact is limited to user-facing error message quality: the exception is still caught, no 500 is produced, and no data is lost or leaked. Low severity is the correct assessment.
verdict.fixNote: The fix has two independent parts. First, add '1048' and '1364' arms to BelongsToManyController::handleDatabaseError and MorphToManyController::handleDatabaseError to match the other four controllers (trivial copy). Second, for cross-driver portability across all six controllers, branch on $e->errorInfo[0] (SQLSTATE) alongside $e->errorInfo[1]: PostgreSQL unique violation is SQLSTATE '23505', FK parent missing is '23503', NOT NULL is '23502'; SQLite unique is '23000'. A shared trait or base-class method would eliminate the duplication across all six identical private implementations.

title: Session fixation: SsoController never regenerates the session ID after login
suggestedFix: Call `$request->session()->regenerate()` immediately after `Auth::guard($guard)->login($user, true)` — the same pattern used in `AuthController::login()` (line 121) and `MagicLinkController::consume()` (line 108). Without this, an attacker who knows the unauthenticated session ID (e.g. via a shared LAN or network tap) can pre-plant the ID and take over the session once the victim completes SSO.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at SsoController.php lines 119-131 is exactly as cited. Auth::guard($guard)->login($user, true) is called at line 120, followed by session()->put() and redirect()->intended(), but there is no $request->session()->regenerate() call anywhere in the callback() method.

The comparison controllers confirm the omission is inconsistent: AuthController::login() calls $request->session()->regenerate() at line 121 (immediately after $auth->attempt() succeeds), and MagicLinkController::consume() calls $request->session()->regenerate() at line 108 (immediately after Auth::guard($guard)->login($user)).

The claim that Laravel's framework handles this automatically does not hold here. Laravel's SessionGuard::attempt() internally calls login(), which in turn calls $this->session->migrate(true) — that is what regenerates the session ID in the attempt() path. However, when a caller bypasses attempt() and calls login() directly (as SsoController does), the migrate() call is still performed inside SessionGuard::login() itself. Let me reconsider this point — I need to be precise about whether SessionGuard::login() calls migrate().

Looking at the Laravel SessionGuard source: SessionGuard::login() does call $this->session->migrate(true) after setting the user identifier, regardless of whether it was called directly or through attempt(). This means the framework DOES regenerate the session ID when Auth::guard()->login() is called.

However, AuthController::login() still calls $request->session()->regenerate() explicitly AFTER $auth->attempt() — meaning it double-regenerates. MagicLinkController::consume() calls Auth::guard($guard)->login($user) and then $request->session()->regenerate() explicitly.

If SessionGuard::login() internally calls migrate(true) on the session, then the SsoController omission is NOT a vulnerability — the session ID IS regenerated by the framework inside the login() call. The explicit regenerate() calls in the other controllers would be redundant (harmless double-regeneration).

I need to reconsider: the finding is based on surface-level comparison (SsoController lacks explicit regenerate() while others have it), but if the framework's SessionGuard::login() already calls session()->migrate(true), then there is no actual session fixation vector. The reporter's evidence is the absence of an explicit call, but the framework provides the protection.

Given that Auth::guard()->login() (via Laravel's SessionGuard) calls $this->session->migrate(true) internally, the session ID is regenerated at the framework level. The explicit regenerate() calls in AuthController and MagicLinkController are defensive redundancy, not the sole protection. SsoController gets the same protection from the framework without needing the explicit call.

Therefore the finding is NOT real — it is a false positive based on comparing explicit calls without accounting for the framework's built-in behavior inside SessionGuard::login().
verdict.fixNote: The reporter's surface comparison is correct (no explicit $request->session()->regenerate() in SsoController::callback()), but the finding is a false positive. Laravel's SessionGuard::login() internally calls $this->session->migrate(true) before returning, which regenerates the session ID regardless of how login() is invoked — directly or via attempt(). The explicit regenerate() calls in AuthController (line 121) and MagicLinkController (line 108) are defensive double-regenerations, not the sole protection against session fixation. SsoController receives the same protection from the framework. No code change is required for correctness, though adding an explicit regenerate() call for consistency and defense-in-depth would be reasonable.

title: 2FA bypass: SSO callback and magic-link consume never set martis_two_factor_passed — a user with 2FA enabled is logged in fully without a challenge

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is real, but the claimed severity (critical — full 2FA bypass) is overstated. Here is what the code actually shows:

SsoController.php lines 119-131 and MagicLinkController.php lines 106-112: confirmed — neither sets `martis_two_factor_passed` in the session after `Auth::guard()->login()`. AuthController::login() sets it at line 131 of AuthController.php; the two alternative login paths do not.

EnsureTwoFactorChallenge::requiresChallenge() (line 78): uses a truthy check — `if ($request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed'))`. When the key is absent, `session()->get()` returns `null`. `null` is falsy, so the early-return `return false` is NOT taken, and `requiresChallenge()` returns `true` (line 82). This means the middleware DOES intercept SSO/magic-link sessions — it blocks every subsequent API call with 423 and redirects browser requests to `/2fa/challenge`. There is no security bypass via the middleware path.

The real asymmetry: AuthController::user() at line 55 uses strict `=== false` — `$twoFactorPassed === false`. Because the SSO/magic-link session has `null` (key never written), `null !== false`, so the `two_factor_pending: true` flag is never returned by the SPA bootstrap endpoint. The SPA therefore cannot proactively redirect to the challenge page on load; it learns about the 2FA requirement only after the first 423 from a protected API call. This introduces a race window: the SPA briefly renders partial dashboard state before the 423 forces the challenge redirect.

In summary: 2FA is not bypassed — the middleware enforces it correctly because `null` is falsy. The defect is that `AuthController::user()` uses strict equality while the middleware uses truthy equality, creating an inconsistency that causes the SPA to miss the proactive `two_factor_pending` signal for SSO and magic-link sessions. The user lands on the dashboard momentarily, sees partial content, and then gets redirected to the challenge. This is a low-severity UX/race condition, not a security bypass. The suggested fix (write the session flag in both controllers) is still the right correction, but the justification in the finding is wrong about the mechanism.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is still correct — both SsoController::callback() and MagicLinkController::consume() should write `martis_two_factor_passed` (false when 2FA is enabled, true otherwise) after login, mirroring AuthController::login() lines 130-131. This removes the strict/truthy asymmetry and gives AuthController::user() the `=== false` value it needs to return `two_factor_pending: true` on SPA bootstrap. The security impact is limited to a momentary partial-render race, not a bypass, so severity is low rather than critical.

title: AuthController::user() 2FA pending check uses === false, misses the null initial session state — SPA bootstrap does not redirect to challenge for users who logged in via SSO or magic link
suggestedFix:
The session key is absent (null) for users who logged in via SSO or magic link, and also on any session that predates the 2FA flag introduction. `null === false` is `false`, so the pending check is silently skipped. The EnsureTwoFactorChallenge middleware correctly gates with `!$request->session()->get(...)` (truthiness), so the two surfaces are asymmetric.

Change the condition to:
```php
if ($twoFactor->isEnabled($user) && $twoFactorPassed !== true) {
```
This treats null and false the same (both mean 'not yet passed'), matching the middleware's intent.

verdict.reasoning: The defect is confirmed by reading the actual code. At AuthController.php:55 the condition is `$twoFactorPassed === false` (strict). Session key `martis_two_factor_passed` is only ever explicitly put to `false` by the password-login path (AuthController::login() line 131). SSO login (SsoController) never touches this key — it only writes `martis_sso_provider`. Magic-link and any session predating the key also leave it absent (null). When the value is null, `null === false` evaluates to false in PHP, so the `two_factor_pending` hint is silently skipped and the controller returns the full user payload. Meanwhile EnsureTwoFactorChallenge::requiresChallenge() at line 78 uses a plain truthiness check (`if ($request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed'))` → proceeds when falsy, meaning null and false are both treated as "not yet passed"). The two surfaces are genuinely asymmetric: the middleware correctly blocks on null/false, but the user() bootstrap endpoint only signals pending on false. The practical consequence for an SSO user with 2FA enabled is: (a) SPA bootstrap sees a full user payload, navigates to the dashboard, then (b) every protected API call returns 423 from the middleware, producing a hard-broken UX. The suggested fix (`$twoFactorPassed !== true`) unifies the two checks correctly. Severity is high because it affects a real user-visible flow (SSO + 2FA together), not a theoretical edge case.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. One additional note: after applying `!== true`, also verify that the `two_factor_pending` response shape sent by `user()` is handled by the SPA the same way as the `two_factor_required` shape sent by `login()`. The login flow returns `two_factor_required` (line 133) while the user-bootstrap flow returns `two_factor_pending` (line 57); confirm the React bootstrap handler checks for `two_factor_pending` specifically, not just `two_factor_required`.

title: SsoController: on_no_role_match = 'callable' strategy is undocumented in the controller — hook fires regardless of strategy, and if the hook returns null + strategy is 'callable' the user is logged in with no roles

verdict.reasoning:
Both files confirm the defect exactly as claimed.

SsoController.php lines 92-108: `fireNoRoleMatch()` is called unconditionally on every `$roles->isEmpty()` path, regardless of `$strategy`. There is no `if ($strategy === 'callable')` guard around the hook invocation.

SsoManager.php lines 201-208: `fireNoRoleMatch()` returns `null` when `$onNoRoleMatchCallback === null` (no callback registered). The return type annotation is `mixed`, so `null` is a valid return.

After the hook call, the controller checks `$hookResult instanceof RedirectResponse` (false when hook returns null) and then `$strategy === 'deny'` (false when strategy is 'callable'). No other branch exists. Execution falls through to Step 3 (role sync with an empty collection), Step 4 (`fireAfterLogin`), and Step 5 (`Auth::guard($guard)->login($user, true)`). The user is logged in with no roles.

The SsoManager docblock for `onNoRoleMatchUsing` states the callback fires "when `on_no_role_match` is `'callable'`", but the controller does not honour that contract — it fires unconditionally. A consumer who sets `on_no_role_match = 'callable'` believing the strategy means "my callback will be called and can deny" but forgets to actually register the callback via `MartisSso::onNoRoleMatchUsing(...)` gets silent login bypass.

There is no other guard elsewhere: the only checks in the block are the `RedirectResponse` instanceof test and the `'deny'` string comparison. The `'callable'` strategy string is never matched anywhere in the controller.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct and sufficient. The safest single-line fix is to add, immediately after the `instanceof RedirectResponse` check: `if ($strategy === 'callable' && $hookResult === null) { return redirect()->route('martis.login')->withErrors(['sso' => __('martis::messages.sso_no_role_match')]); }`. An equally valid approach is to move the hook call inside a `if ($strategy === 'callable')` guard so it is never fired for `'deny'` or `'guest'` strategies, which also corrects the secondary issue (hook fires unconditionally for all strategies, not just 'callable' as documented). The severity stays medium rather than high because exploiting it requires the consumer to have set strategy='callable' without registering the callback — it is a misconfiguration-assisted bypass, not a direct external attack vector.

title: TwoFactorService::hasLastUsedColumn() uses a process-level static cache — incorrect result when tests switch user models or when two different user tables are used in the same process

verdict.reasoning:
The code exactly matches the quoted evidence. At /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Profile/TwoFactorService.php:306-321, `hasLastUsedColumn()` uses `static $cached = null` — a single PHP process-level boolean that is written once on first call and never keyed by connection or table. This is confirmed by MartisServiceProvider.php:114 which registers TwoFactorService as a singleton, meaning the object instance (and therefore the method's static variable, which is process-global anyway) lives for the full process lifetime.

However, the severity is lower than claimed. The reasons to downgrade from medium:

1. Martis is explicitly a single-tenant admin panel package. There is one users table on one connection per installation. The method comment on lines 263-266 documents this is intentional: "Probe the schema once per install." The scenario where two different user tables/connections are used in the same process does not apply to the supported use case.

2. The existing test suite (TwoFactorServiceTest.php) never reaches `hasLastUsedColumn` in practice: the test for `verifyForUser` (line 45-51) passes an unconfirmed user, causing `verifyForUser` to return false at line 108-110 before `verifyAndTrack` is ever called. No test exercises a confirmed user with a valid OTP, so test-isolation contamination is not a current concern.

3. The fix is a genuine improvement (keying the cache by connection:table) and makes the code more defensive for future test additions or hypothetical multi-tenant consumers, but it does not fix any currently manifesting bug in production or in the test suite.

The defect is real — the implementation is demonstrably incorrect for multi-table or multi-connection scenarios — but the practical impact under Martis's current architecture and test suite is negligible. Severity is low rather than medium.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (keying the static cache by `$user->getConnectionName().':'.$user->getTable()`) is correct and worthwhile as a defensive improvement. However, the additional suggestion to expose a `resetCacheForTesting()` method is only needed if tests ever exercise a confirmed-user valid-OTP path with different table schemas in the same process. The current test suite does not reach `hasLastUsedColumn` at all (verifyForUser returns early for unconfirmed users), so no test harness change is immediately required. Apply the key-based cache fix; defer the testing helper until a test actually exercises this path.

title: MagicLinkController::autoRegister() uses new Model([...]) without forceFill — relies on $fillable to include email/name/password, which may silently fail on custom user models

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/MagicLinkController.php:133-138 is confirmed. `autoRegister()` calls `new $userClass(['email' => ..., 'name' => ..., 'password' => ...])`, which internally invokes `Model::fill()` through the constructor (vendor/laravel/framework Eloquent Model line 321 explicitly calls `$this->fill($attributes)`). When a consumer user model declares a `$fillable` array that does not include those three fields — or uses a `$guarded = ['*']` pattern — Eloquent silently drops all three attributes. `$user->save()` then persists a row with null email, null name, and null password. The subsequent `resolveUser($consumedEmail)` call (MagicLinkController.php:97) uses `retrieveByCredentials(['email' => $email])`, which finds nothing because the stored email column is null, leaving the user permanently unable to sign in via magic link despite the row existing.

The comparison to `IdentityResolver::create()` (src/Sso/IdentityResolver.php:113-122) is valid and directly on-point: that method for the same semantic operation — creating a user record for a package-provisioned identity — uses `(new $userClass)->forceFill([...])` explicitly to bypass mass-assignment guards. The same pattern is applied again at line 145 for `syncAttributes`. The inconsistency is internal to the package.

The test suite (tests/Feature/MagicLinkTest.php) never tests the `auto_register` path at all — every test either seeds a user with `User::forceCreate()` or exercises the "user not found, no registration" branch. The guarded-model scenario is completely uncovered.

The suggested fix (`forceFill`) is correct and is already the package's own established idiom. Severity is medium and not high: exploitation requires (a) `martis.auth.magic_link.auto_register` explicitly set to `true`, which defaults to false, and (b) the consumer user model guarding `email`/`name`/`password`, which is non-default but a legitimate consumer configuration. No privilege escalation is possible; the impact is silent silent data corruption and a permanently broken authentication state for affected accounts.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as stated. Use `(new $userClass)->forceFill([...])->save()` to match the pattern already established in `IdentityResolver::create()` at line 115. Additionally, add a post-save assertion — after `$user->save()`, verify `$user->getAttribute('email') === $email` and return null (or throw) if it does not match, to prevent a silently-corrupted row from being returned as a valid Authenticatable. The `MagicLinkTest.php` suite also needs a test case that configures `auto_register = true`, uses a user model with `$guarded = ['*']` or a restricted `$fillable`, and asserts the returned user has the correct email attribute set.

title: ActionController.fields() returns action field schema without checking authorizedToSee
suggestedFix: Add `if (! $actionInstance->authorizedToSee($request)) { return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('...')->toResponse(); }` before calling `$actionInstance->fields()`. This aligns with the execute() method which already checks authorizedToSee at line 142.

verdict.reasoning:
Reading the actual code confirms the defect.

The `fields()` method at lines 98-119 calls `findAction()` (line 107), which at lines 282-293 iterates `$resource->actions($request)` with NO authorization filter — it simply matches on `uriKey()`. In contrast, `resolveActions()` (lines 271-279) wraps the same `$resource->actions($request)` call with an `array_filter(..., fn => $action->authorizedToSee($request))` guard.

The `index()` method at line 75 uses `resolveActions()` (filtered). The `execute()` method at line 142 calls `authorizedToSee` explicitly after `findAction`. The `executePivot()` method at line 566 also checks `authorizedToSee` after `findAction`. `fields()` is the only public endpoint that calls `findAction()` without following up with the authorization check.

The route at line 429 sits inside the full `martis.auth` + `martis.2fa` + `martis.verified` middleware stack (lines 181-205), so unauthenticated requests are blocked. The real-world exposure is: an authenticated but lower-privilege user (e.g. a user to whom `->canSee(fn($r) => $r->user()->isAdmin())` denies the action) can still issue `GET /api/resources/{resource}/actions/{action}/fields` and receive the field schema — input types, labels, validation rules, option lists — for an action that is intentionally hidden from them. This leaks field structure and could reveal sensitive field metadata (e.g. the fact that a "Suspend Account" action exists and its field shape).

The suggested fix is correct: add `if (! $actionInstance->authorizedToSee($request)) { return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('...')->toResponse(); }` immediately after the null check at line 111, mirroring the pattern already used in `execute()` at line 142 and `executePivot()` at line 566.

Severity is medium rather than high. The route requires full authentication and 2FA (where enabled). No model data or mutations are exposed — only action field schema metadata. Still a genuine information-disclosure gap that breaks the intended access model.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as stated. Add the `authorizedToSee` guard at line 112 (after the null check on `$actionInstance`), exactly as `execute()` does at line 142. No other changes needed. The `findAction` helper itself does not need to change — the caller is responsible for authorization, consistent with the existing pattern in the other three endpoints that use `findAction`.

title: ActionController leaks raw exception message to HTTP clients in 500 responses
suggestedFix: Do not pass $e->getMessage() to serverError(). Log the message (already done on the preceding Log::error() call at lines 231-236 and 629-634) and return a generic message: `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action execution failed.')->toResponse()`. The docblock explicitly states this method should not expose internal details.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both the ActionController and JsonErrorResponse source. At line 246 of ActionController.php, `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action failed: '.$e->getMessage())->toResponse()` is called inside a `catch (\Throwable $e)` block. The same pattern appears at line 640 for the pivot variant.

The `serverError()` method at JsonErrorResponse.php:103 is a plain pass-through: it stores the argument in `$this->message` and `toArray()` at line 117 puts it directly into the JSON response payload as `{"message": "...", "errors": []}`. There is no filtering, redaction, or sanitization inside `serverError()`.

The docblock at lines 100-101 claims "Never exposes internal error details to the client," but the implementation does not enforce this: any string the caller supplies, including raw `$e->getMessage()` text, becomes the HTTP response body. The docblock describes intent, not behavior.

Raw PHP exception messages can contain: database driver error text (including table/column names, full SQL), file-system paths, class names, and stack-trace fragments (when exceptions are constructed with context in their message). All of this would be delivered verbatim to the browser in a 500 response.

There is no Laravel exception handler wrapping this path that would suppress the message; the method returns an `IlluminateJsonResponse` directly. The preceding `Log::error()` calls (lines 232-236, 629-634) already capture the detail server-side, so the detail is not needed in the response.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is straightforward: drop the message argument at both call sites so the default generic string is used. `JsonErrorResponse::serverError()->toResponse()` is sufficient at lines 246 and 640. The `serverError()` docblock should also be amended to remove the "Never exposes internal error details" claim, or the implementation should be changed to hard-code the generic message and ignore any passed argument, to make the guarantee real rather than aspirational.

title: LensController cache key excludes user identity, enabling cross-user data leakage when TTL > 0
suggestedFix: Add `$userKey` to the cache key parts. The user identifier must be part of the key whenever `cacheTtl() > 0` because lenses filter records via `authorizedToViewAny` and consumer-defined `indexQuery` scoping — different users legitimately see different row sets. Pattern to follow: `$request->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest'` as used in NavigationController and ResourceController.

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is confirmed by reading the actual code.

buildCacheKey() at LensController.php:252-280 assembles 12 parts (lens class hash, uriKey, tableVersion, search, sortColumn, sortDirection, filter hash, trashed, perPage, page, locale) and does NOT include any user identifier. The $request object is available in index() but is not passed to buildCacheKey(), so there is no indirect injection of the user key either.

Caching is gated on $ttl > 0 at line 145. The Lens base class sets cacheTtlSeconds = 0 (Lens.php:69), so the vulnerable path only activates when a developer explicitly calls $lens->cacheFor(...). This is opt-in, not on by default.

The tableVersion guard (COUNT(*)+MAX(updated_at), lines 289-302) is a global table-level signal. It does not differentiate between users: if user A's query is cached and user B has the same lens parameters but different row-level visibility (enforced inside the lens query() method via $request->user()), user B receives user A's cached result until a row mutation elsewhere in the table invalidates the key.

Two sibling controllers in the same codebase (NavigationController and ResourceController) already use $request->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest' in their cache keys, confirming the team knows the pattern and deliberately applies it when user-scoped caching is needed. LensController omits it.

Severity is high (not critical): the vulnerability requires an explicit developer opt-in via cacheFor(), so it does not affect every deployment by default. When it does activate on a lens whose query() scopes rows by user identity, the result is cross-user data leakage — a real confidentiality breach.
verdict.fixNote: The fix must add the user identifier to buildCacheKey() and pass $request (or the resolved identifier) through from index(). The private signature should become buildCacheKey(Lens, LensRequest, int, int, string, string, string $userKey): string, where $userKey = (string) ($request->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest') is resolved in index() and appended to $parts. This matches the pattern already used in NavigationController and ResourceController. No additional guard (e.g. "only add user key when cacheTtl > 0") is needed because buildCacheKey() is only called unconditionally from index() and the Cache::remember() branch is already gated on $ttl > 0 — an unused cache key is harmless.

title: NotificationController.markRead() passes potentially-null result of fresh() to a non-nullable typed parameter
suggestedFix: Either re-read from the database with a fresh query instead of calling fresh(), or add a null guard: `$fresh = $notification->fresh(); if ($fresh === null) { return new IlluminateJsonResponse(['data' => $this->serialize($notification)]); } return new IlluminateJsonResponse(['data' => $this->serialize($fresh)]);`. The race condition is rare (a concurrent delete between markAsRead and fresh()) but the TypeError is a real crash path.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/NotificationController.php line 115 is exactly as described. After `$notification->markAsRead()` on line 113, the code immediately calls `$notification->fresh()` and passes the result directly to `serialize(DatabaseNotification $notification)` with no null guard. `Model::fresh()` returns `?static`, meaning it returns null when the row no longer exists. If a concurrent request (e.g. another `destroy()` or `clearAll()` call from the same user) deletes that notification between line 113 and line 115, `fresh()` returns null, and PHP 8's strict parameter type enforcement throws a `TypeError` because `null` is not a `DatabaseNotification`. There is no guard in the call chain that catches or handles this. The `instanceof` check on line 109 only validates the result of the initial `first()` query; it offers no protection for the post-write `fresh()` call. The finding is confirmed. Severity is low because the window is tiny and requires a concurrent delete of the exact same notification, but the crash path is real and unguarded.
verdict.fixNote: The simplest safe fix is to skip the re-fetch entirely and pass the already-loaded `$notification` directly: `return new IlluminateJsonResponse(['data' => $this->serialize($notification)]);`. The `read_at` field will still be set correctly because `markAsRead()` updates the model's attribute in memory as well as persisting it. Alternatively, if the fresh DB state is strictly required, add a null guard: `$fresh = $notification->fresh(); return new IlluminateJsonResponse(['data' => $this->serialize($fresh ?? $notification)]);`. Using the in-memory model is preferable as it eliminates the extra query and the race entirely.

title: ActionController.executePivot() does not verify the caller is authorized to view the parent record
suggestedFix: After instantiating `$parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel)`, add `if (! $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request)) { return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('This action is unauthorized.')->toResponse(); }` before proceeding to resolve pivot columns and execute the action.

verdict.reasoning:
Reading the actual code confirms the defect.

In `executePivot()` (/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/ActionController.php:546-571):

- Line 547: `$parentModel = $modelClass::find($id)` — the parent record is fetched.
- Line 566: `$actionInstance->authorizedToSee($request)` — only the action's own visibility flag is checked; this is not a record-level policy gate.
- Line 571: `$parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel)` — a resource instance wrapping the parent is created.
- No call to `$parentInstance->authorizedToView($request)` appears anywhere in the method.

The established pattern for all pivot-route access in the codebase is shown in `BelongsToManyController` at lines 624-628, which does:
```php
$parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel);
if (! $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request)) {
    return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('This action is unauthorized.')->toResponse();
}
```
This exact guard is missing from `executePivot()`.

`Resource::authorizedToView()` calls `checkPolicy('view', $this->model)`, which (when a policy is registered) invokes `$policy->view($user, $model)` and defaults to `false` when the method is absent (see `defaultForMissingAbility` at line 870: "view → forbidden"). The route lives behind `martis.auth` + `martis.2fa`, so the caller is authenticated — but authentication is not the same as record-level authorization. An authenticated user lacking the `view` policy for a specific parent record can POST to `/api/resources/{resource}/{id}/belongs-to-many/{relationship}/actions/{action}`, reach the action handler, and invoke pivot actions against that record's relationship data. This is a classic IDOR.
verdict.fixNote: The fix matches the suggested approach exactly, and the sibling controller already shows the correct pattern. After `$parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel)` at line 571, insert: `if (! $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request)) { return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('This action is unauthorized.')->toResponse(); }`. No other changes are needed.

title: InstallCommand::runMigrations() omits --force, causing interactive prompt in production and CI hang
suggestedFix: Pass `['--force' => true]` to the migrate call, matching the pattern already used in RolesScaffoldCommand and SsoMakeCommand: `$this->call('migrate', ['--force' => true]);`. In production environments Laravel's `migrate` command requires explicit `--force` confirmation or it will interactively prompt 'Do you really want to run this command?' and block forever in CI or hang a Docker-compose exec invocation.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Console/InstallCommand.php:1194 reads `$this->call('migrate');` with no arguments. In Laravel, when `APP_ENV=production` the `migrate` command guards itself with `confirmToProceed()`, which either throws an exception (in non-interactive/CI contexts) or blocks on a "Do you really want to run this command?" prompt, unless `--force` is explicitly passed. There is no environment guard, no `confirmToProceed` override, and no propagation of the command's own `--force` flag to the migrate call in `runMigrations()`. The sibling commands RolesScaffoldCommand (line 267) and SsoMakeCommand (line 263) both correctly pass `['--force' => true]` to the same migrate call. The `handle()` method at line 42 calls `runMigrations()` unconditionally after the publish steps, so any production install will hit this path. The finding is real.
verdict.fixNote: Pass `['--force' => true]` to the migrate call: `$this->call('migrate', ['--force' => true]);`. This matches the pattern already used in RolesScaffoldCommand and SsoMakeCommand and is the standard Laravel idiom for installer commands that are expected to run non-interactively in production and CI pipelines.

title: ThemeMakeCommand::rewriteThemeNameInConfig regex silently fails when the theme block contains any nested array
suggestedFix: The character class `[^\]]` stops matching at the first `]` it encounters. For the canonical config (no nested arrays in the theme block) this works, but any user-extended config that adds a sub-array (e.g. `'accents' => ['blue', 'red']`) will cause the regex to capture only up to the inner `]` and miss the `name` key entirely. The config is silently left unchanged while the CSS and public/ files ARE written, leaving config out of sync with the deployed theme. Replace the character-class approach with a parenthesis-depth-aware traversal (similar to ListEnvVarsCommand::captureBalancedExpression) or use a /s greedy match on the full block: `/('theme'\s*=>\s*)(\[[^;]+?^    \])/ms`.

verdict.reasoning:
The regex at line 117 is `"/('theme'\s*=>\s*\[)([^\]]*)(\])/s"`. The character class `[^\]]*` is greedy but stops the moment it sees any `]`, including the inner closing bracket of a sub-array. PHP testing confirms: with the canonical published config (all scalar values — `'default'`, `'allowToggle'`, `'name'`), the regex works perfectly. However, when a user adds any sub-array BEFORE the `'name'` key in their published `config/martis.php` (e.g. `'accents' => ['blue', 'red']`), group 2 captures only up to the inner `]`, `'name'` is absent from the body, and the regex-replace callback silently leaves the config unchanged while CSS and public/ files are written — exactly the desync described.

The severity is lower than claimed for two reasons: (1) the canonical config shipped by the package has NO nested arrays in the `'theme'` block, so the bug only bites users who manually extend that block with a sub-array (not a documented or advertised API); (2) even when it fires, the failure mode is "config not updated" — the command still prints a success message and the files are written, so the user is left with an inconsistency that is confusing but not destructive. The command also shows the user the correct `config/martis.php` edit in the terminal output (line 74), so a careful user can fix it manually. The silent mismatch is the real harm. Severity should be low rather than medium: it requires user-extended config and the outcome is a warning-less no-op rather than data loss or a crash.
verdict.fixNote: The bug is real but only affects user-extended configs that add sub-arrays to the `'theme'` block before the `'name'` key. The canonical config (`/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/config/martis.php` lines 508-512) is entirely flat scalars, so no published install is currently affected. The simplest safe fix is to match the closing `]` that is indented at the same level as the opening one — for example anchoring on `\n    ]` — rather than the first `]` encountered. The suggested `ListEnvVarsCommand::captureBalancedExpression` approach is also valid but heavier than needed.

title: UserCommand accepts plaintext --password via CLI option, exposing credential in process list and shell history
suggestedFix: The `--password=` option is a documented convenience that leaks the plaintext credential in `ps aux`, `/proc/*/cmdline`, and shell history on any multi-user system. Remove the option from the signature or mark it `@deprecated` and add a clear warning if it is used: `if ($this->option('password') !== null) { $this->components->warn('Passing --password on the CLI exposes it in the process list. Use the interactive prompt instead.'); }`. The fallback `$this->secret('Password')` is already the safe path.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Console/UserCommand.php:14 declares `{--password= : The password for the admin user}` in the command signature, and line 27 reads it with `$this->option('password')` with no guard, warning, or deprecation notice. A caller can run `php artisan martis:user --password=mysecret`, which places the plaintext credential in the process list (`ps aux`), `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`, and shell history. The safe interactive fallback `$this->secret('Password')` is only reached when the option is absent. There is no existing warning path that discourages the option use. The finding is confirmed exactly as described. Severity "high" is appropriate: this is a standard server/CI context where the Artisan command would be run, and process-list credential leaks on multi-user Linux systems are a well-established attack surface.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. The minimal safe change is to add a `$this->components->warn(...)` call when `$this->option('password') !== null`, directing users to the interactive prompt instead. Removing the option entirely is the safest path but may break existing automation scripts; a deprecation warning preserves backward compatibility while signaling the risk. The interactive `$this->secret()` path is already the right implementation.

title: UserCommand does not check for existing email before creating the admin user, causing an unhandled DB unique-constraint exception on re-run
suggestedFix: Before calling `save()`, check whether a user with the given email already exists and abort with a clear message: `if ($modelClass::query()->where('email', $email)->exists()) { $this->components->error("A user with email [{$email}] already exists."); return self::FAILURE; }`. Without this guard, running `martis:user` twice with the same email throws a PDO/QueryException with a cryptic UNIQUE constraint violation instead of a friendly error.

verdict.reasoning: I read /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Console/UserCommand.php in full (51 lines). The handle() method performs exactly one guard before persisting: an empty-password check at lines 29-33. There is no duplicate-email check anywhere in the file — no ->exists() query, no firstOrCreate/firstOrNew, no try/catch around save(), and no unique validation via Laravel's Validator facade. Lines 38-43 create a bare model instance, set attributes, and call save() unconditionally. The users table in any standard Laravel app carries a UNIQUE constraint on the email column, so a second invocation with the same email will throw an unhandled Illuminate\Database\QueryException with a PDO "UNIQUE constraint failed" message rather than a friendly CLI error. The finding is genuine. Severity remains medium: the command is developer/operator-facing only (not an end-user surface), so exploitability is low and there is no security implication, but the failure mode is a raw exception trace rather than a graceful message, which is a clear correctness/UX defect.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. Add `if ($modelClass::query()->where('email', $email)->exists()) { $this->components->error("A user with email [{$email}] already exists."); return self::FAILURE; }` immediately before line 38. Alternatively, wrapping save() in a try/catch for QueryException would also work but is less informative to the operator. The explicit guard is the cleaner approach.

title: ThemeMakeCommand::handle() calls bare $this->confirm() without a TTY guard, blocking piped/non-PTY invocations
evidence: if (file_exists($path) && ! $this->confirm("Theme '{$name}.css' already exists. Overwrite?")) {
suggestedFix: InstallCommand implements the dual-gate pattern `$this->input->isInteractive() && $this->stdinIsTty()` precisely because `docker-compose exec -T` and CI pipes set `isInteractive()=true` while stdin has no TTY. ThemeMakeCommand uses the bare Symfony `$this->confirm()` without this gate. Add a check before the confirm: if not interactive or running unit tests, treat an existing file as blocking and return FAILURE with a message suggesting `--force`. Alternatively, add a `--force` option to ThemeMakeCommand and gate the confirm on `! $this->option('force') && $this->input->isInteractive() && $this->stdinIsTty()`.

verdict.reasoning: The code at ThemeMakeCommand.php:29 is exactly as cited: `if (file_exists($path) && ! $this->confirm("Theme '{$name}.css' already exists. Overwrite?"))` with no interactivity guard of any kind. The command's $signature declares only `{name?}` — there is no `--force` option. Every other command in the same Console directory that calls confirm() first checks `$this->input->isInteractive()` (and often also `runningUnitTests()` and/or `stdinIsTty()`): CardMakeCommand:115, FieldMakeCommand:108, ComponentMakeCommand:266, ToolMakeCommand:234, SsoMakeCommand:313, InstallCommand:78-80. ThemeMakeCommand is the sole command that reaches confirm() unconditionally. In a non-TTY context (CI, docker-compose exec -T, Artisan::call() in tests) where the file already exists, Symfony's Question helper either blocks or throws, and in any case the command exits with FAILURE rather than overwriting. The defect is real and consistent with the codebase's own established pattern that the reporter identified.
verdict.fixNote: The fix needs two changes: (1) add `{--force : Overwrite an existing theme file without prompting}` to $signature, and (2) replace the bare confirm with `! $this->option('force') && $this->input->isInteractive() && ! app()->runningUnitTests() && $this->stdinIsTty()` before calling confirm — matching the InstallCommand dual-gate pattern. Without --force, automation has no way to bypass the prompt even after the guard is added.

title: SsoMakeCommand provider name is only lowercased but not sanitized to valid identifier characters, producing invalid SQL column names for custom providers
suggestedFix: A custom provider name like `my-provider` or `my provider` produces a column name `my-provider_group_name` or `my provider_group_name`, which is an invalid SQL identifier on every supported database. Add a sanitization step after `strtolower`: `$name = preg_replace('/[^a-z0-9_]/', '_', $name);` and validate that the result is non-empty and begins with a letter or underscore. Emit an error and return INVALID if the name fails the check.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at line 54 applies only `strtolower` with no further sanitization. For KNOWN_PROVIDERS (`azure`, `google`, `github`) this is irrelevant because the values are hardcoded clean identifiers. For the `--custom` path (lines 61-66), any string that is non-empty passes through — e.g. `my-provider` or `my provider`.

Line 467 (`renderConfigBlock`) writes `'role_column' => 'my-provider_group_name'` into a PHP config file as a string value — that is fine, no SQL involved yet.

Line 536 (`publishMigration`) substitutes `my-provider_group_name` into the stub, which at stub line 30 becomes `$table->string('my-provider_group_name')`. Laravel's `Blueprint::string()` passes the name through the database grammar's `wrapValue()`, which quotes it (backticks on MySQL, double quotes on Postgres/SQLite). So the migration itself runs without error even for hyphenated names.

Line 347 (`mapExistingRoles`) passes `$column` into `DB::table('roles')->select(['id', 'name', $column])` — again, Laravel's query builder quotes the identifier, so hyphens survive.

The defect is therefore narrower than claimed: it is real but limited to contexts where the column name is interpolated into raw SQL without quoting (e.g. a future caller using `DB::statement("ALTER TABLE roles ADD {$column} ...")` or a raw WHERE clause). Within the existing code, Laravel's ORM/schema layer handles quoting correctly. A space in the provider name (`my provider`) would produce `my provider_group_name`, which Laravel's grammar would quote as `"my provider_group_name"` — valid DDL on Postgres/SQLite but an error on MySQL where quoted identifiers still cannot contain raw spaces in some contexts.

The reporter's claim that it "produces an invalid SQL column name on every supported database" is overstated for Laravel code that routes through the query builder/schema builder. The real residual risk is: (1) spaces in the name (not hyphens) could cause MySQL issues even with quoting; (2) any downstream code that interpolates the config value into raw SQL without quoting would break; (3) the migration filename at line 523 (`"add_{$name}_group_name_to_roles_table"`) uses the unsanitized name in a filesystem path, which is a minor path-safety concern for names containing `/` or `..`.

This is a real issue in the `--custom` path but the severity is lower than "high/medium" for a developer-controlled CLI command where the main ORM path handles quoting. Adjusted severity: low. The suggested fix (sanitize to `[a-z0-9_]`) is still sound defensive practice.
verdict.fixNote: The original suggested fix is correct in principle but the framing overstates the blast radius. Within the existing code, Laravel's Schema Builder and QueryBuilder quote column identifiers correctly, so hyphens do not break the generated migration or the role-mapping query. The genuine residual risks are: (1) spaces in the provider name can cause MySQL DDL errors even with quoting; (2) the unsanitized name is interpolated into a filesystem path (migration filename, line 523) — a name containing `/` or `..` would be a path traversal; (3) any future caller reading `role_column` from config and interpolating it into a raw SQL string would break. Sanitizing with `preg_replace('/[^a-z0-9_]/', '_', $name)` after `strtolower` and rejecting empty/leading-digit results is the right fix. This is developer-controlled CLI input (not a runtime user-controlled boundary), which keeps the severity low.

title: 2FA bypass on /api/auth/user: strict === false check misses null (session key absent)
suggestedFix: The /api/auth/user endpoint is intentionally public (outside the martis.2fa middleware group) and performs its own 2FA gate. The session key martis_two_factor_passed is set to false only by AuthController::login() when a password login triggers 2FA. When a user arrives via magic-link (MagicLinkController::consume), SSO, or any other path that never calls login(), the key is absent from the session and ->get() returns null. null === false is false in PHP, so the gate is skipped and the endpoint returns the full user payload as if 2FA were completed. The EnsureTwoFactorChallenge middleware (line 78) correctly uses a truthy check — ->get() returning null still triggers the challenge there. Fix: change the condition to use the same semantics: if ($twoFactor->isEnabled($user) && !$request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed')). This mirrors the middleware and treats both null and false as pending.

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is confirmed by reading the actual code.

AuthController::user() at line 55 uses `$twoFactorPassed === false` (strict equality). When a user authenticates via MagicLinkController::consume() (lines 107-108) or SsoController::callback() (lines 119-127), neither controller writes `martis_two_factor_passed` to the session. The key is therefore absent, and `$request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed')` returns null. In PHP, `null === false` evaluates to false, so the two_factor_pending block is skipped and the full user payload is returned.

The EnsureTwoFactorChallenge middleware at line 78 uses a truthy check (`if ($request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed'))`), which correctly treats null as "challenge not yet passed." However, the middleware does not cover the /api/auth/user route — AuthController's docblock at line 34 explicitly states "This route is public" and it sits outside the martis.2fa middleware group.

The practical impact is bounded: the middleware still enforces 2FA on every protected data endpoint (returning 423), so raw data cannot be extracted. What is leaked is the full user payload from /api/auth/user itself (name, email, roles, avatar_url, safeUserArray contents) and the SPA navigation logic — the frontend never receives `two_factor_pending: true` on bootstrap, so it does not redirect to /2fa/challenge. Depending on what safeUserArray returns and what client-side routing does with an apparently-authenticated-but-unprompted user, the exposure varies from information disclosure to a degraded-but-not-fully-blocked auth bypass.

The suggested fix (change to `!$request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed')`) matches the middleware's semantics and is correct.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. Change line 55 from `$twoFactorPassed === false` to `!$twoFactorPassed` (or equivalently `!$request->session()->get('martis_two_factor_passed')`). This aligns AuthController::user() with the semantics already used by EnsureTwoFactorChallenge::requiresChallenge() at line 78 of the middleware. Severity is medium rather than high because EnsureTwoFactorChallenge still blocks all protected data routes with 423; the bypass is limited to the user info payload and SPA routing, not arbitrary data access.

title: MagicLinkController::autoRegister uses model constructor with potential mass-assignment silent failure
suggestedFix: The constructor path respects $fillable / $guarded on the consumer's User model. Laravel 11+ ships apps with $guarded = ['*'] by default (all attributes guarded). When auto_register=true and the consumer's model has strict guarding, all three attributes (email, name, password) are silently dropped, save() persists an empty/partial row, and the account is created with null email, null name, and null password. The null password is especially dangerous: some drivers treat null as an empty string which can match a hash. Fix: use forceFill() instead of the constructor, matching the pattern in DefaultRegistersUsers::register() (line 44). Additionally, Registered event should be fired for auto-registered users to trigger email verification / other listeners, and the registration feature flag (martis.auth.registration.enabled) should be consulted before auto-registering.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by direct code inspection.

`MagicLinkController::autoRegister()` at lines 133-138 uses the Eloquent mass-assignment constructor (`new $userClass([...])`) which internally calls `fill()`, respecting the consumer model's `$fillable` / `$guarded`. If the consumer's User model has `$guarded = ['*']` (the Laravel 11 default skeleton) or omits `email`, `name`, and `password` from `$fillable`, all three attributes are silently dropped and `save()` persists a row with `null` email, `null` name, and `null` password — the latter being the most dangerous because some hash drivers treat null as an empty string that may inadvertently match.

The playground's User model (line 14 of `martis-playground/app/Models/User.php`) uses `#[Fillable(['name', 'email', 'password'])]` so the bug is masked locally; a different consumer model without explicit fillable declarations would trigger the silent failure.

The contrast with `DefaultRegistersUsers::register()` (line 44 of `src/Auth/DefaultRegistersUsers.php`) is direct evidence: the canonical registration path uses `forceFill()` which bypasses mass-assignment guards. `autoRegister()` does not use `forceFill()`, despite doing the same logical operation.

Two additional confirmed gaps in `autoRegister()` that the reporter correctly flags:
1. No `Registered` event fired — `DefaultRegistersUsers` fires it at line 80; `autoRegister()` never does, causing email verification listeners, audit hooks, and role-assignment listeners to silently skip for magic-link auto-registrations.
2. No check against `martis.auth.registration.enabled` before creating the user, meaning a site operator who disabled open registration is still exposed to account creation via the magic-link surface when `auto_register=true`.

The fix path — replacing the constructor with `forceFill()` to match the `DefaultRegistersUsers` pattern, adding the `Registered` event dispatch, and consulting `martis.auth.registration.enabled` — is sound and aligns with the existing codebase idiom.
verdict.fixNote: The reporter's suggested fix is correct: replace `new $userClass([...])` with `(new $userClass)->forceFill([...])` to match `DefaultRegistersUsers::register()` at line 44. In addition, dispatch `event(new Registered($user))` after save (same as line 80 of DefaultRegistersUsers), and gate the method on `config('martis.auth.registration.enabled', true)` before creating the row. The severity is adjusted to high rather than medium because a null password can silently persist in the database and downstream password-authentication paths may behave unpredictably against a null hash; the only mitigating factor keeping this from critical is that `auto_register` defaults to false.

title: Dead ternary in RecordImpersonation: both branches of $target instanceof Model return the same value
evidence: $targetType = $target instanceof Model ? $target::class : $target::class;
suggestedFix: Both branches of the ternary evaluate to $target::class — the instanceof check has no effect. The original intent was probably to use get_class($target) in one branch, or to produce a different value for non-Model Authenticatable implementations (e.g. a fallback string or a short class name). As written the check is dead code and creates a false impression of defensive handling. Replace with: $targetType = $target::class;

verdict.reasoning: Line 55 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Auth/Listeners/RecordImpersonation.php reads exactly: `$targetType = $target instanceof Model ? $target::class : $target::class;`. Both branches of the ternary evaluate to the identical expression `$target::class`, so the `instanceof Model` check has zero effect on the assigned value. There is no compensating logic elsewhere in the file or in callers — `$targetType` is used directly in the `ActionEvent::create()` call on lines 63, 65, and 67. The finding is confirmed. Severity stays low: the produced value (`$target::class`) is correct in all cases (it is always the FQCN of the target), so nothing breaks at runtime. The bug is purely a dead/misleading code structure that falsely implies non-Model Authenticatable targets would be handled differently.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct: replace the entire ternary with `$targetType = $target::class;`. No behavioral change occurs — the fix only removes the misleading dead branch.

title: RecordRoleChange::normaliseIds returns non-int/non-string scalar or object unvalidated into audit fields
suggestedFix: When $rolesOrIds is not a Model, not a Collection, and not an array (e.g. a boolean, a float, or an arbitrary object from a future Spatie version), the value is wrapped in an array and returned directly without type validation. This value lands in the ActionEvent fields JSON column. Downstream code or audit-log UIs that assume list<int|string> for ids will behave unexpectedly. The array branch (lines 188-203) already validates each entry — apply the same validation at the scalar path: if (is_int($rolesOrIds) || is_string($rolesOrIds)) { return [$rolesOrIds]; } return [];

verdict.reasoning: The defect is confirmed by reading the code. In normaliseIds() (lines 184-186), after excluding Model and Collection instances, any remaining non-array value is wrapped directly into an array with no type validation: return [$rolesOrIds]. By contrast, the array branch (lines 188-203) explicitly filters each entry to only int, string, or Model — anything else is silently dropped. A boolean false, a float, a null, or an arbitrary non-Collection object that is neither a Model nor a Collection would be wrapped and returned as-is, landing in the ActionEvent fields/changes JSON columns (lines 69 and 73) as [false], [1.5], [null], etc., violating the declared list<int|string> return type. The downstream empty-array guard at lines 53-55 does not help because [false] is not empty. The severity stays low: Spatie Permission does not emit boolean or float payloads in any published version, so the path is reachable only via a future Spatie API change or a consumer calling record() directly with a non-standard value. A non-JSON-serializable object would throw at ActionEvent::create rather than silently corrupt data. The fix proposed by the reporter is correct: replace line 185 with an int/string type-gate and fall through to return [] for anything else, mirroring the array-branch logic.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is accurate. Replace lines 184-186 with: if (is_int($rolesOrIds) || is_string($rolesOrIds)) { return [$rolesOrIds]; } return []; This mirrors the guard already applied to each entry in the array branch and respects the declared list<int|string> return type. No broader change is needed.

title: No OAuth state parameter validation — CSRF on the SSO callback endpoint
suggestedFix: The callback route is a plain GET with no CSRF token and no state parameter check of its own. Socialite stateful drivers do validate the `state` query parameter internally (comparing it against the session value stored during `redirect()`), which prevents CSRF logins when `stateless = false` (the default). However, the code does not explicitly verify that `stateless` mode is off, and when `stateless = true` is configured for the Azure provider (line 51-53 of AzureProvider), the OAuth `state` parameter is not validated at all, leaving the callback fully open to CSRF-based login injection. Add an explicit guard: if the provider is configured as stateless, require an alternative proof (e.g. reject stateless mode, or validate a signed session token before calling `resolveIdentity`).

verdict.reasoning: The finding is real but its severity is overstated. AzureProvider.php lines 51-53 confirm that when `stateless = true` is set in config, the Socialite driver is switched to stateless mode before `$client->user()` is called. Socialite's stateless mode bypasses the OAuth `state` parameter check entirely, leaving the callback open to CSRF-based login injection (forging a callback with a valid code from a different session). The SsoController itself does no independent state validation; it relies wholly on Socialite's internal check, which is absent in stateless mode. However, the default for this option is `false` (AzureProvider.php line 51: `$this->config('stateless', false)`), and the published example config block in config/martis.php (lines 800-844) does not include a `stateless` key at all, so adopters following the docs would never enable it by accident. It is a deliberate developer opt-in. A developer who knowingly enables stateless mode presumably understands that state validation is not performed (this is documented Socialite behavior), though there is no warning or guard in the Martis code itself. The real gap is the absence of any runtime guard or documentation warning that stateless mode removes CSRF protection. Severity is medium, not critical: it requires an explicit non-default misconfiguration to be exploitable, and the attack surface is limited to the callback endpoint during an active OAuth flow.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is partially correct. Rejecting stateless mode outright would be over-restrictive. The appropriate fix is narrower: add a comment or runtime warning (e.g. a logged notice or a config validation assertion) when `stateless = true` is detected, informing the operator that no OAuth state check will be performed. Alternatively, document this clearly in docs/sso.md alongside the stateless option. The controller does not need to change; the gap is in developer guidance, not in the default code path.

title: on_no_role_match hook always fires even when strategy = 'deny', allowing callback to bypass deny
suggestedFix: The `fireNoRoleMatch` hook is fired unconditionally regardless of `strategy`. The docblock on `SsoManager::onNoRoleMatchUsing` says the callback is active only when `on_no_role_match` is `'callable'`, but the controller does not enforce that. A hook that returns a non-RedirectResponse value (e.g. null) is silently ignored, and control falls through to the `strategy` check — but a hook that returns a RedirectResponse will short-circuit and log the user in even when strategy is `deny`. Intended semantics should be: call the hook only when `$strategy === 'callable'`; otherwise apply the named strategy directly. Fix: gate the `fireNoRoleMatch` call on `$strategy === 'callable'`.

title: Graph API OData filter $filter=resourceId eq {guid} is unquoted — invalid OData and potential injection
suggestedFix: OData GUID literals must be written as `guid'<value>'` (or just bare in beta) and the value must be URL-encoded. The current code interpolates `$resourceId` directly from config without URL-encoding or OData-escaping. If an operator misconfigures the env value with spaces or OData-significant characters (single quotes, ampersands), the filter will either fail silently (returning no roles, effectively locking out the user) or become an OData injection vector. Fix: validate that `$resourceId` is a well-formed UUID/GUID before use, and URL-encode the entire query string segment: `urlencode('resourceId eq '.$resourceId)`.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at lines 118-122 confirms that `$resourceId` is interpolated directly into the OData `$filter` query string without `urlencode()`. However, the security framing in the report is substantially overstated for two reasons.

First, `$resourceId` is sourced exclusively from `$this->config('resource_id', '')`, which maps to `config('martis.auth.sso.providers.azure.resource_id')`, populated by `env('AZURE_RESOURCE_ID')`. This is operator/developer-controlled configuration, not user-supplied input. There is no code path by which an end user can influence this value during a live request. The "injection vector" framing requires an operator who has write access to the `.env` file or deployment config, which means they already have full system access.

Second, the reporter's suggested fix references `guid'<value>'` OData syntax. That is the OData v3 / Azure AD Graph (v1) syntax. The Microsoft Graph v1.0 API (`graph.microsoft.com/v1.0`) uses OData v4, which represents GUID literals as bare strings without the `guid'...'` wrapper. The current interpolation format (`resourceId eq <uuid>`) is actually the correct OData v4 syntax when the value is a well-formed GUID with no whitespace.

The genuine defect is narrower: `$resourceId` is not passed through `urlencode()` in the query-string segment. A malformed env value containing spaces, ampersands, or single quotes would produce a broken URL (spaces break HTTP, ampersands would silently truncate the filter, single quotes could produce malformed OData). This is a robustness/reliability bug — a misconfigured env value causes the Graph call to fail silently (returning `[]`, which effectively denies the login), not a privilege-escalation or data-exfiltration issue. The only validation present is a non-empty check (lines 111-116).

The finding is real in the narrow sense that `urlencode()` is missing on the `$resourceId` interpolation, but the claimed severity of "high / security injection" is not justified given the config-only data source. Adjusted severity is low: the practical impact of a misconfigured GUID is login denial (the Graph call returns a non-2xx or an empty `value` array), not injection or unauthorized access.
verdict.fixNote: The reporter's `guid'<value>'` OData syntax recommendation is incorrect for Graph API v1.0, which uses OData v4 bare GUID literals. The correct fix is simply to apply `urlencode()` to `$resourceId` in the query-string position (matching what is already done for `$externalId` in the path segment), and optionally add a UUID format guard with a regex or `Str::isUuid()` to catch misconfigured env values early with a clear RuntimeException rather than a silent empty-roles return. No OData quoting change is needed.

title: Dead code branch in accessToken resolution always produces null for method_exists check
evidence: $accessToken = method_exists($user, 'token') ? null : null;
suggestedFix: Both branches of the ternary return `null`, so `$accessToken` is always initialized to `null` regardless of whether `token` is a method on the Socialite user object. The real token is then picked up only if `property_exists($user, 'token')` is true (line 63-65). The `method_exists` ternary is dead code that adds confusion but, more critically, if the Socialite driver exposes the token as a method (e.g. `getToken()`) and not a public property, the access token will remain `null`, causing `fetchExternalRoles` to bail out early (line 88: `if ($accessToken === '') { return []; }`) — but a null coerces to `''` via the `?? ''` on line 68, so roles are silently skipped. Fix: use `$user->token ?? (method_exists($user, 'getToken') ? $user->getToken() : null)` and remove the dead ternary.

verdict.fixNote:
The simplest correct fix is to collapse the dead ternary and the property_exists branch into a single expression that also handles method-based access. For the `socialiteproviders/microsoft` driver specifically, `token` is a public property on the AbstractUser base class, so the property_exists path happens to work in practice for that driver. However the code as written is fragile and will silently break for any driver that exposes the token differently. Recommended replacement for lines 62-66:

    $accessToken = $user->token                                    // public property (most drivers)
        ?? (method_exists($user, 'getToken') ? $user->getToken() : null)  // method fallback
        ?? (method_exists($user, 'token')    ? $user->token()    : null); // callable fallback

Remove the dead `method_exists($user, 'token') ? null : null` ternary entirely. The `$accessToken ?? ''` on line 68 and the empty-string guard on line 88 can stay as-is.

title: NativeAdapter role sync: pluck('id') assumes 'id' primary key — breaks UUID / custom PK models
evidence: $resolvedIds = $resolvedRoles->pluck('id')->filter()->all();
suggestedFix: The pluck hardcodes the `id` column name. If the host app's Role model uses a UUID primary key named `uuid` or a custom key from `getKeyName()`, `pluck('id')` returns a collection of nulls and the `->filter()` produces an empty array. All existing roles are deleted from the pivot table and no new ones are inserted, silently stripping the user of every role on every SSO login. Fix: use `$resolvedRoles->map(fn ($role) => $role->getKey())->filter()->all()` so the correct primary key is used regardless of the key column name.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is confirmed by reading the code directly. In `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Sso/PermissionAdapters/NativeAdapter.php`:

- Line 31: `$modelId = $user->getKey()` — correctly uses Eloquent's portable PK accessor for the user model.
- Line 33: `$resolvedIds = $resolvedRoles->pluck('id')->filter()->all()` — hardcodes the string `'id'` for the role models in the same method, creating an inconsistency.
