The `$resolvedRoles` collection is always composed of Eloquent model instances (built via `->get()` in `RoleMapper`, lines 89, 130, 154). Eloquent's `Collection::pluck('id')` reads the raw `id` attribute from each model's attribute bag. If the host app's Role model declares a primary key with a name other than `id` (e.g. `uuid`, `role_id`, or any custom key via `protected $primaryKey = '...'`), the attribute `id` will be null on every instance, `->filter()` removes all nulls, and `$resolvedIds` becomes an empty array.

The consequence is silent data corruption: the `DB::transaction` at line 35 first deletes all existing role rows for the user (line 36–39), then the `if ($resolvedIds === [])` guard at line 41 causes an early return without re-inserting anything. Every SSO login strips the user of all roles without any error or log message.

No guard exists upstream to restrict `role_model` to models with an `id` PK. The `role_model` config key accepts any class string. The callable and resolver paths also pass arbitrary Eloquent models into this adapter.

The correct fix is `$resolvedRoles->map(fn ($role) => $role->getKey())->filter()->all()`, which is exactly what line 31 does for the user — the inconsistency between the two lines in the same method confirms this is an oversight rather than an intentional design choice.

Severity assessment: medium. The Spatie default Role model uses an integer `id` PK, so the most common deployment is unaffected. However, UUID-keyed Role models (increasingly common) silently lose all roles on every SSO login — a high-impact data integrity failure for the apps that hit it.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is `$resolvedRoles->map(fn ($role) => $role->getKey())->filter()->all()` on line 33, consistent with how line 31 already handles the user model. No other changes needed in this file.

title: Impersonation stop() endpoint missing authorization check — impersonated target can escalate to operator identity
suggestedFix: During an active impersonation session, the currently authenticated user (from the auth guard's perspective) is the TARGET, not the operator. The stop() endpoint has no check confirming the caller is the ORIGINAL operator. Because the endpoint only requires martis.auth (any authenticated user), the impersonated target can POST /stop. ImpersonationManager::stop() then reads stashed['original'] from the session and calls auth->guard($guard)->login($original) — which logs the OPERATOR into the TARGET's browser session. The target ends up authenticated as the operator (who may be a super-admin). Fix: before calling $this->impersonation->stop(), verify the current session belongs to an ongoing impersonation AND that the caller is allowed to stop it. The simplest safe approach is to check Gate::allows('martis-impersonate') on stop() as well, since only the operator who started impersonation can hold that gate. Alternatively, stash the operator's session id alongside 'original' and reject stop() calls that arrive on a different session.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading ImpersonationController.php:98-107, ImpersonationManager.php:99-132, and routes/martis.php:232-237.

The `stop()` route sits inside the `martis.auth` + `martis.impersonation.duration` middleware group with no per-route gate check (routes/martis.php:236-237). The `stop()` controller method (ImpersonationController.php:98-107) checks only whether the feature is enabled and then calls `$this->impersonation->stop()` — no call to `Gate::allows('martis-impersonate')`, unlike `start()` (ImpersonationController.php:73-75) which does check the gate.

During an active impersonation session `ImpersonationManager::start()` calls `$this->auth->guard($guard)->login($target)` (ImpersonationManager.php:88), so from the auth guard's perspective the currently authenticated user IS the target. The target passes the `martis.auth` middleware, so they can POST `/api/impersonation/stop`.

`ImpersonationManager::stop()` (ImpersonationManager.php:99-132) reads `stashed['original']` from the session bag, resolves it to the operator's full user object via the provider, and calls `$this->auth->guard($guard)->login($original)` (line 127). This logs the operator's identity into the target's browser session, completing the privilege escalation.

The controller's docblock (lines 14-30) explicitly states that the `martis-impersonate` Gate is a required second layer for all endpoints, but the implementation only enforces it in `start()`. The symmetry is broken.

Severity remains critical: the impersonated target (a regular user who did nothing to earn operator privileges) can unilaterally take over a super-admin session by issuing a single authenticated POST to a known URL.
verdict.fixNote: The simplest correct fix is to add `Gate::allows('martis-impersonate')` to `stop()` exactly as done in `start()`. However, during impersonation the current guard user is the TARGET, who will not hold that gate — so the gate check will deny the stop correctly for the target while allowing the operator (who is not impersonating anyone and retains their original gate) to stop from their own session. Alternatively: stash the operator's session ID alongside `original` at start time and reject any `stop()` call arriving on a session ID that does not match. Either approach closes the escalation. The gate check is simpler and consistent with the stated two-layer design in the class docblock.

title: RequestScopedAbilityCache::lookup() is never called — cache is populated but never read, providing zero effect
suggestedFix: The handle() listener (line 69) faithfully populates the in-memory $cache array on every GateEvaluated event, but lookup() is never invoked from any production code path (confirmed by grepping the entire src/ tree — the method only appears in its own file). To short-circuit Gate evaluation the cache would need to be wired as a Gate::before() callback (or the Gate's 'before' stack via $gate->before(fn($user, $ability, $args) => app(RequestScopedAbilityCache::class)->lookup(...))) in MartisServiceProvider. Without that hook the feature description in the class docblock — 'a subsequent lookup returns the cached value without re-running the policy method' — is false: every check still runs the full policy chain regardless. Either wire the Gate::before() callback or remove lookup() and document the cache as observe-only.

verdict.reasoning:
lookup() has zero callers in the entire src/ tree (confirmed by grepping all PHP files for "lookup()"). The method is defined at RequestScopedAbilityCache.php:55 and is referenced only inside its own file. No Gate::before() callback is registered anywhere in the package (grep for "Gate::before" and "gate->before" returned nothing). MartisServiceProvider.php:633-637 wires only the GateEvaluated event listener (handle()), not any before-hook.

However, the finding overstates severity and misdiagnoses the design intent. The class docblock at lines 40-42 explicitly documents the observe-only contract: "The cache only OBSERVES GateEvaluated. It does not short-circuit the gate — every check still hits the policy at least once per request. Subsequent checks read from Map." The reporter's suggested fix (Gate::before()) would contradict this documented boundary.

The real defect is narrower: lookup() is dead public API that no production caller ever reads, making the "Subsequent checks read from Map" promise in the docblock false in practice. The Resource layer (the intended consumer per the docblock's "Wins" section) never calls lookup() before making a redundant can() check. The feature degrades silently to the pre-v1.8.8 full-policy-chain behaviour for every request. No incorrect results, no 500s, no security breach — pure performance promise not delivered.

Severity is low, not high: the feature is off by default (MARTIS_AUTHZ_REQUEST_CACHE=true required), the degradation is the baseline behaviour that existed before v1.8.8, and no data correctness or security boundary is at risk.
verdict.fixNote: Do not wire Gate::before() — that contradicts the documented observe-only design. The correct fix is to have the Resource layer (sidebar menu, schema authorization block, per-record authorization block, action visibility) call app(RequestScopedAbilityCache::class)->lookup($userId, $ability, $model) before a redundant $user->can() call and short-circuit when the cached value is non-null. Alternatively, document lookup() as a public API for host-app use and add a note that the package itself does not yet consume it internally.

title: RecordImpersonation: dead ternary — both branches of instanceof-Model guard produce identical output
evidence: $targetType = $target instanceof Model ? $target::class : $target::class;
suggestedFix: Both branches of the ternary expression return the identical value `$target::class`. The condition `$target instanceof Model` is evaluated but has no effect on the result. The original intent was likely to handle non-Eloquent Authenticatable implementations differently (e.g., storing a generic sentinel string or null for polymorphic morph-map safety). Simplify to `$targetType = $target::class;` and, if non-Model Authenticatable targets need special handling, add an explicit branch.

verdict.reasoning: Line 55 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Auth/Listeners/RecordImpersonation.php reads exactly: `$targetType = $target instanceof Model ? $target::class : $target::class;`. Both branches of the ternary expression return the identical value `$target::class`. The condition is evaluated but has zero effect on the result. This is confirmed dead code. The bug is real. Severity is low: the audit log still records the correct fully-qualified class name for all callers, and there is no runtime error. The only real gap is that a non-Eloquent Authenticatable implementation would be stored with its class name rather than any special sentinel, which may matter for polymorphic morph resolution but is not exercised by any code visible in this file.
verdict.fixNote: The simplest correct fix is `$targetType = $target::class;`. If non-Model Authenticatables need distinct handling (e.g. a sentinel string for morph safety), add an explicit else branch at that point rather than relying on the dead condition.

title: PlanRanker class docblock misrepresents fail-open vs fail-closed behavior for unknown plan tiers
evidence:
* unknown plans rank
 * `-1`, comparisons fail open (no lock), and the trait stays inert.
suggestedFix: The class-level docblock conflates two distinct cases: (1) the REQUIRED plan tier is not in the rank table (lines 47-54: returns false = fail OPEN, user is NOT locked), and (2) the CURRENT user's resolved plan is not in the rank table (line 65: rank defaults to -1, which is less than every declared required rank, so the user IS locked = fail CLOSED). The docblock phrase 'unknown plans rank -1, comparisons fail open' is only accurate for case 1; case 2 is the opposite. Rewrite to: 'When the required tier is absent from the rank table the gate fails open (no lock) so a typo does not accidentally hide features. When the user holds a plan that is not in the rank table they get implicit rank -1 and are locked below every declared tier.'

verdict.reasoning:
The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Gates/PlanRanker.php confirms three distinct paths in isLockedFor():

1. Lines 47-53: required tier absent from rank table → returns false (fail-open). Docblock covers this correctly.

2. Lines 57-62: resolver not configured or returns null → $current is null → returns true directly (fail-closed). The docblock is silent about this path entirely.

3. Line 65-67: resolver returns a plan string that is not in the rank table → $currentRank defaults to -1. Since every declared tier has rank >= 0, -1 < requiredRank evaluates to true → returns true (fail-closed). This is the direct contradiction the reporter identified.

The docblock phrase at lines 34-36 reads: "unknown plans rank -1, comparisons fail open (no lock), and the trait stays inert." This statement is only true in the degenerate case where the rank table is entirely empty (both required and current are absent) — in that case the required-tier null-check at line 48 fires first and returns false before ever reaching line 65. However, once the consumer configures any rank table entries (the normal production scenario), a user whose resolved plan string is absent from the rank table gets rank -1 at line 65, and -1 is less than every declared rank (minimum 0), so they are locked. The docblock assertion "fail open" is the opposite of what the code does in this scenario.

The suggested fix accurately identifies both halves of the behavior: (1) unknown required tier = fail-open at line 48-53; (2) unknown user plan = fail-closed at line 65-67. This is a genuine documentation accuracy defect that could mislead a consumer developer into thinking an unrecognized plan string on a user would simply let them through.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested rewrite is accurate and sufficient. Additionally, the docblock should note case 2 (lines 57-62): when the plan resolver is not configured or returns null, the user is also locked (treated as rank -1 implicitly via the early true return). The revised docblock should cover all three paths: unknown required tier (fail-open), unconfigured resolver (fail-closed), and unknown user plan (fail-closed).

title: LensController cache key missing user identity — cross-user data leak
evidence:
private function buildCacheKey(
    Lens $lensInstance,
    LensRequest $lensRequest,
    int $perPage,
    int $page,
    string $trashed = '',
    string $tableVersion = '',
): string {
    $parts = [
        'martis-lens',
        static::classHash($lensInstance),
        $lensInstance->uriKey(),
        $tableVersion,
        $lensRequest->search,
        $lensRequest->sortColumn ?? '',
        $lensRequest->sortDirection->value,
        md5(json_encode($lensRequest->selectedFilters) ?: ''),
        $trashed,
        $perPage,
        $page,
        app()->getLocale(),
    ];

    return implode(':', array_map('strval', $parts));
}
suggestedFix: Add the authenticated user's identity to the $parts array: `(string) ($lensRequest->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest')`. The Lens::query() receives the full LensRequest (which wraps the HTTP request), so user-scoped lens implementations (e.g. 'my orders') will have their results cached globally and served to every user who hits the same filter/sort/page combination. The fix is a one-liner addition before `app()->getLocale()` in $parts.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both files directly.

In /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Lenses/Lens.php:69, `cacheTtlSeconds` defaults to `0`. The controller at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Controllers/LensController.php:145 only enters the cache path when `$ttl > 0`: `if ($ttl > 0) { $cached = Cache::remember($cacheKey, $ttl, $builder); }`. Caching is therefore opt-in via `->cacheFor(N)`.

When a developer does opt in, `buildCacheKey()` (lines 260-279) assembles: prefix, class hash, uriKey, tableVersion, search, sortColumn, sortDirection, filter hash, trashed mode, perPage, page, and locale. User identity is absent. Two distinct authenticated users who hit the same lens with identical parameters will share the same cache key and receive each other's results.

The finding is real: the missing user-identity dimension in the cache key is a genuine latent cross-user data-leak bug. However, the severity is lower than "critical" because:

1. Caching is disabled by default (`cacheTtlSeconds = 0`). The vulnerability is only reachable when a developer explicitly calls `->cacheFor(N)` on a lens instance.
2. A lens whose `query()` is fully public (no per-user WHERE clause) is not affected even when caching is enabled, because all users legitimately see the same data.
3. The bug manifests only when the combination of (a) `cacheFor` enabled AND (b) a user-scoped query is present. That is a realistic scenario (e.g., "my orders" lenses), but it requires developer action, not a default configuration.

The suggested one-liner fix is correct: add `(string) ($lensRequest->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest')` to `$parts` in `buildCacheKey()`. LensRequest wraps the HTTP request, so `->user()` is available without additional injection.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as stated. One additional hardening: consider whether "guest" (unauthenticated) lenses should be cacheable at all — if the controller already enforces authentication before this code path, the `?? 'guest'` fallback is dead code and could be omitted to avoid confusion. Verify the auth middleware on the martis API routes to confirm whether unauthenticated requests can ever reach LensController::index.

title: Default manage-martis-cache gate allows any authenticated user to wipe/disable caches
evidence: Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($user) => $user !== null);
suggestedFix: The default should be restrictive, not permissive. Replace with a deny-by-default that requires the host to explicitly grant: `Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($user) => false);`. The CacheController docblock already warns hosts to tighten this, but shipping a permissive default means every Martis install that does not override it exposes cache clear/disable/enable to any logged-in user. An unprivileged user can repeatedly call POST /api/cache/clear to disable caching entirely or force expensive invalidation cycles.

verdict.reasoning: The gate definition at MartisServiceProvider.php:325 is confirmed: `Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($user) => $user !== null);`. The cache routes (GET /api/cache, POST /api/cache/clear, POST /api/cache/disable, POST /api/cache/enable, POST /api/cache/reset-override) are registered inside the authenticated+2FA-complete middleware group at routes/martis.php:252-263, so any logged-in, 2FA-complete user satisfies `$user !== null` and passes the gate. CacheController::authorized() (line 112-120) is the only authorization check; it delegates entirely to this gate with no additional role or admin guard. The docblock at MartisServiceProvider.php:308-318 explicitly acknowledges that the default is permissive and instructs hosts to override it, but shipping a permissive default means every install that omits an explicit override exposes all cache mutation endpoints to unprivileged authenticated users. There is no secondary guard elsewhere that mitigates this. Severity remains high: the attack requires an authenticated session with completed 2FA, which restricts exposure to insider threats and reduces it from critical, but the blast radius is all default Martis deployments and the operations (clear, disable, enable) are destructive availability-class actions.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (deny-by-default: `fn ($user) => false`) is correct and sufficient. An alternative that preserves usefulness out of the box would be to require a host-defined admin check, e.g. checking for a conventional `is_admin` boolean or a Martis-managed role if the package ships one. Either way, the default must not grant access to all authenticated users. The CacheController docblock and the stub in the published MartisServiceProvider should also be updated to show the override pattern prominently.

title: Per-request nocache bypass (?nocache=1) is unrestricted — DoS vector for any authenticated user
suggestedFix: Gate-check the bypass before honoring it. Add a permission check, e.g. `Gate::forUser($request->user())->allows('bypass-martis-cache')` (defaulting to false), so only privileged users (developers/admins) can send ?nocache=1. Any authenticated user can currently append ?nocache=1 to any Martis API call to force every expensive metric/navigation/schema computation on every request, bypassing all caching.

verdict.reasoning:
The code was read in full and the claim is confirmed by four converging observations.

1. `bypassed()` at MartisCache.php:298-313 accepts `?nocache=1`, `?nocache=true`, or the `X-Martis-No-Cache: 1` header and returns `true` with no authorization check of any kind inside the method.

2. The sole call site is `remember()` at line 189: `if (! $this->enabled($type) || $this->bypassed())`. The parameter-less call resolves the active HTTP request via `currentRequest()`, so it operates on the real incoming request without any caller filtering.

3. Every route that exercises `remember()` — MetricController (dashboards, resource metric cards), NavigationController (navigation, badges), and ResourceController (schema) — sits inside the `martis.auth` + `martis.2fa` middleware group in routes/martis.php:181-466. `MartisAuthenticate` enforces authentication but only checks `$auth->check()`; there is no role, ability, or admin gate.

4. The `manage-martis-cache` Gate that gates CacheController's persistent admin operations is registered at MartisServiceProvider.php:325 as `fn ($user) => $user !== null` by default and is never applied to the `bypassed()` path. No other gate guards the per-request bypass.

Consequently, any user who can authenticate (the full user base in a typical Martis deployment) can append `?nocache=1` to any Martis API request and cause every expensive computation — metric aggregation, navigation resolution, schema serialization — to bypass the cache and run against the database on every request. This is a genuine availability/resource-exhaustion vector.

Severity assessment: the reporter's "medium" is correct. The attack requires a valid authenticated session (no anonymous access), produces no persistent effect, causes no data leakage, and does not affect data integrity. It is a resource-abuse / availability risk. "Low" would undersell it because the bypass fires on every call across the most expensive subsystems; "high" would oversell it because it requires authentication and cannot take the application down by itself — it degrades it under sustained targeted use. Medium is the right call.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (adding a Gate check inside `bypassed()`) is the right approach, but the default gate (`fn ($user) => $user !== null`) would not actually restrict access unless the host application overrides it. The fix should either: (a) introduce a dedicated `bypass-martis-cache` gate that defaults to `false` (deny-by-default), or (b) add a config flag `martis.cache.allow_nocache_bypass` that defaults to `false` and is only enabled in developer/staging environments. Option (a) aligns better with the existing gate pattern. The `manage-martis-cache` gate should NOT be reused here because its own default allows any authenticated user.

title: clear() version increment is a non-atomic read-modify-write — lost bump under concurrent invalidation
suggestedFix: Replace the PHP-side increment with a DB-side atomic update: `CacheState::query()->updateOrInsert(['type' => $t], [...]) ` using a raw expression for version: `DB::raw('version + 1')`. Or wrap the read+write in a `DB::transaction()` with a `lockForUpdate()` on the CacheState row to prevent the race. Two concurrent Artisan `cache:clear` calls (or two queue jobs) both reading version=N will both write N+1, net result is one effective bump rather than two — some stale entries resurface under the repeated new version.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Cache/MartisCache.php lines 215-232 confirms the race. `state($t)` (line 223) reads from the PHP in-memory array `$this->states`, which is hydrated from a single `SELECT` in `loadStates()`. The increment `$current['version'] + 1` (line 225) happens in PHP, and `writeState()` at line 473 writes it back with `CacheState::query()->updateOrInsert(...)` — a plain upsert with no atomic DB-side increment and no row lock. Two concurrent processes that both call `clear()` before either completes their `writeState()` will each read the same version N and both write N+1, producing one effective bump instead of two. No guard, transaction, or `lockForUpdate()` prevents this. The fix suggested (DB::raw('version + 1') or lockForUpdate inside a transaction) is correct. However, the severity is lower than claimed: `clear()` is an administrative action (Artisan, admin panel), concurrent invocation is uncommon, and the consequence of the race is only that cache entries written under version N+1 in the narrow window between the two concurrent clears survive the second clear — they expire by TTL or are orphaned on the next single-threaded clear. No data corruption, no security impact, no user error. Medium overstates the practical risk; low is appropriate.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. The lightest-weight option is wrapping the read+write pair in a DB::transaction() with a lockForUpdate() on the CacheState row for the given type. Alternatively, updateOrInsert can be replaced with an UPDATE using DB::raw('version + 1') so the increment is fully DB-side. Note that updateOrInsert does not support raw expressions for individual columns directly — a raw increment requires using DB::table('martis_cache_state')->where('type', $t)->increment('version') combined with a separate update for cleared_at and override, all inside a transaction.

title: writeState() always overwrites created_at on update, destroying audit trail
suggestedFix: `updateOrInsert()` sends the full values array on both INSERT and UPDATE. The `created_at` should only be set on INSERT. Use `upsert()` with separate `$uniqueBy` and `$update` arrays, or issue a raw `INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` without `created_at` in the update clause. The current code resets `created_at` to now() on every version bump, clearing the record of when the cache layer was first observed.

verdict.reasoning: The code at MartisCache.php:473-482 calls `CacheState::query()->updateOrInsert(['type' => $type], [..., 'created_at' => Date::now()])`. Reading the actual Laravel Query Builder implementation at vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Query/Builder.php:4342-4358 confirms that `updateOrInsert` takes a separate code path for INSERT vs UPDATE: on INSERT it calls `$this->insert(array_merge($attributes, $values))`, but on UPDATE it calls `$this->limit(1)->update($values)` — passing the entire `$values` array unchanged, including `created_at`. There is no conditional stripping of `created_at` from the update payload. The CacheState model (src/Models/CacheState.php) has no custom mutator for `created_at`, no `CREATED_AT = false` constant, and the `$fillable` guard (`['type', 'version', 'cleared_at', 'override']`) is irrelevant because the call goes through `CacheState::query()` (Query Builder level), which bypasses Eloquent mass-assignment entirely and issues raw SQL. Therefore, on every version bump of an existing cache-state row, the `created_at` column is overwritten with `Date::now()`, destroying the original insert timestamp. The claimed severity of "low" is correct: there is no runtime correctness or security impact; only the audit trail of when a cache layer was first provisioned is lost.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is sound. Replace `updateOrInsert()` with either (a) Eloquent's `upsert()` passing `created_at` only in the insert-column list and omitting it from the update-column list, or (b) a manual SELECT-then-INSERT/UPDATE pattern. Note that Laravel's `upsert()` on MySQL issues `INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` and on SQLite/Postgres issues `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`, so the `created_at` exclusion from the update clause is portable. A simpler alternative that avoids the Query Builder entirely: `CacheState::firstOrCreate(['type' => $type])->update([...])` — `firstOrCreate` sets `created_at` only on INSERT, and the subsequent `update()` call never touches it.

title: normalizedConfig() returns extension TTL raw without normalizeTtl() — zero/negative TTL bypasses 'no expiry' contract
suggestedFix: Pass the extension TTL through normalizeTtl() before returning: `return ['enabled' => $extension['enabled'], 'ttl' => $this->normalizeTtl($extension['ttl'])];`. The `extend()` method accepts `?int $ttl`, so callers can pass 0 or a negative integer. The array shape path (line 342-346) and the legacy bare-int path (line 362-365) both normalize TTL, but this extension fast-path skips normalization. A caller passing `MartisCache::extend('orders', ttl: 0)` gets `addMinutes(0)` which Laravel treats as immediate expiration rather than forever.

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is confirmed by reading the code directly. Three paths exist in normalizedConfig():

1. Array shape (lines 342-346): ttl passes through normalizeTtl() — zero/negative becomes null (no expiry).
2. Bare-int/legacy path (lines 362-365): ttl passes through normalizeTtl() — zero/negative becomes null.
3. Extension fast-path (lines 354-355): returns $extension directly, which was stored verbatim by extend() at line 110. normalizeTtl() is never called.

normalizeTtl() at line 380 returns null for any value <= 0: `return $minutes > 0 ? $minutes : null;`. When the extension fast-path bypasses this, a caller who passed ttl: 0 gets the raw 0 back. remember() at line 194 then executes `Date::now()->addMinutes(0)`, producing an expiry of right now, which causes every cache write to expire immediately instead of being stored forever (the correct semantic for a zero/non-positive input).

The suggested fix is correct: return a normalized copy rather than the raw $extension array. Concretely: `return ['enabled' => $extension['enabled'], 'ttl' => $this->normalizeTtl($extension['ttl'])];`

Severity is low because: (a) ttl: 0 is not documented as a valid/meaningful input to extend() — the docblock says null means "no expiration" and callers who want that pass null; (b) a caller passing a positive integer or null is entirely unaffected; (c) the worst outcome is a cache miss on every request for that extension type, not data corruption or a security issue. The "low" assessment from the reporter is appropriate.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. The one-liner replacement for line 355 is: `return ['enabled' => $extension['enabled'], 'ttl' => $this->normalizeTtl($extension['ttl'])];`. No other callers need changing — the normalization contract is already satisfied on all other return paths within normalizedConfig().

title: TrendMetric uses MySQL-only DATE_FORMAT — breaks on Postgres and SQLite
evidence: ->select(DB::raw("DATE_FORMAT({$dateColumn}, '{$dateFormat}') as date_key"), $expression)
suggestedFix: Branch on the DB driver and use the driver-appropriate expression: MySQL → DATE_FORMAT, Postgres → TO_CHAR with its own format tokens (%Y→YYYY, %m→MM, etc.), SQLite → strftime. A clean approach is a private helper `dateFormatExpression(string $column, TrendPeriod $unit): string` that switches on `DB::getDriverName()`. Day: MySQL `'%Y-%m-%d'`, Postgres `'YYYY-MM-DD'`, SQLite `'%Y-%m-%d'`. Month: MySQL `'%Y-%m'`, Postgres `'YYYY-MM'`, SQLite `'%Y-%m'`. Week requires more care on Postgres (date_trunc + to_char); the simplest portable alternative is to bucket by the ISO week start date.

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code exists exactly as described. At /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Metrics/TrendMetric.php line 153, the only SELECT on date bucketing is:

  ->select(DB::raw("DATE_FORMAT({$dateColumn}, '{$dateFormat}') as date_key"), $expression)

The $dateFormat values are pure MySQL format strings set at lines 140-144:
  TrendPeriod::Day   => '%Y-%m-%d'
  TrendPeriod::Week  => '%x-%v'
  TrendPeriod::Month => '%Y-%m'

No driver-detection guard exists anywhere in TrendMetric.php or any shared Metrics-namespace helper. The codebase does have a getDriverName() pattern inside SearchResolver.php (lines 111, 246-247), proving the team is aware of the technique — but it was never applied here. DATE_FORMAT with these format tokens is MySQL-only; PostgreSQL requires TO_CHAR with different tokens, and SQLite requires strftime. Any app running on Postgres or SQLite will receive a database error the moment a Trend metric is rendered.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct. A private helper dateFormatExpression(string $column, TrendPeriod $unit): string that branches on DB::getDriverName() and emits the driver-appropriate SQL expression is the right approach. The week case deserves extra care on PostgreSQL: %x-%v has no direct TO_CHAR equivalent; the cleanest portable alternative is to cast to the ISO week-start date using date_trunc('week', col)::date and format that as YYYY-MM-DD, then align the PHP CarbonPeriod key format accordingly.

title: TrendMetric casts named range strings ('TODAY', 'MTD', 'QTD', 'YTD') to int 0 — silently returns empty chart
evidence: $range = (int) $request->query('range', '30');
suggestedFix: The base Metric::ranges() includes 'TODAY', 'MTD', 'QTD', 'YTD' as valid range keys, and TrendMetric does not override ranges(). When a user selects any named range, (int)'TODAY' evaluates to 0, making $startDate == $now (subDays(0)), and the query window collapses to a single instant returning no rows and an empty or single-point series. Fix: either (a) override ranges() in TrendMetric to return only numeric ranges (matching the intent of a time-series), or (b) resolve the named range via MetricRange::tryFrom() before the cast, mapping TODAY→1, MTD→current month days elapsed, QTD→days in quarter, YTD→day of year, same pattern ValueMetric uses in calculateDateRange().

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the source. Metric::ranges() (Metric.php:191-215) returns 'TODAY', 'MTD', 'QTD', 'YTD' as valid keys alongside numeric ones, and TrendMetric does not override ranges() — those four options are therefore presented to the user in every trend card. TrendMetric::aggregateByPeriod() reads the range at line 131 as `$range = (int) $request->query('range', '30');`. When the user selects a named range the request carries e.g. `?range=TODAY`; PHP evaluates `(int)'TODAY'` to 0. The match at lines 134-138 then calls `$now->subDays(0)->startOfDay()`, collapsing the window to the current calendar day (midnight to now) for TODAY, and to the same minimal window for MTD/QTD/YTD. ValueMetric (ValueMetric.php:96-97, 126-154) reads the same query param as a raw string and passes it through MetricRange::tryFrom() before branching — a guard that is entirely absent from TrendMetric. MetricRange enum (MetricRange.php) already encodes the four cases. No other guard, wrapper, or controller-layer normalization intercepts the value before aggregateByPeriod() is called.
verdict.fixNote: The simplest fix mirrors the ValueMetric pattern exactly: in aggregateByPeriod(), read the raw string first (`$rangeRaw = $request->query('range', '30');`), resolve via `MetricRange::tryFrom($rangeRaw)`, then derive a `$range` integer only for the null (numeric) branch. For the named presets, map to day counts before feeding the existing subDays/subWeeks/subMonths match — or add named-range branches to that match directly (e.g. MetricRange::Today maps to subDays(1) for a day-granularity chart). An alternative is to override ranges() in TrendMetric to exclude the four named presets entirely, but that removes legitimate functionality. The reporter's option (b) — resolve via MetricRange::tryFrom() — is the correct approach.

title: Metric::resolve cache key has no user or tenant segment — metrics with per-user data leak across accounts
suggestedFix: Both cache paths (the direct cacheFor() path on line 244 and the MartisCache path on line 265) build the key from uriKey + range + filters + locale only. If any metric's calculate() method scopes results to the authenticated user (e.g. 'orders for my team'), the first user to request it writes the result, and every subsequent user with the same range/locale receives that result from cache. Fix: include the auth identifier in the key — `$userKey = (string) ($request->user()?->getAuthIdentifier() ?? 'guest');` — and append it to both key expressions. The MetricController::dashboards() method already does this correctly on line 41 for its cache key, showing the intent. The MetricController::show() method also includes $userKey on line 86. The metric-level cache paths are the gap.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cache key expressions in Metric::resolve() are confirmed in the source at lines 244 and 265 of /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Metrics/Metric.php and neither includes a user identifier:

Line 244: `$cacheKey = 'martis_metric_'.md5($this->uriKey().'_'.$range.'_'.$filters.'_'.app()->getLocale());`
Line 265: `$key = md5($this->uriKey().'_'.$range.'_'.$filters.'_'.app()->getLocale());`

The reporter's claim about MetricController is also accurate: `dashboards()` (line 40-41) and `show()` (line 85-86) both include `$userKey` in their cache keys, but those cache the dashboard structure/definition. The metric result computation paths at MetricController lines 120 and 156 call `$metric->resolve($request)` with no user key injection at the call site, meaning the scoping responsibility falls entirely on Metric::resolve() itself — which omits it.

MartisCache::buildKey (line 323-325) confirms there is no user-level scoping inside MartisCache; it builds `martis:cache:{type}:v{version}:{key}` and the user component must come from the key argument passed in.

The finding is real: any metric whose calculate() method scopes results to the authenticated user directly (e.g. `where('assigned_to', $request->user()->id)`) would have its result cached by the first requestor and served to all subsequent users sharing the same uriKey+range+filters+locale. The framework provides no guard or documentation against this pattern.

Severity assessment (adjusted down from high to medium): The leak requires a metric author to scope results to the current user directly inside calculate() rather than through the idiomatic filterScope mechanism. In the filterScope pattern, filter values are serialized into the $filters cache key segment, so user-scoped filters would produce distinct keys. The vulnerability is real but is conditional on a specific implementation pattern by third-party metric authors. It is also constrained to the admin panel audience (already authenticated users), not anonymous access. These factors reduce practical severity from high to medium, though in a multi-tenant SaaS context it could escalate to high if tenant-scoped metrics are common.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (appending `$request->user()?->getAuthIdentifier()` to both key expressions) is correct and sufficient. A secondary improvement would be to document in the Metric base class docblock that calculate() implementations must not scope by user identity directly — all user-context constraints should flow through the filterScope mechanism, which is already included in the cache key via the $filters segment. This prevents the vulnerability class entirely rather than just widening the cache key.

title: TrendMetric and PartitionMetric interpolate column names directly into DB::raw without quoting — SQL injection and reserved-word breakage
evidence:
$expression = $function === AggregateFunction::Count
    ? DB::raw('count(*) as aggregate')
    : DB::raw("{$function->value}({$column}) as aggregate");
...
->select(DB::raw("DATE_FORMAT({$dateColumn}, '{$dateFormat}') as date_key"), $expression)
suggestedFix: The $column and $dateColumn values come from method callers (i.e. package consumers writing their own TrendMetric subclasses). They are not parameterized: DB::raw("{$function->value}({$column}) as aggregate") interpolates the column name into the SQL string. While the attack vector requires the developer to pass an externally-controlled string (unlikely but not impossible with dynamic column selection), the immediate practical risk is breakage when a column name is a SQL reserved word (e.g. 'order', 'key', 'index'). Fix: wrap column names with `DB::getQueryGrammar()->wrap($column)` before interpolation into the raw expression: DB::raw("{$function->value}("  . DB::getQueryGrammar()->wrap($column) . ") as aggregate"). Same fix applies to $dateColumn in TrendMetric line 153 and $column in PartitionMetric line 82.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at TrendMetric.php:146-153 and PartitionMetric.php:80-82 does interpolate column names directly into DB::raw() strings without quoting. However, the severity claimed ("high / SQL injection") is overstated for two reasons:

1. No user-controlled input reaches $column or $dateColumn. Both values come exclusively from the package consumer's own PHP code (e.g. `$this->sumByDays($request, Order::class, 'total_amount')`). There is no path from $request query parameters or request body to these strings. The AggregateFunction enum's ->value property is one of five hardcoded safe SQL function names ('count','sum','min','max','avg'), so that part is not injectable at all. A true SQL injection via this path would require a developer to deliberately route HTTP input into their own metric column argument, which is far outside the normal attack surface of a library.

2. The real defect is reserved-word breakage and cross-driver portability. Columns like 'order', 'key', 'index', or 'rank' embedded inside DB::raw() bypass Eloquent's grammar wrapping, causing syntax errors on all supported drivers. The $dateColumn in line 153 has the same issue, and PartitionMetric::aggregate() at line 82 has the same pattern. This is a genuine correctness/portability bug that can silently break any consumer metric that happens to aggregate over a column whose name is a SQL reserved word. The suggested fix (DB::getQueryGrammar()->wrap($column)) is correct.

In summary: the finding is real but the severity is low-to-medium, not high. It is a developer ergonomics / reserved-word portability defect, not a user-facing SQL injection vulnerability. Adjusted severity: low (the attack vector requires developer self-sabotage; the practical risk is reserved-word syntax errors on legitimate column names).
verdict.fixNote: The fix direction is correct: wrap $column and $dateColumn with DB::getQueryGrammar()->wrap() before interpolating into DB::raw(). The same applies to PartitionMetric::aggregate() line 82. However this is a developer-ergonomics/portability fix, not a security patch. The $groupBy column in PartitionMetric line 86 is passed directly to the Eloquent builder's ->select() and ->groupBy() (not inside DB::raw()), so the query grammar wraps it automatically — no fix needed there.

title: TrendResult::toArray latestValue uses end() ?: 0 which coerces float 0.0 and a legitimate last-value of false to int 0
evidence: $data['latestValue'] = end($this->values) ?: 0;
suggestedFix: end() returns false for an empty array but also returns 0 (int) or 0.0 (float) for a legitimately zero last value; the ?: operator then replaces any falsy result — including 0.0 — with 0. The intent is to default to 0 only when the array is empty. Use `$last = end($this->values); $data['latestValue'] = $last !== false ? $last : 0;` instead. This correctly emits 0.0 as a real value and only falls back to 0 for an empty series.

verdict.reasoning:
Line 111 of TrendResult.php confirms the exact code cited: `$data['latestValue'] = end($this->values) ?: 0;`. The `?:` operator replaces any falsy result from `end()` -- including a legitimate last value of `0` or `0.0` -- with the integer `0`. The reporter's description of the mechanics is accurate.

However, the practical severity is lower than the report implies for two reasons:

1. The PHP int/float distinction (`0` vs `0.0`) is erased in transit. PHP JSON-encodes `0` as `0` and `0.0` as `0.0`, but JavaScript's `JSON.parse` maps both to the same IEEE-754 double `0`. TrendCard.tsx reads `latestValue` as `number | undefined` and calls `.toLocaleString()` on it, producing `"0"` for either. No downstream consumer distinguishes int 0 from float 0.0, so the coercion from `0.0` to `0` has zero visible impact on rendering or logic.

2. The genuinely observable defect is the empty-array case: `end([])` returns `false`, so `false ?: 0` emits `latestValue: 0` for an empty series. TrendCard.tsx's guard on line 23 checks `displayValue !== undefined`, and `0` is not `undefined`, so `"0"` renders as the KPI value even when there is no data. This is a real but minor display regression (a zero-value stub instead of a blank). The reporter's suggested fix (`$last !== false ? $last : 0`) is correct and addresses this.

The finding is real and the fix is sound, but the stated motivation (preserving `0.0` float precision for TypeScript) is the weakest part of the claim -- that path causes no observable problem. The actual harm is the empty-series false-positive display of `"0"`, which is a lower-severity presentational issue rather than a data-correctness or security issue.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (`$last = end($this->values); $data['latestValue'] = $last !== false ? $last : 0;`) is correct. The motivation about preserving `0.0` float precision is technically accurate in PHP but has no observable effect in the JavaScript consumer, since `JSON.parse` maps both `0` and `0.0` to the same JS number. The real value of the fix is the empty-array case: without it, `showLatestValue()` on an empty series renders `"0"` instead of nothing, because `false ?: 0` produces `0`, which passes the `displayValue !== undefined` guard in TrendCard.tsx (line 23). An alternative that also omits the key for an empty array: `if ($this->values !== []) { $data['latestValue'] = end($this->values); }` -- this is arguably more expressive of the intent.

title: ProgressResult percentage calculation treats negative target as zero — silently renders 0% instead of an error or correct percentage
suggestedFix: The guard `$this->target > 0` is correct for the division-by-zero case but also silently returns 0% when target is negative (e.g. a metric tracking deficit reduction toward -100). The practical risk is low (negative targets are unusual) but could confuse developers. Consider throwing an InvalidArgumentException in the constructor when target < 0, or at minimum documenting that target must be a positive number.

verdict.reasoning: The cited code is confirmed exactly as reported. Line 68 of ProgressResult.php reads `$percentage = $this->target > 0 ? round(($this->current / $this->target) * 100, 1) : 0;`. The constructor (lines 20-24) accepts any `float|int` for `$target` with no validation guard, so a negative value passes through silently and `toArray()` emits `percentage: 0` even though the arithmetic is well-defined (e.g., current=-30, target=-100 should yield 30%). No guard exists elsewhere: all three call sites in ProgressMetric.php (lines 44, 57, 65) forward the developer-supplied target value unchanged. The `avoid()` flag addresses minimization semantics but does not impose a sign constraint on target — a developer building a "reduce defects to 0" metric would use target=0, which hits the same silent-zero branch. The defect is real but the attack surface is zero: target is set exclusively in developer-written metric subclass code, never from user input or HTTP request data. No end-user can trigger the silent-0% path. The risk is purely a developer experience issue — a developer who passes a negative target gets wrong output with no feedback. The reporter's suggested severity of "low" is appropriate; it is a latent correctness bug on an unusual input rather than any exploitable flaw. The suggested fix (throw InvalidArgumentException in the constructor when target < 0, or add a docblock constraint) is correct and sufficient.
verdict.fixNote: The guard `$this->target > 0` correctly prevents division by zero for target=0, but it overloads that protection to also swallow negative targets silently. The cleanest fix is a constructor guard: `if ($target < 0) { throw new \InvalidArgumentException('ProgressResult target must be >= 0.'); }` This makes the contract explicit and gives developers an immediate, actionable error instead of a confusing 0% in production. If negative targets are ever a desired use-case (e.g. deficit-reduction metrics), the percentage formula itself would need to change (use abs($target) or a signed-ratio approach), which is a separate design decision the team should make explicitly rather than inheriting the current silent behaviour.

title: Pivot action execution skips per-model canRun and resource-level authorization checks
suggestedFix: Mirror the per-model authorization loop from `execute()` (lines 154-171) inside `executePivot()`. After resolving `$models`, iterate and call `$actionInstance->authorizedToRun($request, $model)` and `$resourceForModel->authorizedToRunAction()` / `authorizedToRunDestructiveAction()` exactly as the non-pivot path does. A destructive pivot action currently bypasses the delete-policy gate entirely.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms the finding. executePivot (lines 534-642) performs only one authorization check: authorizedToSee at line 566. After that guard passes, it resolves related models via the parent relationship (line 590) and immediately executes the action (lines 613-618) with no per-model authorization loop.

By contrast, the non-pivot execute method (lines 154-171) wraps every resolved model in a loop that calls:
1. $actionInstance->authorizedToRun($request, $model) — the action-level per-model gate (Action.php:185)
2. $resourceForModel->authorizedToRunDestructiveAction($request) or ->authorizedToRunAction($request) — the resource-level policy gate (Resource.php:656, 669)

Both methods exist and are callable. The same $resourceClass variable is available inside executePivot; constructing new $resourceClass($model) per model is the same pattern used in execute at line 160. The loop is simply absent from the pivot path.

The practical consequence: any authenticated user whose role passes authorizedToSee can fire a destructive pivot action (isDestructive() = true) against any set of related model IDs they supply, regardless of model-level policies or the runDestructiveAction ability. The delete-policy gate is fully bypassed for all pivot actions. The claimed severity of critical is confirmed: this is a complete authorization bypass on a destructive write path, not a hardening gap.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is straightforward: after line 596 (the isSole check) and before field validation, add the same loop that execute() uses at lines 154-171. Guard it with if (! $actionInstance->isStandalone()), then iterate $models, call $actionInstance->authorizedToRun($request, $model), construct $resourceForModel = new $resourceClass($model), and branch on $actionInstance->isDestructive() to call authorizedToRunDestructiveAction or authorizedToRunAction. The suggested fix in the report is correct.

title: Action execute() resolveModels() bypasses indexQuery, scopes, and authorizedToViewAny — IDOR on action target models
suggestedFix: Apply `$resourceClass::applyScopes($request, $query)` and `$resourceClass::indexQuery($request, $query)` to the query inside `resolveModels()` before the `whereIn`, exactly as `ResourceController` does. Also add an `authorizedToViewAny` guard before loading models, matching the check in `LensController::index` line 50.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by reading the actual code.

resolveModels() at lines 300-323 of ActionController.php executes: `$modelInstance->newQuery()->whereIn($modelInstance->getKeyName(), $ids)->get()` with no applyScopes, no indexQuery, and no authorizedToViewAny guard. The IDs come directly from `$request->input('resources', [])`.

The execute() method does check authorizedToSee (action-level, line 142) and, for non-standalone actions, calls authorizedToRun($request, $model) and authorizedToRunAction/authorizedToRunDestructiveAction (lines 154-170) per-model AFTER models are already loaded. However:

1. authorizedToRun only fires if the developer explicitly set a canRunCallback; the default is `return true` (Action.php:185-192), making it non-mandatory.
2. authorizedToRunAction/Destructive checks the *action permission* (policy->runAction falling back to update/delete), not record *visibility*. A model that applyScopes or indexQuery would have excluded can still pass authorizedToUpdate.
3. authorizedToViewAny — present in ResourceController (line 89) and LensController (line 50) — is completely absent from ActionController::execute().
4. applyScopes and indexQuery are both absent from resolveModels(), as confirmed by Resource.php which defines them at lines 230 and 278.

Contrast: ResourceController applies both applyScopes and indexQuery (lines 119-120) and calls authorizedToViewAny (line 89) before fetching models. ActionController skips all three.

Practical impact: in a multi-tenant application where indexQuery adds `WHERE tenant_id = ?`, an authenticated user of tenant A can submit IDs belonging to tenant B's records in the resources array and execute the action against them, since the bare newQuery() has no such constraint. Similarly for soft-deleted records excluded by a global scope. This is a real IDOR — the attacker only needs to be authenticated and able to see the action endpoint.

The claimed fix (apply applyScopes + indexQuery inside resolveModels, add authorizedToViewAny before) matches exactly what ResourceController does and would close the gap.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct in substance. One precision: authorizedToViewAny should be called on `new $resourceClass` (a blank instance, no model) before resolveModels(), exactly as ResourceController line 89 does: `$instance->authorizedToViewAny($request)`. Inside resolveModels(), the query should be built as `$resourceClass::applyScopes($request, $modelInstance->newQuery())` followed by `$resourceClass::indexQuery($request, $query)` before the whereIn, to honour both global-scope injections and the developer's custom visibility hook. The severity is adjusted to high rather than critical because exploitation requires a valid authenticated session with access to the resource's action routes; unauthenticated access is not possible. In a multi-tenant or row-level-security deployment the impact is severe but the attack surface is narrower than a fully unauthenticated IDOR.

title: ExecuteAction job double-encodes original/changes columns — JSON stored as string instead of object
evidence:
ActionEvent::where(...)
    ->update([
        'status' => $status,
        'exception' => $exception ?? '',
        'original' => json_encode($originalDiff),
        'changes' => json_encode($changesDiff),
    ]);
suggestedFix: Remove the `json_encode()` calls. `ActionEvent` declares `'original' => 'array'` and `'changes' => 'array'` casts. However, `Builder::update()` bypasses Eloquent casting, so the JSON-encoded string is stored literally. The `ActionController::logActionEvent()` path (line 463-478) passes raw arrays to `ActionEvent::create()`, which correctly triggers the cast. Either pass raw arrays to `update()` (and rely on Eloquent's cast on read — but note `update()` still bypasses casts on write for non-JSON columns on some drivers), or use `json_encode()` consistently and remove the cast, or update via model instances rather than a mass-update query.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. ActionEvent declares `'original' => 'array'` and `'changes' => 'array'` casts (ActionEvent.php lines 56-58). The columns are `text` in the migration. At ExecuteAction.php lines 123-124, `json_encode($originalDiff)` and `json_encode($changesDiff)` are passed to `Builder::update()`. Laravel's `Builder::update()` is a raw query-builder operation that bypasses Eloquent's `setAttribute()` / cast pipeline entirely — the value is bound directly to PDO. The `'array'` cast in Laravel stores via `json_encode` on write (through `setAttribute`) and restores via `json_decode` on read. Because `Builder::update()` skips the write side of the cast, the column receives the already-`json_encode`d string. On read, the cast's `json_decode` call converts the outer JSON layer back to a PHP string (the inner JSON), not an array. This is the double-encoding: the stored bytes are a JSON-encoded JSON string rather than a JSON-encoded array. The create path in `ActionController::logActionEvent()` correctly passes raw arrays to `ActionEvent::create()` (which routes through `setAttribute` and applies the cast once), making the two paths inconsistent. The bug means any consumer that reads `original` or `changes` from an `ActionEvent` row written by the queued job path gets a string instead of an array, breaking any diff display or further processing that expects an associative array.
verdict.fixNote: The simplest fix is to remove the explicit `json_encode()` calls at lines 123-124 and pass the raw PHP arrays directly to `Builder::update()`. However, note that `Builder::update()` still bypasses casts on write even without the manual `json_encode`, so the raw array would be passed to PDO as-is and PHP would serialize it as `Array` (lossy). The correct fix is to use model instances rather than a mass-update query: load the matching `ActionEvent` record, set its attributes, and call `save()`, which routes through `setAttribute()` and correctly applies the `'array'` cast. Alternatively, call `json_encode()` explicitly and remove the `'original'` and `'changes'` entries from the `casts()` array so the encoding contract is uniform across both create and update paths.

title: LensRequest::withOrdering passes raw HTTP sort column to orderBy() with no whitelist validation
suggestedFix: Validate `$this->sortColumn` against the lens's declared sortable fields before injecting it into the query, exactly as `ResourceController::applySorting()` does (lines 1606-1616: builds `$sortableAttributes` from fields that return `isSortable() === true` and checks `in_array($sort, $sortableAttributes, true)` before calling `orderBy`). `LensController` should resolve the lens fields and pass the allowed set into `LensRequest`, or `withOrdering` should accept an explicit `$allowed` list parameter. Without this, any authenticated user can inject an arbitrary column expression as the sort key.

verdict.reasoning:
Reading the full call chain confirms the vulnerability exists with no mitigating guard:

1. `LensRequest::fromRequest` (LensRequest.php:113-114) assigns the raw HTTP `sort` query parameter to `$req->sortColumn` with no validation: `$req->sortColumn = is_string($rawSort) && $rawSort !== '' ? $rawSort : null;`

2. `LensRequest::withOrdering` (LensRequest.php:72-73) passes that value directly to `$query->orderBy($this->sortColumn, ...)` with no allowlist check.

3. `LensController::index` (LensController.php:64) constructs the `LensRequest` from the raw HTTP request and delivers it to the lens's `query()` method. Neither the controller nor the abstract `Lens` base class builds or enforces any sortable-fields whitelist before or after this delegation.

4. Every concrete lens in both the package tests (`LensTestTopRevenueLens` at LensControllerTest.php:50) and the playground (`MostValuableClients` at MostValuableClients.php:31, `CriticalProjects`, `OverdueInvoices`) calls `$request->withOrdering(...)` with no custom validation layer around it. The API contract on `Lens::query()` is open-ended — there is no mechanism in the base class that would enforce a whitelist before `withOrdering` is reached.

5. By direct contrast, `ResourceController::applySorting` (ResourceController.php:1606-1616) builds `$sortableAttributes` from fields where `isSortable() === true`, then enforces `in_array($sort, $sortableAttributes, true)` before calling `orderBy`. This guard is entirely absent in the lens code path.

There is no framework-level protection: Laravel's query builder does not sanitise column-name arguments to `orderBy`. An authenticated user with access to any lens can inject an arbitrary string as the sort column. The severity remains high: the endpoint is behind authentication, but once authenticated the attack surface is unrestricted — all lens endpoints share the same unguarded path. On MySQL and Postgres this enables at minimum schema disclosure through error messages on invalid expressions and, depending on driver configuration, expression injection into ORDER BY (e.g. subqueries, conditional expressions used for time-based blind extraction).
verdict.fixNote: The fix should mirror exactly what ResourceController::applySorting does: resolve the lens's declared fields, build an allowlist of attributes where isSortable() returns true, and check the incoming sort column against that list before calling orderBy. The cleanest approach is to add a withOrdering overload (or a new method) on LensRequest that accepts an explicit $allowedColumns list, and have LensController build that list from the resolved lens fields before constructing the LensRequest — the same fields array that is already resolved at LensController.php:114 via resolveLensFields(). Developers who call withOrdering with no allowlist in their own Lens::query() implementations would need to update those callsites.

title: LensRequest::withFilters does not guard against null/empty values before calling filter->apply()
suggestedFix: Add the same null/empty guard used in `ResourceController::applyFilters()` (line 1570): `if ($value === null || $value === '') { continue; }` before calling `$filter->apply()`. Without this guard, a `DateFilter::apply()` receiving `null` passes `null` directly to `whereDate($column, $op, null)`, which produces `WHERE date_col = ''` or a driver-specific error. `DateRangeFilter::apply()` is protected by its own `is_array($value)` check, but `DateFilter` and any custom `SelectFilter` are not.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by direct code reading.

LensRequest::fromRequest() (lines 98-108) stores the raw JSON-decoded filter map into $req->selectedFilters with no null/empty filtering at intake. A payload like {"created_at": null} or {"created_at": ""} is valid JSON and produces an array entry with a null or empty-string value that survives into selectedFilters.

LensRequest::withFilters() (lines 46-58) iterates selectedFilters, skips only when the filter instance is not found ($filter === null), and calls $filter->apply($this, $generic, $value) with no null/empty guard on $value.

The counterpart in ResourceController::applyFilters() (lines 1570-1572) has the explicit guard:
  if ($value === null || $value === '') { continue; }
before calling $filter->apply(). That guard is absent in LensRequest::withFilters().

DateFilter::apply() (line 66) passes $value directly to $query->whereDate($this->column, $this->operator->value, $value) with no internal null check, so a null value reaches the query builder unconditionally.

No mitigating factor was found: there is no shared helper or base-class guard, no validation layer between fromRequest() and withFilters(), and the referenced ResourceController guard does not apply to the lens code path at all. The asymmetry is structural and confirmed.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as stated: add `if ($value === null || $value === '') { continue; }` in LensRequest::withFilters() before the method_exists/apply call, mirroring ResourceController::applyFilters() line 1570. No change to DateFilter itself is needed since the guard belongs at the dispatch site.

title: ActionController returns HTTP 404 instead of 403 for authorization failures on individual models
suggestedFix: Return `JsonErrorResponse::forbidden(...)` (HTTP 403) for all three authorization failure branches. Using 404 for authorization failures is an intentional obfuscation pattern in some APIs, but here the message body explicitly says 'not authorized', which negates the security benefit while confusing API consumers. If the team decides to keep 404 for security-through-obscurity, the message should be neutralized to not reveal the authorization reason. The current combination (404 + explicit 'not authorized' message) is the worst of both worlds.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the actual code. In `ActionController::execute()` (lines 156-168), three per-model authorization branches all call `JsonErrorResponse::notFound(...)`, which the `JsonErrorResponse` class (line 86-89) maps to HTTP 404. The message bodies are "You are not authorized to run this action on one or more selected resources.", "You are not authorized to run this destructive action.", and "You are not authorized to run this action." — explicit authorization language in 404 responses.

The `JsonErrorResponse::forbidden()` factory (lines 93-97) exists, returns HTTP 403, and is already used correctly one guard earlier in the same method at line 143 (`authorizedToSee` check). There is no alternative guard elsewhere that would make these branches unreachable; `resolveActions()` (line 277) filters by `authorizedToSee`, but `authorizedToRun` and `authorizedToRunDestructiveAction`/`authorizedToRunAction` are distinct per-model checks that can still fail after the `authorizedToSee` gate passes. The fix is straightforward: replace `notFound` with `forbidden` on all three branches at lines 157, 163, and 167.

The claim's severity assessment is accurate: the combination of 404 status and "not authorized" message text is the worst of both worlds — it reveals the authorization reason in the body (negating any security-through-obscurity benefit of 404) while still misleading API consumers about the actual error category. This is a genuine defect, not a deliberate pattern, given that `forbidden` is used correctly just above it.
verdict.fixNote: Replace all three `JsonErrorResponse::notFound(...)` calls in the per-model authorization loop (ActionController.php lines 157, 163, 167) with `JsonErrorResponse::forbidden(...)`. The `forbidden()` factory is already imported and used correctly at line 143 in the same method. No other changes are needed — the message strings can stay as-is once the status code is corrected.

title: Dashboard lock state cached and served stale on the /api/dashboards list endpoint
suggestedFix:
Dashboard::toArray() calls $this->lockPayloadNow() at line 298 of Dashboard.php, which evaluates the lock predicate and bakes the result into the serialized array. That serialized array is then stored in the cache under the user+locale key. If the user's plan is upgraded or downgraded, the cached payload still contains the old lock state until the TTL expires — a locked user who buys a plan upgrade keeps seeing the locked sidebar entry for the full cache window.

The show() endpoint already has the correct fix (it evaluates lockPayloadFor() outside the cache closure and short-circuits). Apply the same pattern to dashboards(): move the lock evaluation outside the cache boundary, or strip the lock field from the cached payload and re-inject it at response time. Alternatively, clear the 'dashboards' cache layer on plan/role changes.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms the defect. In MetricController::dashboards() (lines 41-46), the cache closure calls $d->toArray() on each DashboardContract instance. Dashboard::toArray() (Dashboard.php line 298) calls $this->lockPayloadNow(), which resolves the active request from the container and evaluates the live lock predicate via lockPayloadFor(). That result — including the lock key — is serialized into the array that gets stored in the cache under the user+locale key. On subsequent requests within the TTL the stored array is returned as-is; lockPayloadNow() is never called again, so plan/role changes are not reflected until the cache expires.

The show() endpoint has an explicit pre-cache guard (lines 70-79) that evaluates lockPayloadFor() before entering the cache block and short-circuits when the result is non-null. The inline comment at lines 66-69 names the reason: "We deliberately bypass the dashboard cache here so a plan upgrade lands immediately." That protection is absent from dashboards().

The more serious direction is downgrade: a user whose plan is revoked would see the sidebar list entry as unlocked (lock: null in the stale cache) even though show() would correctly block full navigation. The sidebar's lock icon and click-intercept are driven by the list payload, so the UX-level soft-gate would fail silently for the window between downgrade and cache expiry. show()'s guard does prevent actual content access, which caps the blast radius, but the list-level stale lock state is a real correctness and minor authorization-surface defect. Severity is medium rather than high: show() provides a backstop against full content access on downgrades, and the window is bounded by TTL — but the stale list state is a genuine bug the reporter correctly identified.
verdict.fixNote: The show() endpoint already demonstrates the correct pattern: evaluate lockPayloadFor($request) before the cache block and short-circuit when non-null. The same guard should be applied per-dashboard inside the dashboards() closure, or the lock key should be stripped from the cached array and re-injected outside the cache boundary at response time. Alternatively, invalidate the 'dashboards' cache layer on plan/role change events. The show() guard is not sufficient on its own because the sidebar list drives the lock icon and click-intercept affordances independently of full-page navigation.

title: MenuItem::resolveDashboardItem does not include the dashboard icon in the serialized payload when icon() returns a value
suggestedFix:
Dashboard::icon() was added in v1.11.4 and is now part of the concrete class (not the DashboardContract interface). resolveDashboardItem() deliberately falls back only to $this->icon (the MenuItem-level override) and ignores $dashboard->icon(). The comment says 'Dashboard does not expose icon() in the contract' — but the method exists on the concrete class. The Sidebar uses the dashboard icon correctly (it reads it from the /api/dashboards list payload via DashboardDefinition.icon), but a custom mainMenu() that places a Dashboard via MenuItem::dashboard() will serve icon: null to the sidebar instead of the dashboard's configured icon, silently discarding it.

Fix: in resolveDashboardItem(), after instantiating $dashboard, resolve the icon with: 'icon' => $this->icon ?? (method_exists($dashboard, 'icon') ? $dashboard->icon() : null). This mirrors the pattern used by resolveLensItem and resolveToolItem for their respective icon() calls.

verdict.reasoning: Code confirms the defect. In resolveDashboardItem (MenuItem.php:324-342), the variable $dashboard passes an `instanceof Dashboard` guard at line 328, so it is guaranteed to be the concrete Dashboard class. Dashboard::icon() exists at Dashboard.php:159-162 and is not part of DashboardContract, but the instanceof guard makes it safely callable. The assignment at line 338 is `'icon' => $this->icon` — it never consults $dashboard->icon(). The comment "Dashboard does not expose icon() in the contract" is accurate but does not justify the omission: resolveToolItem at line 314 reads `'icon' => $this->icon ?? $tool->icon()` on the ToolContract interface where icon() IS declared. After the instanceof Dashboard guard, $dashboard->icon() is equally safe to call. DashboardContract has no icon() method (verified in DashboardContract.php lines 17-101), but that is immaterial once the instanceof guard resolves the concrete type. The auto-build path is unaffected because Dashboard::toArray() at line 293 includes the icon. The gap is isolated to MenuItem::dashboard() calls where the consumer relies on the dashboard's own withIcon() value and does not also call ->icon() on the MenuItem wrapper. The severity is low: it only affects the custom mainMenu() path, the sidebar still works via the auto-build path, and the consumer can work around it with a one-line ->icon() call on the MenuItem.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix using `method_exists($dashboard, 'icon')` is unnecessarily defensive since the instanceof Dashboard guard already guarantees the method exists. A cleaner fix is `'icon' => $this->icon ?? $dashboard->icon()` directly at line 338 — no method_exists check needed. This exactly mirrors the pattern in resolveToolItem at line 314.

title: HasPolicy static cache uses self:: in trait body — flushPolicyCache() on one consuming class does not flush caches for other consumers
suggestedFix: In PHP, a static property declared in a trait is copied per consuming class. self:: inside a trait method resolves to the class that inlined the trait at compile time. Because each consumer (Dashboard, Tool, Resource) gets its own copy of $resolvedHasPolicyPolicies, calling Dashboard::flushPolicyCache() only clears the Dashboard copy. Tests that call flushPolicyCache() on one class and then test another class via the same trait will still hit the stale cache. In test code this is the documented pattern (each test calls flushPolicyCache() on the exact class it needs), but a helper that calls Martis::flushAllPolicyCaches() would need to enumerate all trait consumers. Low practical impact in production (each request starts fresh); medium impact in test suites that share the process.

verdict.reasoning:
The PHP mechanics are confirmed real. Live PHP execution proves that `self::` inside a trait method binds to the per-consuming-class copy of a static property: flushing `A::flush()` leaves `B`'s cache intact. `HasPolicy` is used by `Dashboard` (src/Dashboards/Dashboard.php:28) and `Tool` (src/Tools/Tool.php:66). Calling `Dashboard::flushPolicyCache()` clears only `Dashboard::$resolvedHasPolicyPolicies`; `Tool::$resolvedHasPolicyPolicies` survives untouched.

However, two facts shrink the severity well below what the reporter implied:

1. `Resource` does NOT use this trait at all. It declares its own `private static array $resolvedPolicies = []` (Resource.php:69) and overrides `flushPolicyCache()` (Resource.php:591) independently. The reporter's example of "Dashboard vs Resource cross-contamination" is impossible — they share no static property.

2. The only real consumers of `HasPolicy` are `Dashboard` and `Tool`. Every `flushPolicyCache()` call in the entire test suite targets `Resource::flushPolicyCache()`, which operates on Resource's private, trait-independent cache. No test calls `Dashboard::flushPolicyCache()` or `Tool::flushPolicyCache()` at all — so the latent isolation bug is currently inert in the test suite.

Production impact is zero: PHP-FPM creates a fresh process per request, so the static cache never persists between requests. The defect is a latent test-isolation hazard that would trigger only if a future test populates `Dashboard`'s cache and then inspects `Tool` (or vice versa) after calling `flushPolicyCache()` on only one of them. That test does not exist today. The severity is low, exactly as claimed, but confined to the Dashboard/Tool pair only.
verdict.fixNote: The fix scope is narrower than the reporter stated. Resource is unaffected (it owns its own cache independently). Only Dashboard and Tool share the trait. A shared flush helper such as a static `HasPolicy::flushAll()` that maintains a registry of consumers is one option, but the simpler and more practical fix for tests is a base test helper that calls both `Dashboard::flushPolicyCache()` and `Tool::flushPolicyCache()` together whenever either is needed. In production no fix is required since PHP-FPM resets static state per request.

title: 2FA setup silently overwrites confirmed secret — no password/OTP guard
suggestedFix: In `generateSetup()`, check whether the user already has a confirmed 2FA (`$this->isEnabled($user)`) and refuse (throw `\InvalidArgumentException`) unless the caller supplies a valid current password or current OTP. The corresponding `twoFactorSetup` route in `ProfileController` should then validate `current_password` (via `current_password` rule) before delegating — identical to how `twoFactorDisable` is protected. Without this guard anyone who steals an active session can silently rotate the victim's TOTP secret, causing the legitimate TOTP app entry to stop working while the attacker can enrol their own device.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. `ProfileController::twoFactorSetup()` (line 119-125) calls `TwoFactorService::generateSetup()` with no prior credential check. The method unconditionally overwrites `two_factor_secret` and saves (TwoFactorService.php:47-48). The route at martis.php:309 sits inside the `martis.2fa` middleware group (line 205), but that middleware (`EnsureTwoFactorChallenge`) only blocks requests when 2FA is enabled AND the session has not yet passed the challenge. Once a session is fully authenticated with 2FA satisfied (`martis_two_factor_passed = true`), the middleware is a no-op and `twoFactorSetup` is reachable with no additional guard. By contrast, `twoFactorDisable` (line 168-181) explicitly validates `current_password` via the `current_password` Laravel rule, making the asymmetry intentional and the omission on `twoFactorSetup` a genuine gap. An attacker holding a stolen authenticated session can POST to `/api/profile/2fa/setup`, receive a new TOTP secret/QR, confirm it with their own authenticator, and take over the victim's TOTP enrolment without knowing the password or the existing OTP. The `confirm()` method (line 64-85) only checks that `two_factor_secret` is non-null — it does not verify the session owns the pending setup.
verdict.fixNote: The severity is high rather than critical because exploitation requires an already-authenticated session (not just any unauthenticated request) — the attacker must first steal a valid session where 2FA has already been passed. A critical rating would apply if the route were unauthenticated. The fix is exactly as suggested: in `ProfileController::twoFactorSetup`, add `$request->validate(['current_password' => ['required', 'string', 'current_password']]);` before delegating to `generateSetup()`, mirroring the guard already present on `twoFactorDisable`. No change is required inside `TwoFactorService::generateSetup()` itself — the guard belongs at the controller layer where HTTP context is available.

title: hasLastUsedColumn static cache leaks across users under long-running processes
suggestedFix: The PHP function-scope `static` variable is process-persistent under Laravel Octane, Swoole, RoadRunner, and FrankenPHP. In those runtimes the cached value from the first request is reused for every subsequent request in the same worker. If a consumer runs a database migration that adds or drops the `two_factor_last_used_at` column while the process is live, the stale `false` cache will silently disable replay-protection for all subsequent logins in that worker. Change the cache key to the connection + table string so the check is at least scoped correctly, or use `Cache::remember()` with a short TTL (60s) keyed on the model's connection and table name so it survives across requests but invalidates quickly after migrations.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at TwoFactorService.php:306-321 is confirmed exactly as quoted. The `static $cached = null` variable is PHP function-scope static: it is process-persistent and is never reset between requests. The docblock at line 304 claims "Resolves once per request" but the implementation delivers "once per process lifetime."

Two compounding facts make this real:

1. TwoFactorService is registered as `$this->app->singleton(TwoFactorService::class)` (MartisServiceProvider.php:114), not `scoped()`. The comment at line 116-121 explicitly notes that `MartisCache` was changed to `scoped()` for per-request reset, but TwoFactorService was not given the same treatment.
