Set `open={showConfirm}`, pass `confirmMessage={t('sessions_revoke_others_confirm', ...)}`, fire the revoke logic in `onConfirm`, and cancel in `onCancel`. The `resourceLabel` prop can be set to an empty string or omitted if the modal variant is adjusted, since this is not a record-delete flow but a session-revoke flow. Consider adding a `variant='danger'` or reusing the existing `confirmMessage` prop to surface a custom message without showing the resource-label delete pattern.

title: GlobalSearch: redundant double-guard on item.url before navigation
suggestedFix: The inner `if ('url' in item && item.url)` is always true when the outer `if (item.url)` is true. Collapse to a single guard: `if (item.url) { navigate(item.url); onClose() }`. The current nesting is dead logic but harmless — it just masks the intent.

verdict.reasoning: Lines 304-309 of GlobalSearch.tsx contain the exact code cited. The outer `if (item.url)` already guards for a truthy url value. Inside that block, `'url' in item` is unconditionally true because every variant of the `PaletteItem` union (lines 83-88) declares a `url` field — `resource`, `action`, `view-all`, and `record` have `url: string`; `recent` has `url: string | null`; `recent-query` has `url: null`. Since the key is present on all union members, `'url' in item` can never be false for a valid `PaletteItem`. The inner `item.url` re-checks the same value already confirmed truthy by the outer guard. The result is a permanently true inner condition, making `navigate` and `onClose` reachable only via the outer guard — the inner if adds no protection and no narrowing. The finding is real. Severity remains low: the nesting is dead logic with no correctness impact at runtime — `navigate` is called whenever and only whenever `item.url` is truthy, exactly as intended — but it obscures intent and could confuse future maintainers who might think the `'url' in` check is discriminating between union variants.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested collapse to `if (item.url) { navigate(item.url); onClose() }` is correct. No TypeScript type assertion is needed because the outer check already narrows `item.url` to a truthy string for all union variants that can reach this branch.

title: NotificationBell action_url: internal-looking paths use hard full-page reload instead of SPA navigation
suggestedFix: Paths starting with `/` inside a Martis SPA are almost always in-app routes. Using `window.location.href` causes a full page reload and discards all React Query caches. Use `navigate(item.action_url)` (from `useNavigate`) for paths that start with `/martis` or the configured prefix, or simply always use `navigate` for relative paths and `window.open` only for absolute `http(s)://` URLs.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at NotificationBell.tsx lines 178-181 is exactly as reported. The component uses window.location.href for any action_url starting with "/" and window.open for all others — there is no SPA navigation path at all.

Three independent confirmations make this real:

1. The component has no import of useNavigate from react-router-dom. Every other navigation-capable component in the codebase (Topbar.tsx:2, GlobalSearch.tsx:3, ActionDrawer.tsx:2, RelationshipTableShell.tsx:3, and all resource pages) imports and calls useNavigate. NotificationBell is the only component with a clickable navigation action that skips it entirely.

2. The canonical docblock in MartisNotification.php line 27 shows the intended usage pattern as `actionUrl: route('martis.spa').'/resources/invoices/42'`, which evaluates to a path like /martis/resources/invoices/42. That path starts with "/" and therefore always takes the window.location.href branch, causing a full-page reload on every notification click for in-app links.

3. NotificationControllerTest.php line 94 confirms the test expectation is `'/martis/resources/invoices/42'`, a slash-prefixed path. There is no guard, SPA-prefix check, or shared helper that intercepts this before the window.location.href call executes.

No false-positive grounds apply: there is no alternative code path, no wrapping navigator, no framework-level interception. The SPA router (createBrowserRouter) is in place; React Query caches would be discarded on every in-app notification click.
verdict.fixNote: The fix proposed by the reporter is correct in direction. The cleanest implementation is to add `import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom'` at the top of the file, call `const navigate = useNavigate()` inside the component, then replace the branch with: if `item.action_url.startsWith('/')` use `navigate(item.action_url)`, else `window.open(...)`. No prefix-matching heuristic is needed because the SPA router handles any in-app relative path; the only paths that should open a new tab are absolute http(s) URLs, which already fall into the else branch.

title: DrawerDetail: Delete button always visible regardless of authorizedToDelete
suggestedFix: The Table component checks `row._authorization?.authorizedToDelete` before rendering the delete button. The DrawerDetail footer does not. Add a guard: `const deleteDisabled = activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToDelete === false` and either hide the button or disable it when that is true. Without this, a user who can open a detail drawer can always click Delete, relying solely on the server-side check — which is correct defence-in-depth, but the UI misleads the user.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the source. DrawerDetail.tsx lines 106-113 renders the Delete button with no guard on `activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToDelete`. The sibling surfaces that handle the same pattern all apply a guard: ResourceDetail.tsx:270 derives `const canDelete = auth?.authorizedToDelete !== false` and conditions the button at line 345 with `{!isDeleted && canDelete && (...)}`. Table.tsx:598 derives `const deleteDisabled = row._authorization?.authorizedToDelete === false` and passes it to the RowActionButton disabled prop. DrawerDetail has neither. The `activeRecord` variable is already in scope at line 48, so `activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToDelete` is reachable. The defect is real. Severity is adjusted down from high to medium because the server-side policy still rejects unauthorized DELETE mutations — no data can be destroyed by an unauthorized user through this path — but the missing UI guard causes visible mislead (an enabled Delete button for a user who cannot delete) and requires the user to attempt the action before learning they are not authorized.
verdict.fixNote: Mirror the ResourceDetail.tsx pattern: derive `const canDelete = activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToDelete !== false` after line 48 where `activeRecord` is assigned, then wrap the delete button in `{canDelete && (...)}`. Optionally also guard the Edit button with `authorizedToUpdate` for consistency, since ResourceDetail does so at line 269/333.

title: DrawerDetail: Edit button always visible regardless of authorizedToUpdate
evidence:
<button
  type="button"
  onClick={() => onEdit(recordId ? Number(recordId) || recordId : undefined)}
  className="martis-btn-primary inline-flex items-center gap-1.5"
>
  <PencilSimpleIcon size={14} />
  {tAct('edit')}
</button>
suggestedFix: Add `const editDisabled = activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToUpdate === false` and conditionally hide or disable the Edit button. The Table component already does this (line 597); the drawer should mirror the same authorization checks.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the full file. DrawerDetail.tsx has zero references to `_authorization`, `authorizedToUpdate`, or `authorizedToDelete`. Both the Edit button (lines 114-121) and the Delete button (lines 106-113) are rendered unconditionally regardless of the record's authorization payload.

The contrast with the rest of the codebase is unambiguous:
- Table.tsx:597 derives `editDisabled = row._authorization?.authorizedToUpdate === false` and passes it to RowActionButton.
- ResourceDetail.tsx:268-273 derives `canUpdate = auth?.authorizedToUpdate !== false` and gates the Edit button behind it.

The `activeRecord` object (line 48) is in scope and the `_authorization` key is populated by the backend (evidenced by its use in Table and ResourceDetail), so the data needed for the guard is available but simply not consumed.

Severity remains medium (not high): the backend policy gate on PUT/DELETE endpoints blocks actual writes, so this is a UI authorization-surface inconsistency rather than a direct privilege-escalation bypass. However, it violates the established per-record authorization pattern used everywhere else in the UI, and a user with `authorizedToUpdate === false` can still navigate to the edit form and waste a round-trip (or observe the edit form contents) before being rejected. The Delete button is also missing its guard (`authorizedToDelete`), making the scope slightly wider than the reporter noted.
verdict.fixNote: Both buttons need guards, not just Edit. Add `const canUpdate = activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToUpdate !== false` and `const canDelete = activeRecord?._authorization?.authorizedToDelete !== false`, then either hide or disable each button accordingly (matching the pattern in ResourceDetail.tsx:269-270). Also guard against the loading state: when `isLoading` is true, `activeRecord` is undefined, so the buttons should be disabled or omitted until the record resolves.

title: DrawerCreate/DrawerUpdate form items keyed by array index, breaking React reconciliation on field reorder
suggestedFix: The same issue exists in DrawerUpdate (lines 277-284). Use a stable key: for `tab_group`, `section`, and `panel`, use their `label` property; for scalar fields, use `field.attribute`. Index keys cause input state to persist in the wrong field when the server reorders fields (e.g. via a conditional `->when()`), and can cause controlled components to lose focus. DrawerDetail also uses `key={\`tg-${idx}\`}` (line 162-168) with the same risk.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at DrawerCreate.tsx:197-204 and DrawerUpdate.tsx:278-284 confirms the index-key pattern. For tab_group, section, and panel items, `key={idx}` is used, while scalar fields correctly use `key={field.attribute}` at line 208. The pattern is real.

However, the claimed severity scenario is overstated. The schema prop originates from a single React Query fetch in ResourceIndex (queryKey: ['schema', resource]) and is passed as a stable object reference through buildOverrideProps. The fieldsForCreate array is computed server-side once, serialized to JSON, and does not reorder between renders during an active form session. There is no in-session reordering mechanism: PHP's hideWhenCreating/showOnCreating are applied at schema serialization time before the JSON is sent; they do not cause the array to reorder on subsequent renders of the same component instance. The only way the bug would manifest is if React Query refetches and returns a different field order while the drawer is open — a theoretical race requiring a server-side definition change mid-session.

DrawerDetail's `key={\`tg-${idx}\`}` (line 163) and `key={\`panel-${idx}\`}` (line 166) and `key={\`section-${idx}\`}` (line 169) are also index-keyed, but DrawerDetail is read-only (no controlled inputs), so there is zero practical consequence there.

The finding is real as a best-practice violation and a latent reconciliation risk, but the severity is low rather than medium: the scenario that triggers visible user harm (input state migration to wrong field) requires a schema refetch that produces a reordered field list during an open form session, which the current architecture does not produce under normal operation.
verdict.fixNote: The fix suggestion (use label for structural items, field.attribute for scalar fields) is correct for DrawerCreate and DrawerUpdate. DrawerDetail does not need fixing because it is display-only with no controlled input state. The scenario involving PHP ->when() is not accurate: those methods filter fields at schema serialization time, they do not cause in-session reordering of the fieldsForCreate array.

title: BrowserSessionsSection: i18n-hardcoded English strings in relativeTime() helper
evidence:
function relativeTime(timestamp: number, locale = 'en'): string {
  const seconds = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000) - timestamp
  if (seconds < 60) return 'just now'
  if (seconds < 3600) return `${Math.floor(seconds / 60)} min ago`
  if (seconds < 86400) return `${Math.floor(seconds / 3600)} h ago`
  ...
}
suggestedFix: The strings 'just now', 'min ago', 'h ago' are hardcoded English. The function receives `locale` (from `i18n.language`) and uses it correctly for `Intl.DateTimeFormat`, but ignores it for the early branches. Use `Intl.RelativeTimeFormat` for all branches, or at minimum route these strings through the `t()` function in the calling component.

verdict.reasoning: Lines 58-68 of BrowserSessionsSection.tsx confirm the defect exactly as reported. The `relativeTime` function accepts a `locale` parameter and correctly threads it to `Intl.DateTimeFormat` for timestamps older than 24 hours (line 64), but returns hardcoded English literals for the three short-duration branches: `'just now'` (line 60), the template literal `` `${Math.floor(seconds / 60)} min ago` `` (line 61), and `` `${Math.floor(seconds / 3600)} h ago` `` (line 62). The caller at line 205 does pass `i18n.language`, so the intent to be locale-aware is present but incomplete. No guard elsewhere neutralises this: the component's translation namespace is `'profile'` (line 71), all other visible strings go through `t()`, but these three short-duration labels bypass it entirely. The `deviceLabel` helper (lines 45-55) has an analogous problem (`'Unknown device'`, `'Browser'`, the `' on '` conjunction) but that is outside the reported scope. Severity is low: purely a cosmetic/convention gap, no data leak or logic error, only affects non-English users of the sessions panel.
verdict.fixNote: The cleanest fix is to replace the three early-return strings with `Intl.RelativeTimeFormat` calls using the `locale` argument — for example `new Intl.RelativeTimeFormat(locale, { numeric: 'auto' }).format(-Math.floor(seconds / 60), 'minute')`. This keeps i18n consistent with the existing `Intl.DateTimeFormat` path and avoids adding translation keys for what are effectively numeric expressions. If `Intl.RelativeTimeFormat` browser support is a concern for the target baseline, routing them through `t()` with interpolation keys is the fallback. Also note that `deviceLabel` (lines 45-55) has the same hardcoded-English pattern and should be fixed in the same pass.

title: SecuritySection: passwordError default value contains Portuguese — violates English-in-code rule
evidence: setPasswordError(t('current_password_wrong', { defaultValue: 'Password incorrecta. Tente novamente.' }))
suggestedFix: The `defaultValue` is an English fallback used when the translation key is missing — it should always be in English. Change to: `{ defaultValue: 'Incorrect password. Please try again.' }`. This conforms to the workspace rule that code (including i18n fallback strings) stays in English.

verdict.reasoning: The translation key `current_password_wrong` does not exist in any of the three server-side lang files (`resources/lang/en/profile.php`, `resources/lang/pt_BR/profile.php`, `resources/lang/pt_PT/profile.php`). The i18n setup in `resources/js/lib/i18n.ts` fetches all translations from `/api/translations/{locale}` at runtime - there is no bundled JSON fallback. Because the key is absent from every PHP lang file, the API response will never include it, and react-i18next will always fall through to the `defaultValue` option. The `defaultValue` string is `'Password incorrecta. Tente novamente.'` which is Portuguese (European). This string will be rendered in the UI for every locale, including English and pt_BR users who would see a European-Portuguese error message when they type the wrong current password during the 2FA-disable flow. This is both a convention violation (English-in-code: the fallback string in code must be English) and a functional UX defect (wrong-language text shown in production because the key is missing from the translation catalogue entirely). The suggested fix is correct in spirit but incomplete: the `defaultValue` should be changed to English AND the key `current_password_wrong` must be added to all three locale files (`en/profile.php`, `pt_BR/profile.php`, `pt_PT/profile.php`) so the translation catalogue actually covers it. The severity is low-to-medium: the wrong-password path is an error state in a rare flow (disabling 2FA), so impact is limited, but it is a real runtime defect that will reproduce in every deployment today.
verdict.fixNote: The `defaultValue` fix alone is insufficient. The key `current_password_wrong` is missing from all three PHP lang files. Both fixes are required: (1) add `'current_password_wrong' => 'Incorrect password. Please try again.'` to `resources/lang/en/profile.php`, an appropriate translation to `pt_BR/profile.php`, and another to `pt_PT/profile.php`; (2) change the `defaultValue` in `SecuritySection.tsx:54` to English (`'Incorrect password. Please try again.'`) as the last-resort fallback if translations fail to load. Without step 1, even with an English `defaultValue`, the actual translation served to pt_BR and pt_PT users would still be missing.

title: ToastContext safety-net clear() wipes all active toasts in queue
suggestedFix: Replace toastRef.current?.clear() with a targeted remove by reference. Capture the PrimeReact message object returned by the show() call into a local ref and call toastRef.current?.remove(messageRef.current) inside the timeout instead of clear(). This limits the safety-net to the specific toast that triggered the timer and leaves other queued messages untouched.

verdict.reasoning: Lines 110-112 call toastRef.current?.clear() inside a setTimeout set to life + 1000 ms (6000 ms). clear() is PrimeReact Toast's "remove every active message" method, not a targeted remove. The return value of toastRef.current?.show() on line 71 is discarded, so no per-message handle is ever stored. Each addToast invocation registers an independent timer. When two toasts are queued within 6 seconds of each other, whichever timer fires first calls clear() and silently destroys all remaining toasts, regardless of their own life or sticky settings. The comment on line 108 ("force-clear ALL messages") confirms this is intentional but it is still a behavioral defect: subsequent toasts get a shorter visible lifetime than their life parameter promises. The fix (store the return value of show() and call remove(msg) instead of clear()) is correct and feasible.
verdict.fixNote: PrimeReact's Toast.show() returns the enqueued ToastMessage object. Capture it: const msg = toastRef.current?.show(...). Then in the timeout call toastRef.current?.remove(msg) instead of clear(). This limits the safety-net to the one toast that registered the timer and does not affect any other queued messages.

title: uploadRequest() unconditionally redirects on 401, missing publicProbes allowlist present in request()
suggestedFix: Extract the publicProbes constant to module scope and apply the same guard used in request(): `if (res.status === 401 && !publicProbes.includes(path)) { redirectOnSessionExpiry() }`. The path parameter is already available in uploadRequest's closure so no signature change is needed.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by direct code inspection.

In `request()` (lines 159-185), the 401 guard is:
```
const publicProbes = ['/api/auth/user', '/api/preferences', '/api/navigation']
if (res.status === 401 && !publicProbes.includes(path)) {
  redirectOnSessionExpiry()
}
```
The allowlist is declared as a local constant inside `request()` and the check short-circuits the redirect for those three paths.

In `uploadRequest()` (lines 291-294), the 401 guard is:
```
if (res.status === 401) {
  redirectOnSessionExpiry()
}
```
There is no allowlist check at all. The `publicProbes` constant is not in scope here (it is local to `request()`), and `uploadRequest()` does not have an equivalent guard.

In practice, `uploadRequest()` is only called through `api.upload()` (line 337-338), which is wired to form submissions that contain `File` objects (file-field resources). A 401 from a public or unauthenticated probe would not normally reach `uploadRequest()` -- those probes are JSON GET calls that go through `request()`. So the realistic attack surface is narrow: a user who is logged out but somehow triggers a multipart upload (e.g. the session expires mid-upload) will be redirected unconditionally. The same redirect also happens in `request()` for non-public paths, so the behavior difference is only for the three `publicProbes` paths. None of those three paths (`/api/auth/user`, `/api/preferences`, `/api/navigation`) would ever be called as a multipart upload, so the asymmetry has no observable impact in any realistic execution path.

The code divergence is real: `uploadRequest()` unconditionally redirects on 401 while `request()` has the allowlist guard. However, the claimed consequence -- a redirect loop or broken public-page behavior -- does not materialize in practice because `api.upload()` is never called for the three listed public probes. The finding is technically accurate as a code consistency/maintainability issue, but the severity is overstated. It is not a medium-severity bug with user-visible impact; it is a low-severity code inconsistency that could become a problem if `uploadRequest` were ever wired to a public endpoint in future.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct in principle -- extract `publicProbes` to module scope and apply the same guard in `uploadRequest()`. However, the claimed severity of "medium" is too high. Because `api.upload()` is only ever called for multipart file-field submissions (guarded by `hasFileValues()`), none of the three public probe paths (`/api/auth/user`, `/api/preferences`, `/api/navigation`) can reach `uploadRequest()` in any current call path. The inconsistency is a future-proofing / code hygiene issue rather than a present-day bug with observable consequences. Adjust to low severity.

title: Hardcoded English fallback strings in buildOverrideProps bypass i18n system
evidence:
addToast('success', schema!.messages?.created ?? 'Record created successfully.')
addToast('success', schema!.messages?.updated ?? 'Record updated successfully.')
addToast('success', schema!.messages?.deleted ?? 'Record deleted successfully.')
suggestedFix: Replace each hardcoded English fallback with a tMsg() call using the same translation keys already used elsewhere in these files (record_created, record_updated, record_deleted). The same pattern repeats in ResourceDetail.tsx (lines 152, 158, 164, 199, 439, 446, 452, 480), ResourceCreate.tsx (lines 380, 386, 392), and ResourceUpdate.tsx (lines 314, 321, 327).

verdict.reasoning: The cited code at ResourceIndex.tsx lines 459, 465, and 471 was read directly. The three `addToast` calls inside `buildOverrideProps` use `schema!.messages?.created ?? 'Record created successfully.'` (and the updated/deleted analogues) as their final fallback. The `messages` property on the resource schema is typed as optional (`messages?: ResourceMessages` in types/index.ts:466), so the hardcoded English fallback is reachable whenever the PHP backend does not include the `messages` key in the schema JSON. The file already imports `tMsg` from `useTranslation('messages')` at line 38 and uses it correctly in all non-override toast callsites (e.g. line 344 `tMsg('record_deleted')`). The keys `record_created`, `record_updated`, and `record_deleted` exist in all three locale files (en, pt_PT, pt_BR) and in the Vitest mock in test-setup.ts. Every other file in the subsystem (ResourceCreate.tsx:266, ResourceUpdate.tsx:177, InlineCreateModal.tsx:101, DrawerDetail.tsx:54) follows the correct pattern with `tMsg(key)` as the fallback. Only `buildOverrideProps` escapes the i18n system, meaning pt_PT and pt_BR users see raw English text after create/update/delete operations that go through an override component.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (replace each hardcoded string with `tMsg('record_created')`, `tMsg('record_updated')`, `tMsg('record_deleted')`) is correct. No additional changes are needed: the keys already exist in all locales and tMsg is already in scope. The same fix applies to the analogous lines in ResourceDetail.tsx, ResourceCreate.tsx, and ResourceUpdate.tsx cited by the reporter, but those were not part of this adversarial check.

title: CacheAdmin uses native window.confirm instead of PrimeReact confirmation dialog
evidence:
if (!window.confirm(message)) return
...
if (!window.confirm(t('cache_clear_all_confirm', 'Clear every Martis cache?'))) return
suggestedFix: Replace both window.confirm calls with PrimeReact's confirmDialog() from primereact/confirmdialog. Add a single <ConfirmDialog /> mount to the component tree and convert the two callbacks to use the accept/reject pattern. This aligns with the design-system convention used everywhere else in the admin panel.

verdict.reasoning: Both window.confirm calls are confirmed at exactly the cited lines (85 and 92) in /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/resources/js/pages/CacheAdmin.tsx. CacheAdmin.tsx is the ONLY file in the entire resources/js tree that uses window.confirm. The project's established pattern for destructive-action confirmation is a custom inline modal component (DeleteModal, UnsavedChangesDialog, ActionModal, GateModal, InlineCreateModal) rendered via React portals with full i18n, theming, and keyboard handling. No file in the codebase imports or uses PrimeReact's confirmDialog/ConfirmDialog. The finding is real: window.confirm is inconsistent with the project's modal convention, bypasses i18n styling, blocks the JS thread, and cannot be themed. However, the suggested fix is wrong in its specifics: the correct remedy is a custom modal following the DeleteModal pattern (or a shared ConfirmModal), NOT PrimeReact's confirmDialog() which is not used anywhere in this codebase. Severity remains low because this is a UX/convention issue with no correctness or security impact.
verdict.fixNote: The fix direction is correct (replace with a React modal) but the specific API is wrong. PrimeReact confirmDialog() is not used anywhere in this codebase. The correct fix is to follow the existing pattern: create a small inline ConfirmModal (or reuse DeleteModal with a custom confirmMessage prop) that renders via React portal, uses i18n keys, and accepts onConfirm/onCancel callbacks — matching DeleteModal.tsx and the other custom modal components in resources/js/components/.

title: IDOR: executePivot loads parent record without authorizedToView check
suggestedFix:
After instantiating $parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel) (line 571), add: if (! $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request)) { return JsonErrorResponse::forbidden('This action is unauthorized.')->toResponse(); }

Also add the authorizedToRun per-model loop from execute() (lines 154-170) for non-standalone pivot actions, matching the pattern in execute(). Compare with BelongsToManyController::resolveContext() which correctly calls authorizedToView at line 626.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at ActionController.php:546-571 confirms the gap. After loading $parentModel = $modelClass::find($id) at line 547, the method constructs $parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel) at line 571 but never calls $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request) before proceeding. The only authorization calls are: (a) $actionInstance->authorizedToSee($request) at line 566, which checks the Action's own visibility policy, not the parent record's view policy; and (b) no per-model authorizedToRun loop for the related models (unlike execute() lines 154-170).

The contrast with BelongsToManyController::resolveContext() is accurate: that method calls $parentInstance->authorizedToView($request) at line 626 immediately after wrapping the parent model, before doing anything else. executePivot skips this step entirely.

Route-level guards (martis.auth + martis.2fa + martis.verified, confirmed in routes/martis.php lines 181-205) establish that the user is authenticated and has completed 2FA, but these do not perform per-record view authorization. authorizedToView delegates to checkPolicy('view', $this->model) on the Resource, which hits the Laravel Gate with the bound Eloquent instance — a distinct check that executePivot never makes.

The practical impact: an authenticated admin user who lacks 'view' policy permission on a parent record (e.g. tenant A's record) can POST to /api/resources/{resource}/{parentId}/belongs-to-many/{relationship}/actions/{action} and execute a pivot action on that parent's related records. They can also enumerate record existence via the 404/200 distinction. The missing authorizedToRun per-related-model loop is a secondary gap.

Severity adjustment from critical to high: the route requires authentication + completed 2FA, so this is not unauthenticated access. It is a privilege-escalation within the authenticated admin panel (policy bypass), which maps to high rather than critical.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix is correct: after line 571 ($parentInstance = new $resourceClass($parentModel)), add the authorizedToView guard before resolvePivotColumns. Additionally, consider adding an authorizedToRun loop over $models after line 590 (once the related models are loaded), mirroring the execute() pattern at lines 154-170, to cover per-related-record authorization for non-standalone pivot actions.

title: Default manage-martis-cache gate allows all authenticated users to clear/disable caches
evidence: Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($user) => $user !== null);
suggestedFix: The default is intentionally permissive (documented at line 311-316). Consider tightening the default to deny or to require a dedicated flag (e.g. config('martis.cache.require_admin', false)), with a clear upgrade note. At minimum ensure the docs prominently warn that cache clearing has side effects for all users and that the gate should be overridden in any multi-role deployment.

verdict.reasoning:
The gate definition at MartisServiceProvider.php:325 is confirmed verbatim: `Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($user) => $user !== null)`. This is the live default for every fresh Martis installation.

Reading the route file (routes/martis.php:181-263) shows the cache routes are nested inside a middleware group requiring `martis.auth` + `martis.2fa` + `martis.locale` + `martis.verified`. This means the outer auth stack already guarantees `$user !== null` before the gate is ever evaluated. The gate's own null check (CacheController.php:115-117) and the `fn ($user) => $user !== null` closure are therefore both tautologies in normal request flow: the gate grants every authenticated admin-panel user access to `POST /api/cache/clear`, `POST /api/cache/disable`, `POST /api/cache/enable`, and `POST /api/cache/reset-override`.

The docblock at lines 307-318 acknowledges this is intentionally permissive and instructs host apps to override it with `Gate::define('manage-martis-cache', fn ($u) => $u->is_admin)`. The `Gate::has()` guard at line 321 does ensure a consumer-defined gate takes precedence, so the override path works correctly.

The finding is real but the severity is accurately medium, not high/critical: the exposure requires a fully authenticated + 2FA-verified admin session (unauthenticated requests never reach these routes), the admin_ui surface is disabled by default via `martis.cache.admin_ui` config, and the permissive default is documented. The risk is specifically for multi-role deployments where not all admin-panel users should be able to clear production caches or disable cache layers, which is a common real-world scenario that relies on consumers reading the docs and proactively overriding the gate.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (add a `config('martis.cache.require_admin', false)` flag or tighten the default) is reasonable but the simplest safe path is to flip the default to deny (`fn ($user) => false`) and document that consumers must explicitly open it. Alternatively, keep the permissive default but add a prominent runtime warning (logged at boot when `APP_ENV=production` and the gate has not been overridden) so the permissive posture is impossible to miss in production deployments. The `Gate::has()` guard at line 321 already makes the override mechanism correct; the only gap is that the default is too open for multi-role admin panels.

title: Unsanitized sort column in BelongsToManyController — arbitrary column enumeration
suggestedFix: Mirror the pattern used in HasManyController::applySorting() (lines 475-507): extract sortable attributes via Field::flattenLayoutFields() on the related resource, then guard with in_array($sort, $sortableAttributes, true) before calling orderBy(). The $relatedResourceClass variable is already in scope at this block.

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code at BelongsToManyController.php:82-83 is confirmed real. Lines 77-84 of the `index` method read `$rawSort` directly from `$request->query('sort')` and pass it to `$query->orderBy($rawSort, $direction->value)` with no allowlist check. The only guard is `is_string($rawSort) && $rawSort !== ''`, which prevents empty/null but permits any arbitrary string.

Every peer controller in the package applies a sortable-attribute allowlist before calling `orderBy`:
- HasManyController::applySorting() lines 493-507: builds `$sortableAttributes` from `Field::flattenLayoutFields()` filtered by `isSortable()`, then does `if (!in_array($sort, $sortableAttributes, true)) return;`
- ResourceController::applySorting() lines 1604-1616: identical pattern
- MorphManyController lines 566-577: identical pattern

BelongsToManyController is the sole exception. The route is registered as a GET endpoint (`/resources/{resource}/{id}/belongs-to-many/{relationship}`) accessible to any authenticated user who can view the parent resource. There are no middleware-level query-parameter validators for this route in martis.php. The `SortDirection` enum correctly constrains the direction argument, but the column name is fully user-controlled. Eloquent's `orderBy(string $column)` passes the value through to raw SQL without quoting when it receives a plain string, so injection via expression-like column names (e.g. `IF(1=1,sleep(3),0)`) or probing hidden columns is viable on MySQL. The `$relatedResourceClass` variable is in scope at the offending block, so the fix is straightforward — mirror the HasManyController pattern.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct as described. Use `$relatedResourceClass` (already in scope from `$ctx`) to instantiate the related resource, call `Field::flattenLayoutFields($instance->fields($request))`, extract sortable attribute names, and guard with `in_array($rawSort, $sortableAttributes, true)` before calling `orderBy`. Also check MorphToManyController — it extends the same pattern and may carry the same omission.

title: Unsanitized sort column in MorphToManyController — arbitrary column enumeration
suggestedFix: Same fix as BelongsToManyController: validate $rawSort against the sorted list of sortable attributes from Field::flattenLayoutFields() on $relatedResourceClass before calling orderBy(). ResourceController, HasManyController and MorphManyController all apply this guard; this controller pair missed it.

verdict.reasoning:
Lines 80-87 of MorphToManyController.php confirm the unguarded path verbatim:

    $rawSort = $request->query('sort');   // line 80 — direct query string read
    ...
    if (is_string($rawSort) && $rawSort !== '') {
        $query->orderBy($rawSort, $direction->value);   // line 86 — no allowlist
    }

There is no call to Field::flattenLayoutFields(), no sortableAttributes array, and no in_array() check anywhere in this method. The value from the query string flows directly into orderBy().

The claimed fix controller — BelongsToManyController — has the identical unguarded pattern at its own lines 77-84 (confirmed by grep output), so the suggestion that it was already fixed there is wrong; both controllers share the same gap.

The three controllers that DO apply the allowlist guard are ResourceController (line 1614), HasManyController (line 503), and MorphManyController (line 576). All three call Field::flattenLayoutFields() to build a $sortableAttributes set and reject any sort column not present in it.

Severity assessment: Laravel's query builder quotes identifiers (MySQL backticks, Postgres double-quotes), which blocks classic keyword injection through the column position. However, an unauthenticated or low-privilege caller can enumerate valid column names on the underlying pivot/related table through timing or sort-order side-channels, and can cause unexpected query behaviour or errors. This is a genuine security gap (missing allowlist where the codebase clearly intends one), but the practical exploitability for data exfiltration is lower than a raw SQL injection. "high" is defensible but "medium" is a more precise fit given the identifier-quoting mitigation in all supported drivers.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix describes BelongsToManyController as already fixed — it is not. BelongsToManyController has the same unguarded orderBy at its own line 83. Both controllers need the flattenLayoutFields allowlist applied. The fix pattern from HasManyController (lines 482-507) is the correct template to follow for both.

title: LensRequest::withOrdering passes unvalidated sort column to orderBy()
suggestedFix: The $sortColumn value comes directly from $source->query('sort') (LensRequest.php line 114) and is never validated against a set of declared sortable fields. Because Lens::query() is abstract and implementors call $request->withOrdering($query) as a convenience, any authenticated user can pass any column name. Add an optional $allowedColumns parameter (list<string>) to withOrdering() and skip the orderBy when the value is not in the list. LensController should populate it from Field::flattenLayoutFields() on the lens fields before passing the LensRequest down.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the code directly. LensRequest::fromRequest() at line 113-114 takes the raw `sort` query parameter and stores it unvalidated in $sortColumn. withOrdering() at lines 72-73 then passes $sortColumn directly to $query->orderBy() with no allowlist check. Contrast with ResourceController::applySorting() (lines 1606-1616), HasManyController (lines 495-503), and MorphManyController (lines 568-576), which all build a $sortableAttributes array from fields marked ->isSortable() and reject any sort value not in that list via in_array(..., true) before calling orderBy(). LensController never performs this check — it calls LensRequest::fromRequest() at line 64 and then passes the LensRequest into the lens-defined query() via executeLensQuery(), where the lens implementor may call $request->withOrdering($query). withOrdering() offers no $allowedColumns parameter and performs no validation. The $sort query string is therefore fully attacker-controlled. Eloquent's identifier quoting prevents classical SQL injection, but the vector allows schema probing (column-not-found errors in non-production environments, or observable differences in response time/error shape). The severity is medium rather than high: the attack requires an authenticated session with lens access and does not allow arbitrary SQL injection, only column name probing and potential exception leakage.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix (adding an $allowedColumns list<string> parameter to withOrdering()) is correct in direction but incomplete on its own: lens implementors can opt out by simply not passing the list. The more robust fix mirrors what ResourceController does: LensController::resolveLensFields() already calls Field::flattenLayoutFields() and Field::filterForContext() (lines 383-391). Before calling LensRequest::fromRequest(), the controller should extract the sortable attribute names from those fields and either (a) validate $lensRequest->sortColumn against them immediately after fromRequest() — setting it to null if not in the list — or (b) add the allowedColumns guard inside withOrdering() while also having the controller inject the list. Option (a) is simpler and consistent with how ResourceController handles the same problem. The direction check is safe: SortDirection::tryFrom() at line 117 already coerces the direction to a typed enum value, so only the column name is the exposure.

title: TrendMetric uses MySQL-only DATE_FORMAT — fatal on SQLite/Postgres
evidence: ->select(DB::raw("DATE_FORMAT({$dateColumn}, '{$dateFormat}') as date_key"), $expression)
suggestedFix: Detect the driver from $model::query()->getConnection()->getDriverName() (the same pattern SearchResolver already uses at line 110-113). Branch the raw expression: MySQL keeps DATE_FORMAT; SQLite uses strftime(); Postgres uses TO_CHAR(). The SearchResolver already shows the safe driver-detection idiom. The week format '%x-%v' needs per-dialect equivalents (SQLite: strftime('%Y-W%W'); Postgres: TO_CHAR(col, 'IYYY-IW')).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by direct code reading. At /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Metrics/TrendMetric.php:153, the `aggregateByPeriod` method unconditionally emits:

  ->select(DB::raw("DATE_FORMAT({$dateColumn}, '{$dateFormat}') as date_key"), $expression)

There is no driver-detection guard anywhere in the file, the Metrics directory, or anywhere in the package source tree (grep for `getDriverName` in `src/` returns only `SearchResolver.php:111` and `SearchResolver.php:246-247`, which are unrelated to metrics).

`DATE_FORMAT()` is a MySQL-only SQL function. On SQLite it causes a fatal `SQLSTATE[HY000]: General error: 1 no such function: DATE_FORMAT`; on PostgreSQL it causes `ERROR: function date_format(...) does not exist`. Both errors surface as a 500 for every chart rendered by any `TrendMetric` subclass when the application uses SQLite (common in tests and CI) or PostgreSQL.

The week format `%x-%v` (ISO year + ISO week number) is additionally non-trivial to port: SQLite has no direct ISO-year equivalent in `strftime`, and PostgreSQL requires `TO_CHAR(col, 'IYYY-IW')`. The fix requires per-driver branching, exactly as `SearchResolver.php:110-113` already demonstrates.

Severity assessment: high is correct. Any non-MySQL database driver renders every trend metric completely broken at the SQL layer, not just degraded. This is a fatal runtime failure across the full surface of the TrendMetric subsystem on SQLite/PostgreSQL.
verdict.fixNote: The fix requires three dialect branches for the raw SELECT expression. MySQL keeps `DATE_FORMAT(col, fmt)`. SQLite uses `strftime(fmt, col)` with format remapping: `%Y-%m-%d` stays the same, `%Y-%m` stays the same, and `%x-%v` has no direct equivalent — the safest SQLite approximation is `strftime('%Y', col) || '-W' || printf('%02d', strftime('%W', col))` (uses ISO-week-of-year via `%W`). PostgreSQL uses `TO_CHAR(col, fmt)` with remapping: `%Y-%m-%d` becomes `YYYY-MM-DD`, `%Y-%m` becomes `YYYY-MM`, `%x-%v` becomes `IYYY-IW`. Driver detection should follow the `SearchResolver` idiom at lines 110-113: `$model::query()->getConnection()->getDriverName()`. The `groupBy('date_key')` and `pluck('aggregate', 'date_key')` calls are portable and require no change.

title: MCP bearer token compared with non-timing-safe string equality
suggestedFix: Replace the `!==` comparison with `hash_equals($expected, $request->getHeaderLine('Authorization'))`. Both the `listen()` path (line 89) and the `createRequestHandler()` path (line 135) must be fixed. `hash_equals` requires both strings to be the same length, so build the expected string the same way first, then compare. This prevents timing-oracle attacks where an attacker probes one character at a time.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Mcp/Transport/AuthenticatedStreamableHttpTransport.php confirms the finding at both cited locations. Line 86-89 (listen() path) and lines 132-135 (createRequestHandler() path) both build `$expected = 'Bearer '.$this->token` and compare it against the attacker-controlled Authorization header using PHP's `!==` operator, which short-circuits at the first differing byte and leaks how many characters match.

No mitigating guards are present: the transport is a raw ReactPHP HTTP server bound to an arbitrary host/port (constructor takes both), with no framework-level rate limiting, no IP allowlist, and no additional authentication layer visible in this file. An external network caller controls the Authorization header value entirely.

The timing-oracle attack is theoretically valid: an attacker can enumerate the token one character at a time by measuring response latency across many requests. The fix (replacing `!==` with `hash_equals($expected, $actual)`) is correct and standard.

Severity is downgraded from high to medium because practical exploitation requires: (a) network-level timing precision across many thousands of requests, (b) stable low-jitter conditions (harder over the public internet, more feasible on LAN), and (c) knowledge that the endpoint exists and what the token format is. PHP interpreter overhead and HTTP server overhead add noise that makes the attack harder to execute reliably than a direct memory-comparison timing attack would be. The vulnerability class is real and the fix is cheap, so it should be corrected, but the exploitability bar is meaningfully higher than a high-severity rating implies.
verdict.fixNote: Both sites must be fixed. The standard idiom `hash_equals($expected, $request->getHeaderLine('Authorization'))` is correct: hash_equals returns false immediately on length mismatch (so no length oracle is introduced), and compares in constant time otherwise. No padding or pre-hashing of both sides is needed. The fix is a one-line change at each of the two comparison sites (line 89 and line 135).

title: Raw exception message from arbitrary Throwable surfaced in 500 JSON response body
evidence: return JsonErrorResponse::serverError($e->getMessage() ?: 'Error deleting record.')->toResponse();
suggestedFix: The delete catch-all path deliberately forwards `$e->getMessage()` to the client (justified in the v1.8.2 comment) so 'the operator sees what actually broke'. This is acceptable for an admin panel where only authenticated staff reach it, but the message can still disclose DB schema, file paths, or third-party service internals. Replace with a stable user-facing string (e.g. 'Error deleting record.') in the JSON body and keep the full message only in the `Log::error` context. The same pattern appears in `ActionController` lines 246 and 640 ('Action failed: '.$e->getMessage(), 'Pivot action failed: '.$e->getMessage()).

verdict.reasoning: The code at ResourceController.php:460 does forward `$e->getMessage()` to the JSON response body, exactly as described. The same pattern appears in ActionController at lines 246 and 640. However, the route definition in routes/martis.php:181-467 places the destroy endpoint inside a triple-layered protected group: `martis.auth` (session authentication), `martis.2fa` (completed two-factor if enabled), and `martis.verified`. MartisAuthenticate.php throws AuthenticationException for unauthenticated requests, so the path is completely unreachable without a valid session. Inside the controller, a further per-resource `authorizedToDelete()` gate fires at line 427 before any database operation begins. The audience for this error message is therefore a confirmed, authenticated, authorized admin operator, which is exactly who the v1.8.2 comment (lines 445-451) intends to inform. DB schema names, observer class names, and third-party service messages disclosed to a logged-in admin are within the existing trust boundary of an admin panel: that operator already sees raw IDs, field names, and model data throughout the UI. The finding is real as a pattern (the message string does reach the client) but the claimed severity of "high" is incorrect for an endpoint that is structurally inaccessible to unauthenticated users. Downgraded to low.
verdict.fixNote: The pattern is an intentional UX decision for admin operators, not an unauthenticated information-disclosure vulnerability. If the team wants to tighten it anyway, replace the raw `$e->getMessage()` in the JSON body with a stable string such as 'Error deleting record.' and keep the full message exclusively in the `Log::error` context array (where it already also appears at line 456). The same optional hardening applies to ActionController lines 246 and 640. This is a code-quality improvement, not a security fix.

title: AzureProvider Graph API call has no HTTP timeout — SSRF amplification and hung requests
suggestedFix: Add `->timeout(10)` (or a config-driven value) to the `Http::` chain. Without a timeout, a slow or unresponsive Graph endpoint will hold a PHP-FPM worker for the default cURL limit (often 0 = forever), enabling a slow-loris-style denial-of-service via the SSO callback. Additionally, the `$url` in `fetchAppRoleAssignments` and `fetchGroupMemberships` is constructed from the hard-coded `graph.microsoft.com` base, which is safe, but `$externalId` is taken from the Socialite token and interpolated after `urlencode()`; that is correct but worth a comment.

verdict.reasoning: The code at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Sso/Providers/AzureProvider.php lines 164-166 confirms the finding verbatim: `Http::acceptJson()->withToken($accessToken)->get($url)` has no `->timeout()` modifier. A search across the entire package src/ directory finds no global Http timeout macro, no `Http::globalTimeout()`, and no config key that would supply a default. Laravel's Http facade wraps Guzzle; Guzzle's default timeout is 0 (infinite) unless explicitly set. A slow or hung graph.microsoft.com endpoint will therefore hold the PHP-FPM worker for the entire SSO callback indefinitely. The "SSRF amplification" framing in the report title is inaccurate — the URL is constructed from a hard-coded host with only urlencode($externalId) interpolated, so there is no SSRF to internal hosts. The real risk is PHP-FPM worker pool exhaustion (slow-loris-style DoS) during SSO logins. Given that this is a low-volume admin-panel SSO path against a well-known Microsoft endpoint, the severity is low rather than medium; fixing it is a one-liner (`->timeout(config('martis.auth.sso.providers.azure.graph_timeout', 10))`) but the exploitability in practice is limited.
verdict.fixNote: Add `->timeout(config('martis.auth.sso.providers.azure.graph_timeout', 10))` to the Http chain in `graphCall()`. The "SSRF amplification" label in the original report is misleading — there is no SSRF vector here because the base URL is hard-coded to graph.microsoft.com and $externalId is urlencode()'d. The actual risk is FPM worker exhaustion under a slow upstream, not data exfiltration or internal host probing. Severity should be low, not medium.

title: Raw exception message from arbitrary Throwable in ActionController action-execution response
evidence: return JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action failed: '.$e->getMessage())->toResponse();
suggestedFix: Same pattern as the ResourceController delete path. Third-party action handle() methods can throw exceptions whose messages contain SQL snippets, internal service URLs, or stack trace fragments. Return a generic 'Action failed.' to the browser and keep `$e->getMessage()` only in the server-side log. Also applies to line 640 ('Pivot action failed: '.$e->getMessage()).

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is confirmed by direct code reading. Two pieces of evidence are definitive:

1. `JsonErrorResponse::serverError()` at /Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/src/Http/Resources/JsonErrorResponse.php:101 has an explicit docblock "Never exposes internal error details to the client" and its safe default is the generic string `'An unexpected error occurred.'`. The method signature is `serverError(string $message = 'An unexpected error occurred.')`.

2. ActionController.php:246 calls `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action failed: '.$e->getMessage())` and line 640 calls `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Pivot action failed: '.$e->getMessage())`. Both pass raw Throwable message strings into that parameter, directly contradicting the contract the class declares for itself.

No intercepting layer exists to sanitize these. The package's `Handler::register()` only intercepts `MartisException` and `ThrottleRequestsException` via `renderable()` callbacks. The code itself confirms the design intent at line 242: `MartisException` is re-thrown to be handled elsewhere; all other `\Throwable` types fall through to the `serverError()` call. But those are precisely the unknown third-party exceptions that can carry SQL snippets, internal service URLs, or file paths in their messages.

The severity is medium. The path is: (a) an authenticated user triggers a bulk or pivot action, (b) the user-space action's `handle()` method throws an arbitrary exception (e.g. from a PDO driver, an HTTP client, or a third-party SDK), (c) the message is returned verbatim in the JSON 500 response body. The endpoint requires the user to be authenticated and authorized to run the action, which lowers the blast radius compared to unauthenticated disclosure — hence medium rather than high. The fix is straightforward: replace both call sites with `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action failed.')` (and the pivot variant), keeping `$e->getMessage()` only in the `Log::error()` call that already exists directly above each one.
verdict.fixNote: Both call sites already have a `Log::error()` call immediately above them that correctly logs `$e->getMessage()` server-side. The fix is only to remove the message from the response: change line 246 to `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Action failed.')->toResponse()` and line 640 to `JsonErrorResponse::serverError('Pivot action failed.')->toResponse()`. No other changes needed. The `serverError()` docblock already documents this as the intended contract.

title: abort(500, ...) with internal config error message on email verification route
evidence: abort(500, 'Auth user model is not configured.');
suggestedFix: The second argument to `abort()` becomes the exception message and in some Laravel error-handler configurations (particularly with debug=true or custom error pages) can be included in the HTTP response body. In a production admin panel this is a configuration error message, not a real secret, but it reveals internal structure. Use `abort(500)` without a message and instead throw a logged exception, or redirect to a safe error page. At minimum, this is a hardening recommendation.

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code at line 96 does exist exactly as reported:

    abort(500, 'Auth user model is not configured.');

The second argument to Laravel's `abort()` helper is passed as the `$message` parameter to `HttpException`, which stores it as the exception message. In Laravel's default debug-off production configuration, this message is NOT rendered in the HTTP response body for 500 errors — the framework swallows it and returns a generic error page or JSON envelope. So for most production deployments this is benign.

However, the claim is still technically real for two documented reasons:

1. When `APP_DEBUG=true` (which is not uncommon in staging environments or misconfigured production deployments), Laravel's `Handler::render()` includes the exception message in the JSON error response (`{"message": "Auth user model is not configured."}`) and in the Ignition debug page. The message confirms that the panel has a misconfigured auth provider — indirect information disclosure about the internal structure.

2. If the consuming application registers a custom exception handler or API error formatter (e.g. `Handler::renderable()` that serialises all HttpExceptions to JSON), the message would leak through without `APP_DEBUG`.

The message itself ("Auth user model is not configured.") is a configuration error string, not a secret. It reveals that (a) a Martis admin panel is installed, (b) the auth guard is broken. This is low-severity information disclosure, not a critical leak.

The `resolveUserModel()` method at line 155 confirms the path that triggers this: if `auth.providers.<provider>.model` is not set, not a string, or not a subclass of `Model`, the method returns null, and the verify() method hits the abort(500). This is a developer-misconfiguration code path, not a user-controlled one — a regular visitor cannot trigger it by crafting a request, because the abort fires before any user input is consumed, based purely on the server's own config state.

Verdict: REAL but LOW severity. The suggested fix (use abort(500) without a message and log instead) is the correct hardening step. Severity stays at LOW because the message contains no secrets, the path requires a broken server config, and standard Laravel production settings suppress the message in HTTP responses. It is not invalid.
verdict.fixNote: The fix is correct: replace `abort(500, 'Auth user model is not configured.')` with a logged exception followed by `abort(500)`. For example: `report(new \RuntimeException('Martis: auth user model is not configured — check auth.guards and auth.providers in config.'));` followed by `abort(500);`. This keeps the diagnostic information in the server log while emitting no message in the HTTP response under any configuration. The concern is real but only materialises when APP_DEBUG=true or with a custom exception renderer — standard production Laravel suppresses the second argument of abort(500).

title: Duplicate 'search' top-level key in config/martis.php silently drops default_limit and min_query
evidence:
Line 474: 'search' => [
    'default_limit' => (int) env('MARTIS_SEARCH_DEFAULT_LIMIT', 5),
    'min_query' => (int) env('MARTIS_SEARCH_MIN_QUERY', 2),
],

... (231 lines later) ...

Line 705: 'search' => [
    'enabled' => true,
    'placeholder' => null,
    'mode' => env('MARTIS_SEARCH_MODE', 'bar'),
    'mobileMode' => env('MARTIS_SEARCH_MOBILE_MODE', 'icon'),
],
suggestedFix: Merge both 'search' blocks into one. The final array should include all six keys: default_limit, min_query, enabled, placeholder, mode, mobileMode. The SearchController at src/Http/Controllers/SearchController.php:169-170 reads config('martis.search.default_limit') and config('martis.search.min_query') — because PHP replaces the first 'search' block with the second, these keys are absent at runtime and the env vars MARTIS_SEARCH_DEFAULT_LIMIT and MARTIS_SEARCH_MIN_QUERY are silently ignored even though docs promise they work.

verdict.reasoning: The config file is a single PHP array returned at line 3. Two top-level 'search' keys exist: line 474 (default_limit, min_query) and line 705 (enabled, placeholder, mode, mobileMode). PHP silently discards the first duplicate key, so at runtime config('martis.search') contains only the four keys from line 705. SearchController::globalDefaults() at lines 169-170 calls config('martis.search.default_limit', 5) and config('martis.search.min_query', 2). Because those keys are absent, config() falls back to its own inline defaults (5 and 2), not the env-driven values. The env vars MARTIS_SEARCH_DEFAULT_LIMIT and MARTIS_SEARCH_MIN_QUERY are permanently silenced. The application does not 500 because the config() fallback rescues the values, but any operator who sets those env vars to tune search behaviour will see zero effect. Severity is high (not critical): no crash, no security issue, but documented env-var tuning is silently dead.
verdict.fixNote: Merge both 'search' blocks into one at either location. The merged block must include all six keys: default_limit, min_query, enabled, placeholder, mode, mobileMode. Remove the now-redundant standalone block. After merging, config('martis.search.default_limit') and config('martis.search.min_query') will resolve correctly from the env vars. The config() fallback defaults in SearchController can stay as safety floors but will no longer be the primary source.

title: configuration.md 'Locales and i18n' section shows wrong top-level flat keys instead of nested 'locales' block
evidence:
Docs show:
'locale_fallback_chain' => env('MARTIS_LOCALE_FALLBACK_CHAIN', 'en'),
'app_locale_namespaces' => env('MARTIS_APP_LOCALE_NAMESPACES', ''),
'rtl_locales'           => env('MARTIS_RTL_LOCALES', 'ar,fa,he,ur'),

Actual config (martis.php lines 442-455):

Actual code reads: config('martis.locales.fallback_chain') and config('martis.locales.app_namespaces') (TranslationsController.php:140, 168).

Also fix the key name: the doc says 'app_locale_namespaces' but the config key is 'app_namespaces' (inside the 'locales' group).

The docs at configuration.md:1185-1196 show three flat top-level config keys (`locale_fallback_chain`, `app_locale_namespaces`, `rtl_locales`) with raw env string values. The actual martis.php:442-455 nests all three under a `'locales'` group, with different inner key names (`fallback_chain`, `app_namespaces`, `rtl_locales`) and array values produced by array_filter/array_map. TranslationsController.php:140 reads `config('martis.locales.fallback_chain')` and line 168 reads `config('martis.locales.app_namespaces')`, confirming the nested paths are the live ones. Both the structure and two of the three key names in the doc are wrong. Severity is medium rather than high: the doc is descriptive reference, not generated code; a developer following it to override the config would get silent fallbacks to defaults rather than a 500 or security breach.
verdict.fixNote: Replace the flat three-key block in the 'Locales and i18n' section with the actual nested structure: 'locales' => ['fallback_chain' => env('MARTIS_LOCALE_FALLBACK_CHAIN', 'en'), 'app_namespaces' => env('MARTIS_APP_LOCALE_NAMESPACES', ''), 'rtl_locales' => env('MARTIS_RTL_LOCALES', 'ar,fa,he,ur')]. Update the table to use 'locales.fallback_chain' and 'locales.app_namespaces' as the key column values. Note that the actual runtime values are arrays (split on comma), not raw strings, but showing the env var defaults in the doc snippet is acceptable for readability as long as the nesting and key names are correct.

title: configuration.md 'Password reset' section shows wrong config structure: uses snake_case and wrong nesting

'auth' => [

The correct shape is auth.passwordReset.broker. authentication.md (line 124) already shows the correct shape.

The actual config/martis.php lines 850-861 uses 'passwordReset' (camelCase) with 'broker' nested inside it. title: configuration.md 'Login throttle' section shows auth.login_throttle nesting that does not exist — config/code uses throttle.login_attempts

title: configuration.md sticky_views 'scope' table documents 'request' as a valid value; only 'session' and 'local' are supported
evidence:
Docs (line 1139): "| `scope` | `session` | `session` keeps the memory until the session expires; `request` is a no-op for one navigation only. |"

The authoritative sticky_views.md (lines 63-64) lists only 'session' and 'local' as valid:
- session (default) — sessionStorage. Wipes when the tab closes.
- local — localStorage. Survives tab close.

The config comment in martis.php lines 651-658 also lists only: 'session', 'local', 'server' (reserved future). 'request' is not mentioned anywhere in code.
suggestedFix: Change the scope description in the duplicate Sticky views section (line 1127-1139) to match sticky_views.md: valid values are 'session' (sessionStorage, wipes on tab close) and 'local' (localStorage, survives). Remove the erroneous 'request' value.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading four authoritative sources:

1. `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/docs/configuration.md:1139` - The offending line reads: "`scope` | `session` | `session` keeps the memory until the session expires; `request` is a no-op for one navigation only." The value `request` appears as a documented valid option.

2. `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/config/martis.php:651-654` - The inline PHP comment lists only three values: `session` (default), `local`, and `server` (reserved for next iteration). The word `request` does not appear anywhere near this section.

3. `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/docs/sticky_views.md:63-65` - Lists only `session` (sessionStorage, wipes on tab close), `local` (localStorage, survives tab close), and `server` (reserved). No `request`.

4. `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/resources/js/lib/useStickyView.ts:24-30` - The runtime `getStorage()` function checks `if (scope === 'local')` and `if (scope === 'session')`, then falls through to `window.sessionStorage` for all other values (the comment says this fallback is specifically for the reserved `server` scope). Passing `request` would silently fall through to sessionStorage, effectively behaving as `session` with no error and no indication that the config value was invalid.

5. `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/resources/js/lib/config.ts:484` - The TypeScript type definition for `scope` is `'session' | 'local' | 'server'`. The string literal `'request'` is entirely absent from the type.

The documentation at configuration.md:1139 incorrectly documents `request` as a valid scope value (described as "a no-op for one navigation only") when it is not handled anywhere in the codebase. The description also appears to conflate `request` with `local` (the valid alternative to `session`). The finding is real. Severity remains medium: any consumer who sets `MARTIS_STICKY_VIEWS_SCOPE=request` would silently fall through to `sessionStorage` behavior (same as `session`) with no runtime error, so there is no data loss or crash, but the documentation actively misleads about the supported API surface.
verdict.fixNote: The description column in configuration.md:1139 should be corrected to match the other authoritative sources. Replace the erroneous `request` mention with a description that lists the two real supported values: `session` (sessionStorage, wipes on tab close) and `local` (localStorage, survives tab close). The corrected row should read: "`scope` | `session` | Where state is persisted: `session` — sessionStorage, wipes on tab close; `local` — localStorage, survives tab close." This aligns with sticky_views.md:63-64, config/martis.php:651-654, and the TypeScript type at config.ts:484.

title: overrides.mdx has different wording for the `--frontend` troubleshooting hint
evidence: The pkg source at `/Users/lmoura/projects/martis/martis-package/docs/overrides.md:794` reads: `Any key that appears in martis:list-overrides but not in componentRegistry.keys() is a missing TSX file under resources/js/martis-extensions/ — the most common reason an override fails to resolve.` The mdx at line 805 reads: `Any key that appears in martis:list-overrides but not in componentRegistry.keys() is a missing registration in your extension bundle entry — the most common reason an override fails to resolve.`
suggestedFix: Minor wording divergence ('missing TSX file under resources/js/martis-extensions/' vs 'missing registration in your extension bundle entry'). The mdx wording is slightly more accurate for v1.9+ since registrations can come from `index.ts` imports and not only from auto-discovered TSX files. Sync the pkg to the mdx's wording.

verdict.reasoning:
Both files were read directly. The divergence is confirmed at the exact cited lines.

pkg doc (`martis-package/docs/overrides.md:794`): "missing TSX file under `resources/js/martis-extensions/`"
mdx (`martis-docs/src/content/customization/overrides.mdx:805`): "missing registration in your extension bundle entry"

The reporter claims the MDX wording is more accurate for v1.9+. This is incorrect. The actual code in `martis-package/stubs/extensions/index.ts.stub` and both `vite.extensions.config.ts` instances (stub and playground) make the mechanism explicit: the bundle entry uses `import.meta.glob` to auto-discover every `.tsx` file in the four buckets (`tools/`, `fields/`, `cards/`, `overrides/`) and registers them automatically by derived key. The stub comment at line 22 is unambiguous: "No manual `componentRegistry.register(...)` calls — the generator drops the `.tsx` in the right bucket and the loop below picks it up at the next `npm run build:extensions`."

So "missing TSX file under `resources/js/martis-extensions/`" (pkg wording) is precisely correct for v1.9+: the registration happens automatically from file presence, so the only thing a consumer can be missing is the TSX file itself.

The MDX wording "missing registration in your extension bundle entry" describes the pre-v1.9 pattern where users manually called `componentRegistry.register()` inside `index.ts`. Under v1.9+ `index.ts` is never edited by the consumer.

The doc-drift finding is real, but the suggested fix direction is wrong. It is the MDX that should be updated to match the pkg doc, not the pkg doc updated to match the MDX. Severity remains low: this is documentation only, no runtime impact, and the surrounding context in both files (including the `--frontend` flag description at pkg line 757 that correctly references `index.ts` static parsing) provides enough guidance for a careful reader to infer the right approach.
verdict.fixNote: The suggested fix direction is reversed. The pkg doc wording is correct for v1.9+ auto-discovery: the consumer only needs to place a TSX file in the right bucket; no manual registration call is needed. It is the MDX that should be corrected: replace "missing registration in your extension bundle entry" with "missing TSX file under `resources/js/martis-extensions/`" to match the pkg doc and the actual auto-discovery implementation in `index.ts.stub`.
